key_facts: Unix philosophy: each tool addresses one problem, small and general, combined via pipes to solve complex tasks.
The shell is an ordinary user-mode program, not a privileged system task, enabling multiple shell implementations.
Three standard streams: 0=stdin, 1=stdout, 2=stderr, identified by small integer file descriptors.
The original Unix shell is the Bourne Shell (Steve Bourne, Bell Labs); bash (Bourne-Again SHell) is the recommended successor.
Environment variables are set with 'export var=value'; 'set' shows all shell variables, 'printenv' shows only exported ones.
Unix tools originated at AT&T Bell Labs in the early 1970s on slow teletype terminals, favouring compact text notations.

terms.Bourne Shell: Original Unix shell written by Steve Bourne at Bell Labs, basis for bash and sh.
terms.bash: Bo(u)rn(e)-Again SHell; GNU reimplementation of Bourne Shell, widely available and recommended.
terms.stdin/stdout/stderr: Standard streams 0, 1, 2 provided to every process by the shell at startup.
terms.environment variable: Named string value exported to a shell and all programs it invokes, accessed via $NAME.
terms.export: Shell built-in that makes a variable visible to child processes as part of the environment.
terms.file descriptor: Small integer identifying an open stream/file within a process (0, 1, 2 for standard streams).
terms.Unix philosophy: Design principle: small tools each doing one thing well, combined to solve larger problems.

exam_tips: Know the three standard stream numbers (0, 1, 2) and their purposes; questions may ask about redirection syntax targeting specific descriptors.
Distinguish between shell built-ins (like cd) and external programs; built-ins affect shell state directly.
Be able to explain why the shell can be an ordinary user-mode program and why this matters for extensibility.
Remember that 'export' is required for a variable to be visible to child processes, not just the current shell.

key_facts: '>' redirects stdout to a file (truncating), '>>' appends; '<' redirects stdin from a file.
'2>' redirects stderr; '2>&1' redirects stderr to wherever stdout currently goes; order matters when combining with pipes.
Pipe '|' connects stdout of one process to stdin of another; both run concurrently.
'*' expands to all non-dot filenames in current directory; '?' matches any single character; expansion done by shell before command executes.
Inside single quotes all special characters lose meaning; inside double quotes '\', '$', and backtick retain special roles.
Job control: '&' starts background job; Ctrl-Z suspends; 'fg'/'bg' move jobs; 'jobs' lists; 'kill %n' terminates job n.

terms.redirection: Shell mechanism to connect a command's standard streams to files rather than terminal.
terms.pipe: Shell construct '|' feeding stdout of one command directly to stdin of the next.
terms.tee: Command that copies stdin to stdout and simultaneously writes a copy to a named file.
terms.here document: '<<WORD' syntax embedding inline input in a script up to a line matching WORD.
terms.glob/wildcard expansion: Shell replacement of patterns like '*' and '?' with matching filenames before command execution.
terms.job control: Shell facility to run, suspend, background, and foreground multiple processes.
terms.2>&1: Redirect file descriptor 2 (stderr) to the same destination as file descriptor 1 (stdout).
terms.$?: Shell variable holding exit code of the most recently executed foreground command.

exam_tips: Order of redirections matters: 'cmd >log 2>&1' differs from 'cmd 2>&1 >log'; know which is correct to merge stderr into a file.
Understand that pipeline stdout redirection is set up before per-command redirections, explaining why '2>&1 |' works.
Know that wildcard expansion is performed by the shell, not the invoked program, so it works with any command.
Single vs double quotes is commonly tested: single quotes suppress ALL special characters including '$' and backticks.

key_facts: Shebang line '#!/bin/sh' as first line of a file tells the kernel which interpreter to use; '#' makes it a comment in the script.
Script arguments: $1, $2, ... for positional args; $* for all args; $# for count; $0 for script name.
Shell control structures: if/then/elif/else/fi, while/do/done, for/do/done, case/esac.
'test' command (also written '[') checks file existence, permissions, string and integer comparisons; used heavily in conditionals.
Shell functions defined as 'function name() { commands }' are more efficient than separate scripts on PATH.
Command substitution via $(command) or backticks embeds command output into another command or assignment.

terms.shebang: First line '#!/path/to/interpreter' directing kernel to use specified program to run the script.
Positional parameters holding arguments passed to a shell script.
terms.$*: Special variable expanding to all positional parameters as a single string.
terms.$#: Special variable holding the count of positional parameters passed to a script.
terms.test / [: Shell built-in evaluating conditions on files, strings, and integers; returns exit code 0 for true.
terms.command substitution: $(cmd) or `cmd` syntax replacing itself with the stdout of the executed command.
terms.shell function: Named reusable block of shell commands defined with 'function name() { ... }' syntax.
terms.&& and ||: Short-circuit operators: A && B runs B only if A succeeds (exit 0); A || B runs B only if A fails.

exam_tips: Know the syntax of if/while/case exactly, including required semicolons or newlines before 'then', 'do', 'in'.
Distinguish $* (all args as one string) from $@ (all args as separate words) — subtle but sometimes tested.
Remember that shell functions execute in the current shell and can modify shell state, unlike subshell or separate scripts.
The '[ ]' test syntax requires spaces around brackets and the closing ']'; omitting spaces is a common error.

key_facts: grep searches files for lines matching a regular expression; -i case-insensitive, -v invert, -l list filenames only, -c count matches.
sed processes a stream line by line; 's/pattern/replacement/g' substitutes globally; commands can be address-limited by line number or regex.
find traverses directory trees; conditions include -name, -atime/-mtime/-ctime, -type, -perm; actions include -print and -exec.
find's -exec runs a command per matched file with '{}' as placeholder and '\;' terminating; xargs is more efficient for many files.
sed back-references: \(...\) captures a group; \1-\9 replays captured text in replacement.
tr performs character-by-character substitution, deletion (-d), or squeeze (-s) of repeated characters on a byte stream.

terms.grep: Tool printing lines of files matching a regular expression; egrep supports extended regex.
terms.sed: Stream editor applying scripted substitutions and deletions to input line by line, outputting to stdout.
terms.find: Utility traversing directory hierarchies and selecting files by name, date, type, or permissions.
terms.xargs: Builds and executes commands using lines from stdin as arguments, handling large argument lists safely.
terms.tr: Character transliteration filter replacing, deleting, or squeezing characters in a byte stream.
terms.-exec: find action executing a command for each matched file, with '{}' substituted by filename.
terms.egrep / fgrep: Variants of grep: egrep supports full extended regex (|, +, ?, grouping); fgrep matches fixed strings only.
terms.address (sed): Line number, '$', or /regex/ prefix in sed limiting which lines a command applies to.

exam_tips: Know which regex features require egrep vs grep: alternation '|', grouping '()', '+' and '?' require egrep.
sed back-references use backslashed parens '\(' '\)' in the pattern and '\1' in replacement — different from perl/egrep.
find conditions are ANDed by default; -o is OR; '!' negates; -exec {} \; runs per-file vs xargs for batching.
Common exam pattern: chain find | xargs grep or find -exec; know the difference in efficiency and syntax.

key_facts: RCS uses lock-modify-unlock model: 'ci' checks in, 'co' checks out read-only, 'co -l' locks for editing.
diff default output shows line numbers and changed lines; '-u' produces unified diffs suitable for patch and human reading.
CVS and Subversion use copy-modify-merge: files not locked by default; conflicts detected at commit time and merged.
Subversion ('svn') tracks file moves/copies unlike CVS; supports atomic commits of entire directory trees.
RCS stores files as base version plus incremental diffs internally; 'rcsdiff' compares working file to repository version.
Git (mentioned in conclusion context) and SVN/CVS support branching; branches allow parallel development without disturbing trunk.

terms.RCS: Revision Control System; tracks changes to individual files using lock-modify-unlock model.
terms.ci / co: RCS commands: 'ci' checks a file into the repository; 'co' checks it out (read-only or locked).
terms.diff: Tool comparing two files and outputting their differences; '-u' gives unified diff format.
terms.patch: Tool applying a diff/unified-diff output to reconstruct the modified file.
terms.CVS: Concurrent Versions System; layer above RCS adding directory-tree and network support with copy-modify-merge.
terms.Subversion (svn): Version control system replacing CVS; supports atomic commits, file moves, and remote repositories.
terms.lock-modify-unlock: RCS model where only one user may edit a file at a time via exclusive locks.
terms.copy-modify-merge: CVS/SVN model where multiple users edit simultaneously and conflicts are merged at commit time.
terms.unified diff (-u): diff output format showing context lines with +/- prefixes, widely used for patches.

exam_tips: Know the fundamental difference between RCS lock-modify-unlock and CVS/SVN copy-modify-merge workflows.
diff with no options vs '-u' vs '-e' produce very different outputs; know what each is used for.
rcsdiff compares working file to latest checked-in version — useful for seeing uncommitted changes.
Understand why branching is needed for long-running parallel development and what 'merging back to trunk' means.

key_facts: make reads a Makefile (default filename), compares file timestamps of targets and dependencies, and runs commands only when needed.
Makefile rules: 'target: dependencies' on one line followed by tab-indented shell commands; tab (not spaces) is mandatory.
make macros: $@ = current target filename; $< = prerequisite (source) filename; $* = prerequisite without suffix.
Suffix rules like '.c.o:' define generic build recipes; SUFFIXES must be declared if using non-standard extensions.
Variables in Makefiles are accessed as $(VAR) with round parentheses; can be overridden on the command line.
'makedepend' auto-generates header file dependencies and appends them to Makefile, preventing stale build bugs.

terms.make: Build automation tool that tracks file dependencies via timestamps and executes minimal rebuild commands.
terms.Makefile: File containing targets, dependencies, and recipes read by make (also accepted: 'makefile').
terms.target: Named output file (or phony goal) in a Makefile rule that make attempts to keep up to date.
terms.dependency: File listed after ':' in a make rule; if newer than target, the recipe is executed.
terms.suffix rule: Generic make rule like '.c.o:' specifying how to build one file type from another.
terms.$@: make automatic variable expanding to the name of the current target being built.
terms.$<: make automatic variable expanding to the first prerequisite (source) of the current rule.
terms.SHELL = /bin/sh: Recommended Makefile setting ensuring consistent command interpretation regardless of user's shell.
terms.touch: Unix command updating a file's timestamp to now, used to trick make into skipping a rebuild.

exam_tips: Recipe lines in Makefiles MUST start with a tab character, not spaces — a very common and confusing error.
Know the three automatic variables $@, $<, $* and when each is used in suffix/pattern rules.
make rebuilds a target if ANY dependency has a newer timestamp; understand how this propagates through a dependency graph.
Phony targets (like 'clean', 'depend') do not correspond to real files; know their purpose and that they always run.

key_facts: Perl regex match: $var =~ /pattern/; substitute: $var =~ s/pattern/replacement/flags; 'g' flag for global replacement.
Captured groups in Perl regex use plain parentheses (not backslashed); captured text available as $1, $2, ... after match.
Perl reads all input into @array with '<STDIN>' or '<>'; scalar $_ holds current line in implicit loops.
Hash tables (associative arrays) referenced as %hash; individual entries as $hash{key}; 'keys %hash' returns all keys.
Backticks in Perl execute a shell command and return its output as a string/list, e.g. @lines = `grep -n pat file`.
Perl's 'split(/regex/, $string)' splits a string on regex matches returning an array; 'chomp' removes trailing newlines.

terms.=~: Perl binding operator applying a regex match or substitution to a variable.
terms.$_: Perl default variable implicitly used by many operations (print, chomp, regex match) when no explicit variable given.
terms.@array: Perl array variable (list); elements accessed as $array[n]; assigned from list expressions or '<>'.
terms.%hash: Perl hash (associative array); entries accessed as $hash{key}; 'keys %hash' returns list of keys.
terms.chomp: Perl function removing trailing newline from a string or all elements of an array.
terms.split: Perl function dividing a string by a regex delimiter, returning a list of substrings.
terms.backquotes / qx: Perl syntax executing a shell command and capturing its stdout as a string.
terms.s/pat/repl/flags: Perl substitution operator; 'g' replaces all occurrences; 'i' case-insensitive.

exam_tips: Perl regex groups use plain '()' not backslashed '\(\)' as in sed/grep — a very common confusion in exam answers.
Know that $1, $2 etc. hold captured groups after a successful match and can be used in the replacement of s///.
Understand the difference between scalar ($), array (@), and hash (%) sigils and when each is used.
Backtick command substitution in Perl works like shell backticks but returns output as string/array to Perl, not to the shell.

key_facts: A generative model specifies a parameterized distribution PrX(x; θ) and is fitted by maximizing the log likelihood of observed data over θ.
Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) chooses parameters θ to maximize log Pr(data; θ), which is equivalent to minimizing negative log likelihood.
For a Markov chain, the likelihood of a sequence factors as Pr(x0,...,xn) = Pr(x0) * product_{i=1}^{n} Pr(xi | x_{i-1}) by memorylessness.
Autoregressive modelling fits a Markov model by regressing xi against xi-1, which is a special case of MLE for sequence data.
The sum of independent Poisson random variables is also Poisson: Pois(x) + Pois(y) ~ Pois(x+y).
The R number in epidemic models equals λ × (1/p), the expected new infections per person over their entire illness.

terms.generative model: A probability model PrX(x; θ) that can generate synthetic data by sampling from the distribution
terms.maximum likelihood estimation: Choosing parameters θ to maximize the probability (likelihood) of the observed dataset
terms.autoregressive model: A sequence model where each item is predicted from the previous item, equivalent to regression of xi against xi-1
terms.emission probability: In a hidden Markov model, q(xi|zi) is the probability of observation xi given hidden state zi
terms.log likelihood: The logarithm of the likelihood function, used for numerical stability and to convert products to sums
terms.teacher forcing: Another name for autoregressive modelling, where the true previous value is used to predict the next value

exam_tips: When deriving MLE for a Markov chain, remember to drop terms that don't depend on the parameters being estimated (e.g. the distribution of X0).
Know how to write out the full likelihood for a sequence model and reduce it using conditional probability and memorylessness.
Be careful to distinguish between fitting a Markov chain (counting transitions for discrete state spaces) and fitting a parametric model using MLE for continuous state spaces.
Autoregressive fitting for Xi+1 = a + bXi + N(0,σ²) reduces exactly to linear regression—know how to show this.

key_facts: The stationary distribution π of a Markov chain satisfies π = πP (where π is a row vector), plus the normalization constraint π⊤1 = 1.
Detailed balance πx Pxy = πy Pyx implies stationarity: if detailed balance holds then π is a stationary distribution.
The stationarity equations (P-I)⊤π = 0 combined with 1⊤π = 1 can be solved using np.linalg.lstsq with an overdetermined system.
For a random walk on an undirected graph, the stationary probability of state x is proportional to the degree mx (number of edges from x).
Multi-step transition probabilities satisfy P(Xn = j | X0 = i) = [P^n]_ij, i.e. the (i,j) entry of the n-th power of the transition matrix.
Projection onto a subspace minimizes squared distance; fitting linear models is equivalent to projecting response vectors onto the column space of the feature matrix.

terms.transition matrix: Matrix P where Pij is the probability of jumping from state i to state j in one step
terms.stationary distribution: Distribution π such that if X0 ~ π then X1 ~ π; satisfies π = πP
terms.detailed balance: Condition πx Pxy = πy Pyx for all states x,y; sufficient (but not necessary) for π to be stationary
terms.irreducibility: Property that there exists a path from any state to any other state in the Markov chain
terms.n-step transition probability: P(Xn = j | X0 = i) = [P^n]_ij, the (i,j) entry of the transition matrix raised to the n-th power
terms.least-squares solution: Solution returned by np.linalg.lstsq that minimizes squared residuals, handles overdetermined or underdetermined systems

exam_tips: When asked to find the stationary distribution, first check if detailed balance applies—it requires much less algebra than solving the full stationarity equations.
For multi-step transition probabilities, always use matrix exponentiation: P(X2=j|X0=i) = [P²]_ij, derived using LoTP and memorylessness.
Remember that π = πP means π is a LEFT eigenvector of P with eigenvalue 1; don't confuse with Pπ = π (right eigenvector convention).
When setting up stationarity equations, you have one redundant equation—use np.linalg.lstsq with the normalization constraint added as an extra row.

key_facts: The Cambridge weather example has state space {r, d, g} with transition matrix where rows sum to 1; P(X2=r|X0=g) = [P²]_gr = 0.15.
Memorylessness in Markov chains: P(Xn=xn | Xn-1=xn-1,...,X0=x0) = P(Xn=xn | Xn-1=xn-1); conditional on the present, the future is independent of the past.
The law of total probability has a 'with baggage' version: P(A|C) = Σ_i P(A|Bi,C) P(Bi|C), carrying the conditioning event through every term.
Bayes's rule with baggage: P(A|B,C) = P(B|A,C) P(A|C) / P(B|C).
The mean-reverting random walk Xn+1 = μ + λ(Xn - μ) + N(0,σ²) with 0<λ<1 models 'red noise'; λ≈1 gives long excursions, λ≈0 gives rapid reversion.
For the hitting probability theorem, hx = Σ_y Pxy hy for all x not in A, with hx = 1 for x in A; if multiple solutions exist, take the minimum non-negative solution.

terms.memorylessness: The Markov property: P(Xn=xn | Xn-1,...,X0) = P(Xn=xn | Xn-1); the future depends only on the present state
terms.hitting probability: hx = P(ever hit set A | start at x); satisfies hx = Σ_y Pxy hy for x not in A
terms.white noise: Independent identically distributed noise at each timestep, as in N(0,σ²) added independently each step
terms.red noise: Correlated noise modelled as a mean-reverting random walk; hot/cold spells persist across timesteps
terms.time-homogeneous Markov chain: A Markov chain where the transition probabilities are the same at every timestep, allowing clock-resetting
terms.absorbing state: A state with no outgoing transitions except to itself; once entered, the chain cannot leave

exam_tips: Always carry the 'baggage' through every term when applying LoTP or Bayes's rule with a conditioning event—this is a common source of errors.
For hitting probability problems, derive equations from scratch using LoTP + memorylessness + clock-resetting rather than just citing the theorem.
When computing multi-step probabilities, introduce intermediate states via LoTP with baggage, then apply memorylessness to drop the extra conditioning.
For the hitting probability system of equations with finite state space, use np.linalg.solve after setting up the modified matrix with boundary conditions imposed.

key_facts: The existence and uniqueness theorem for stationary distributions requires (1) finite state space and (2) irreducibility; under these conditions a unique stationary distribution exists.
A Markov chain is aperiodic at state x if there exists n0 such that P(Xn=x|X0=x) > 0 for all n ≥ n0; irreducibility plus one aperiodic state implies all states are aperiodic.
Ergodic theorem: for irreducible Markov chains with stationary distribution π, P(lim_{n→∞} (1/n)Σ1_{Xi=x} = πx) = 1 regardless of initial state X0.
Convergence theorem: if a chain is irreducible and has an aperiodic state, then lim_{n→∞} P(Xn=x|X0=x0) = πx for all states x and x0.
Time averages (ergodic averages over a single trace) and ensemble averages (over many simulations) can differ for non-irreducible chains.
The pathological chain with two disjoint communicating classes has multiple stationary distributions; the general solution is a convex combination of the per-class stationary distributions.

terms.ergodic average: Time average (1/n)Σ_{i=1}^{n} 1_{Xi=x} over a single simulation trace
terms.ensemble average: Average E[·] over all possible traces, representing probability at a given instant across many simulations
terms.aperiodic state: State x where P(Xn=x|X0=x) > 0 for all sufficiently large n; the chain can return in variable numbers of steps
terms.tipping point: A state after which the chain's long-run behaviour is determined; e.g. state a in the pathological chain
terms.irreducible Markov chain: A chain where every state is reachable from every other state; guarantees unique stationary distribution for finite state spaces
terms.communicating class: A maximal set of states that can all reach each other; irreducible chains have exactly one communicating class

exam_tips: To check aperiodicity, find two return times to a state whose GCD is 1—then by the integer linear combination argument all sufficiently large return times are achievable.
Distinguish carefully between time averages converging to π (ergodic theorem, requires irreducibility) and ensemble probabilities converging to π (requires irreducibility + aperiodicity).
For chains that are not irreducible, the stationarity equations may have multiple solutions; the physical interpretation is that different runs end up in different communicating classes.
When asked whether a stationary distribution exists and is unique, explicitly verify both conditions: finite state space AND irreducibility.

key_facts: Drift δ(x) = E(Xn+1 - Xn | Xn = x); the drift model xn+1 = xn + δ(xn) is a deterministic approximation to the Markov chain.
For the epidemic model Xn+1 = Xn + Pois(rXn/d) - Bin(Xn, 1/d), drift is δ(x) = x(r-1)/d; positive for r>1 giving exponential growth at rate (r-1)/d per day.
For the active-users model Xn+1 = Xn + Pois(λ) - Bin(Xn, 1/d), drift is δ(x) = λ - x/d with fixed point x̂ = λd.
A recurrent neural network models sequences via (pi, vi) = fθ(xi-1, vi-1), Xi ~ Cat(pi), where vi is a learned history digest replacing fixed-window history.
The likelihood of a sequence from a hidden Markov model requires summing over all hidden trajectories: Pr(x0..xn) = Σ_{z0..zn} Π q(xi|zi) p(z0) Π p(zi|zi-1), making it intractable.
Markov's trigram model generates the next character based on the preceding two characters; it is equivalent to a Markov chain on pairs of tokens.

terms.drift: Expected rate of change δ(x) = E(Xn+1 - Xn | Xn = x); characterizes the tendency of the chain to move up or down
terms.fixed point: State x̂ where the drift is zero: δ(x̂) = 0; the chain fluctuates around this value
terms.drift model: Deterministic approximation xn+1 = xn + δ(xn) that ignores random fluctuations and follows expected change
terms.recurrent neural network (RNN): A sequence model where a neural network fθ updates a hidden state vi from the previous token and state, encoding history
terms.hidden Markov model: A two-layer model with hidden Markov chain Z0,Z1,... and observations Xn generated from Zn; allows for sequence modelling with latent dynamics
terms.particle filter: Computational Bayesian method maintaining a weighted sample ('swarm') from P(Zi|X0..Xi), updated at each timestep with new observations
terms.Viterbi algorithm: Dynamic programming algorithm that finds the most likely hidden state sequence in an HMM given observations
terms.trigram model: Language model generating next character based on preceding two characters; equivalent to Markov chain on character pairs

exam_tips: For drift analysis, calculate E(Xn+1 | Xn=x) using linearity of expectation on each term separately (e.g. E[Pois(λ)]=λ, E[Bin(x,p)]=xp).
Know how to derive the likelihood formula for a Markov chain sequence using repeated application of conditional probability and memorylessness.
For the RNN, understand that the log likelihood is tractable (explicit formula exists) whereas for hidden Markov models it requires summing over exponentially many hidden paths.

When comparing epidemic and active-users models, focus on whether new arrivals are proportional to current state (epidemic: Pois(rXn/d)) or constant (active-users: Pois(λ))—this determines whether there is a stable fixed point.

key_facts: Competitive equilibrium occurs where supply and demand curves intersect (market clearing), giving equilibrium price p* and quantity q*.
Consumer surplus is the total amount consumers save relative to their reservation (willingness-to-pay) price; producer surplus is the total amount firms save relative to their marginal costs.
Perfect competition maximises total welfare (consumer + producer surplus) under assumptions: price-taking, no transaction costs, perfect information, no externalities, rational behaviour.

The 1st Welfare Theorem states that competitive equilibrium is Pareto efficient given fixed resources; the 2nd Welfare Theorem states any Pareto efficient allocation can be supported as a competitive equilibrium for some endowments.
Pareto efficiency means no reallocation can make someone better off without making someone else worse off; it is a weak notion — both pure monarchy and pure communism can be Pareto efficient.
Partial equilibrium studies one market in isolation; general equilibrium studies the whole economy with interacting markets.

terms.Pareto improvement: A change that makes at least one person better off without making anyone worse off.
terms.Pareto efficient: An allocation where no Pareto improvement is possible.
terms.Consumer surplus: The aggregate difference between consumers' willingness-to-pay and the market price they actually pay.
terms.Producer surplus: The aggregate difference between the market price firms receive and their marginal costs of production.
terms.Deadweight loss: The reduction in total welfare caused by market distortions such as monopoly pricing.
terms.Market clearing: The condition where quantity supplied equals quantity demanded, determining equilibrium price and quantity.
terms.Willingness to pay (WTP): The maximum price a consumer is prepared to pay for a good.
terms.Marginal cost: The cost of producing one additional unit of output.

exam_tips: Be precise about the distinction between Pareto efficiency and social desirability — examiners test whether students understand that Pareto efficiency says nothing about equity or fairness.
Know the five assumptions required for the welfare theorems and be able to name a real-world violation of each (e.g., monopoly violates price-taking).
Questions often ask you to identify and shade consumer surplus, producer surplus, and deadweight loss on a supply-demand diagram — practise drawing and labelling these correctly.
Do not confuse partial equilibrium (one market) with general equilibrium (whole economy) — exam questions may specify which framework to use.

key_facts: Digital goods have high fixed costs but near-zero marginal cost — under perfect competition, price approaches zero, so firms must use other strategies to recover fixed costs.

A discriminating monopolist who can charge each consumer their exact willingness-to-pay achieves Pareto efficiency (no deadweight loss) but captures all surplus, leaving consumers with zero consumer surplus.
Consumer lock-in occurs when switching costs (technical, financial, psychological) prevent users from moving to competitors, allowing firms to raise prices or restrict choice.

Direct network effects: product value increases with the number of users of the same product (e.g., WhatsApp). Indirect network effects: value increases with the number of complementary goods or services (e.g., more apps for a platform).
Market tipping: once a platform exceeds a critical mass of users, network effects create a winner-takes-all outcome, leading to highly concentrated digital markets.
Bundling can increase revenue by exploiting heterogeneous valuations across consumers — a bundle price can capture both consumers even when individual product prices would not.

terms.Lock-in: A situation where users face high switching costs that prevent them from changing to a competing product or service.
terms.Network effect: The phenomenon whereby a product or service becomes more valuable as more people use it.
terms.Direct network effect: Value to a user increases directly with the number of other users of the same product.
terms.Indirect network effect: Value increases due to growth in complementary goods or services rather than the user base itself.
terms.Market tipping: The process by which a market with network effects converges to a single dominant firm past a threshold of adoption.
terms.Versioning: Price discrimination strategy of offering different tiers of a product (e.g., Basic/Pro/Premium) to capture different willingness-to-pay levels.
terms.Windowing: Releasing a product sequentially across formats or channels at different prices to segment consumers by willingness-to-pay over time.
terms.Bundling: Selling multiple products together at a single price to exploit differences in consumer valuations across products.
terms.Decoy effect: Offering a dominated option to make another option appear more attractive, shifting consumer choice.
terms.Increasing returns to scale: A situation where average cost falls as output increases, common in digital goods with high fixed and near-zero marginal costs.

exam_tips: Examiners frequently ask why digital markets tend towards monopoly — link your answer explicitly to near-zero marginal cost, economies of scale, network effects, and lock-in as reinforcing mechanisms.

When discussing price discrimination, distinguish clearly between first-degree (perfect/discriminating), second-degree (versioning/bundling), and third-degree discrimination — confusing these is a common error.
Be prepared to analyse the Booking.com or Apple decoy examples: explain the economic mechanism (exploiting reference dependence or anchoring) not just describe the observation.
The bundling example is frequently testable — be able to show arithmetically why a bundle price can yield higher revenue than separate pricing when consumer valuations are negatively correlated.

key_facts: An externality is a cost or benefit from an activity affecting others that is not reflected in market prices; negative externalities lead to overproduction, positive externalities to underproduction relative to the social optimum.

A public good is non-rivalrous (one person's use does not reduce availability) and non-excludable (cannot prevent others from benefiting); private markets underprovide public goods due to the free-rider problem.
A public bad is non-rivalrous and non-excludable harm — e.g., spam, botnets, online misinformation — and private markets overproduce them.
A club good is non-rivalrous but excludable — multiple users can share it without reducing others' benefits, but access is restricted.
Adverse selection arises from hidden information before a transaction (e.g., used-car lemons), causing low-quality goods to crowd out high-quality ones and potentially collapsing the market.
Moral hazard arises from hidden actions after a transaction — e.g., insured drivers taking less care — because one party can shift risk to another without being monitored.
The Pigou-Dalton principle: due to diminishing marginal utility, transferring income from rich to poor generally increases social welfare.
Collusion (tacit or explicit) allows firms to extract monopoly rents; AI trading algorithms have been shown to learn to collude tacitly without explicit communication.

terms.Externality: A cost or benefit from an economic activity that falls on third parties and is not reflected in market prices.
terms.Public good: A good that is both non-rivalrous and non-excludable, leading to underprovision by private markets.
terms.Public bad: A non-rivalrous and non-excludable harm, overproduced by markets because costs are shared involuntarily.
terms.Club good: A good that is non-rivalrous but excludable, shared within a restricted group.
terms.Free-rider problem: The incentive to benefit from a public good without contributing to its provision, leading to underprovision.
terms.Adverse selection: Market failure caused by hidden information before a transaction, leading to suboptimal market outcomes.
terms.Moral hazard: Market failure caused by hidden actions after a transaction, where one party takes on more risk because another bears the cost.
terms.Rent: Income derived from market power rather than productive activity; undeserved profit sustained by barriers to competition.
terms.Tacit collusion: Firms coordinating prices or output without explicit agreement, e.g., through algorithmic pricing.
terms.Asymmetric information: A situation where one party to a transaction has more or better information than the other.

exam_tips: Know all four good-type categories (private, public, club, public bad) and be able to classify real examples — examiners test the rivalrous/excludable matrix.
Distinguish adverse selection (hidden information, pre-transaction) from moral hazard (hidden action, post-transaction) clearly — confusing the two is among the most common errors.

When answering on digital externalities (e.g., data collection, social media misinformation), relate back to why the Coase theorem bargaining solution fails in practice (diffuse harms across millions of users).
Lobbying and collusion questions may ask you to connect rent-seeking behaviour to market power — be ready to use the concept of 'rent' as undeserved income sustained by barriers to competition.

key_facts: The course covers four standard auction types: English (ascending), Dutch (descending), first-price sealed-bid, and second-price sealed-bid (Vickrey).
Revenue Equivalence Theorem: under standard assumptions (symmetric, risk-neutral bidders, independent private values), all four major auction formats yield the same expected revenue to the seller.
In a second-price (Vickrey) auction, bidding one's true valuation is a dominant strategy — it is always optimal regardless of what others do.
Nash equilibrium: a strategy profile where no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally deviating, given the strategies of all other players.
Mechanism design ('reverse game theory') asks what rules or institutions should be designed to achieve a desired outcome, given strategic agents.
Winner's curse: in common-value auctions, the winner tends to have overestimated the value; rational bidders should shade their bids below their estimates to correct for this.

terms.English auction: An open ascending-bid auction where the highest bidder wins and pays their bid.
terms.Dutch auction: An open descending-bid auction where the first bidder to accept the current price wins.
terms.First-price sealed-bid auction: Bidders submit one sealed bid; the highest bidder wins and pays their bid.
terms.Second-price sealed-bid auction (Vickrey: Bidders submit one sealed bid; the highest bidder wins but pays the second-highest bid.
terms.Dominant strategy: A strategy that yields the best outcome for a player regardless of what other players do.
terms.Nash equilibrium: A set of strategies where no player can benefit by unilaterally changing their strategy, given others' strategies.
terms.Revenue equivalence theorem: Result showing that four standard auction formats yield the same expected revenue under symmetric, risk-neutral, independent private value conditions.
terms.Winner's curse: The tendency for the winner of a common-value auction to have overbid because the highest estimate of value is likely an overestimate.
terms.Mechanism design: The field that designs rules and incentives to achieve desired outcomes in the presence of strategic, self-interested agents.
terms.Combinatorial auction: An auction in which bidders can place bids on combinations (packages) of items rather than individual items.

exam_tips: Know why truthful bidding is dominant in second-price auctions — be able to prove it by showing bidding above or below valuation cannot improve outcomes.
Revenue equivalence is a key theorem: state its assumptions carefully (risk-neutral, symmetric, independent private values) — exam questions often test whether you know when it fails.
The winner's curse applies to common-value auctions, not private-value auctions — be clear about this distinction and able to explain the correction strategy (bid-shading).
Mechanism design questions often appear as 'design a system that...': frame your answer around incentive compatibility and individual rationality constraints.

key_facts: Criminal law involves the state prosecuting individuals for wrongs against society; civil law involves disputes between private parties seeking remedies such as damages or injunctions.
Contract law requires offer, acceptance, consideration, and intention to create legal relations for a contract to be legally binding.
Tort law provides civil remedies for wrongs causing harm without necessarily involving a contractual relationship; negligence requires duty of care, breach of that duty, and resulting damage.
Defamation (libel for written, slander for spoken) involves a false statement of fact that damages someone's reputation; truth is an absolute defence.
Jurisdiction determines which court and legal system has authority over a dispute — particularly complex for online activities crossing national boundaries.
Equity is a body of law developed to provide fairness where common law rules produce harsh results, including remedies like injunctions and specific performance.

terms.Criminal law: Body of law under which the state prosecutes individuals for acts deemed harmful to society, with penalties including imprisonment.
terms.Civil law: Body of law governing disputes between private parties, with remedies typically being compensation (damages) or injunctions.
terms.Tort: A civil wrong causing harm or loss, giving rise to liability independent of any contract.
terms.Negligence: A tort requiring proof of a duty of care, breach of that duty, and resulting damage causally linked to the breach.
terms.Defamation: Publication of a false statement of fact that damages a person's reputation; libel (written), slander (spoken).
terms.Contract: A legally binding agreement requiring offer, acceptance, consideration, and intention to create legal relations.
terms.Jurisdiction: The authority of a court or legal system to hear and decide a case.
terms.Injunction: A court order requiring a party to do or refrain from doing a specific act.
terms.Consideration: Something of value exchanged between parties to a contract, required for the contract to be legally enforceable.
terms.Equity: A body of legal principles developed to supplement common law and ensure fairness in specific circumstances.

exam_tips: Distinguish carefully between criminal and civil law — different standards of proof (beyond reasonable doubt vs. balance of probabilities) and different parties (state vs. private individuals).
For negligence questions, always apply the three-part test: duty of care, breach, damage — missing any element means no liability.
Jurisdiction issues are especially relevant for internet law questions — be ready to explain why it is difficult to determine which country's law applies to online conduct.
Defamation questions often test the difference between libel and slander, and the key defences (truth, honest opinion, privilege) — know these precisely.

key_facts: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requires lawful basis for processing personal data, data minimisation, purpose limitation, and grants data subjects rights including access, rectification, and erasure.
The Computer Misuse Act 1990 (UK) criminalises unauthorised access to computer systems, unauthorised access with intent to commit further offences, and unauthorised modification of computer material.
E-commerce regulations impose disclosure requirements on online traders and affect the formation of electronic contracts.
The Digital Services Act (EU) and Online Safety Act (UK) impose duties on platforms to moderate content and address harmful material.
Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations (PECR) govern cookies, electronic marketing, and interception of communications.
Geo-blocking and data localisation rules create frictions in digital markets — e.g., EU rules requiring data to be stored within the EU affect cloud providers.

terms.GDPR: General Data Protection Regulation — EU law governing the processing of personal data, with extraterritorial effect for data of EU residents.
terms.Computer Misuse Act 1990: UK legislation criminalising unauthorised access to computer systems and unauthorised modification of computer material.
terms.Data subject: An individual whose personal data is being processed.
terms.Data controller: An entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data.
terms.Right to erasure: GDPR right allowing individuals to request deletion of their personal data under certain conditions.
terms.PECR: Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations — UK rules governing cookies, unsolicited electronic marketing, and interception.
terms.Digital Services Act: EU regulation imposing due-diligence and content-moderation obligations on large online platforms.
terms.Online Safety Act: UK legislation placing duties on platforms to protect users from harmful content online.
terms.Lawful basis: A GDPR requirement that processing of personal data must have a recognised legal justification (e.g., consent, legitimate interest, contract).
terms.Geo-blocking: Restricting access to digital content or services based on the user's geographic location.

exam_tips: GDPR questions often test the lawful bases for processing and data subject rights — memorise the six lawful bases and the main rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection).
The Computer Misuse Act has three main offences at different levels of severity — be able to state each and give an example.

Examiners may ask you to apply internet law to a scenario involving liability of platforms for user-generated content — know the distinction between active and passive hosts and safe harbour provisions.
Jurisdictional complexity is a recurring theme — be ready to explain why the same online act may be legal in one country and illegal in another, and how courts attempt to resolve this.

key_facts: Virtue ethics (Aristotle) focuses on the moral character of the agent — virtues lie between extremes (vices), e.g., courage lies between rashness and cowardice — and are developed through habit and practice.
Consequentialism judges actions solely by their outcomes; utilitarianism (Bentham, Mill) seeks to maximise total welfare W = ΣUi ('greatest happiness of the greatest number').

Deontology (Kant) holds that some actions are morally required or forbidden regardless of consequences; the Categorical Imperative requires acting only according to principles you could will to be universal laws.
Rawlsian ethics ('veil of ignorance') requires making moral decisions without knowing one's social position; this leads to maximising the welfare of the worst-off: W = min Ui.
Arrow's Impossibility Theorem (1951) proves it is impossible to design a social welfare function that simultaneously satisfies universal admissibility, non-imposition, and non-dictatorship.

Professional codes of ethics (e.g., ACM Code) are typically rules-based and include principles such as: avoid harm, be honest and trustworthy, respect privacy, honour confidentiality, and contribute to human well-being.
The Menlo Report provides core principles for computer science research ethics: respect for persons, beneficence, justice, and respect for law and public interest.

terms.Virtue ethics: Ethical framework focusing on the moral character and virtues of the agent rather than rules or consequences.
terms.Consequentialism: Ethical framework holding that the morality of an action depends solely on its consequences.
terms.Utilitarianism: Consequentialist theory aiming to maximise total happiness or welfare across all affected individuals.
terms.Deontology: Ethical framework holding that actions are right or wrong based on duties and principles, not consequences.
terms.Categorical Imperative: Kant's principle that one should act only according to maxims one could universalise as laws for everyone.
terms.Rawlsian ethics: Deontological approach using the 'veil of ignorance' to derive fair principles; prioritises the worst-off (maximin).
terms.Veil of ignorance: Rawls' thought experiment: making moral decisions without knowing one's own class, wealth, or social position.
terms.Arrow's Impossibility Theorem: Proof that no voting or social welfare function can satisfy all reasonable fairness criteria simultaneously.
terms.ACM Code of Ethics: Professional rules-based ethical code for computing professionals emphasising harm avoidance, honesty, fairness, and privacy.
terms.Ubuntu ethics: African ethical philosophy emphasising communal harmony, reciprocity, human dignity, and collective wellbeing over individualism.
terms.Collingridge dilemma: The observation that early technology is easy to regulate but risks are unclear; later, harms are clear but regulation is difficult.

exam_tips: Be able to apply all four frameworks (virtue ethics, ubuntu, consequentialism, deontology) to a single scenario and show how they may reach different conclusions — exam questions frequently ask for multi-framework analysis.
For trolley-problem style questions, explicitly state which framework you are applying and derive the answer from its principles rather than from intuition alone.
Arrow's Impossibility Theorem is technically testable — know the three conditions it requires and why satisfying all three simultaneously is impossible.

Professional ethics questions (ACM Code, Menlo Report) often ask you to identify which principle is violated in a given research or industry scenario — practise applying these to real cases like the Facebook emotional manipulation study.

key_facts: Coordinated vulnerability disclosure involves confidential disclosure to those who can fix a vulnerability, followed by public disclosure after an agreed period, changing the incentives of vendors to patch.
The Facebook emotional manipulation study (2014) manipulated 700,000 users' news feeds without informed consent, inducing negative emotions — widely condemned as an ethics violation.

Facial recognition technology raises competing ethical concerns: consequentialist arguments support its use if social benefits outweigh harms (security, convenience), while deontological arguments oppose it even with positive outcomes if it violates duties of consent and privacy.

Algorithmic bias in predictive systems (e.g., predicting criminality from facial images) can reproduce and amplify existing biases in criminal justice systems; such approaches are pseudoscientific and have been widely condemned.
The Reddit AI persuasion study (2020s) used AI-authored posts impersonating human identities without user consent — condemned for deception and experimenting on unwitting participants.

Privacy surveillance involves tensions between security/public safety benefits (consequentialist) and individual rights to privacy and autonomy (deontological), with technology companies acting as political actors shaping these debates.

terms.Coordinated vulnerability disclosure: The practice of privately reporting a security vulnerability to the affected party and publicly disclosing it after a set period to incentivise patching.
terms.Algorithmic bias: Systematic errors in algorithmic outputs that create unfair outcomes for particular groups, often reflecting biases in training data or model design.
terms.Predictive policing: The use of algorithms and data analysis to forecast where crimes are likely to occur or who is likely to commit them, raising concerns about bias and civil liberties.
terms.Informed consent: Ethical requirement that research participants understand and voluntarily agree to participation before the research takes place.
terms.Facial recognition: Technology that identifies or verifies individuals by analysing facial features, raising concerns about privacy, accuracy, and potential misuse.
terms.Front page test: An informal business ethics heuristic asking whether a decision would be reported as harmful or embarrassing if published in a newspaper.
terms.Menlo Report: A framework for ethics in computer science research establishing principles of respect for persons, beneficence, justice, and respect for law and public interest.
terms.Research Ethics Board: An independent committee (Ethics Committee in UK, IRB in US) that reviews research proposals to protect participants from harm.
terms.Bug bounty: A programme offering financial rewards to researchers who responsibly disclose security vulnerabilities to the affected organisation.
terms.Biological determinism: The discredited belief that behaviour, including criminality, is determined by biological traits — pseudoscientific basis for some flawed predictive algorithms.

exam_tips: Vulnerability disclosure questions often test the incentive structures: know why the party with the ability to fix a vulnerability may lack the incentive, and how timed public disclosure changes this.
When analysing surveillance or facial recognition scenarios, examiners expect you to apply multiple ethical frameworks and note conflicts — a purely one-sided answer will lose marks.

For research ethics questions, identify which of the Menlo Report's four principles (respect for persons, beneficence, justice, respect for law) is violated and explain how — use real case studies as evidence.

Algorithmic bias questions should address both the source of bias (training data, model design, feedback loops) and the ethical framework for evaluating harm — avoid vague statements about 'fairness' without definition.

key_facts: A surface is a closed 2-manifold if it is locally homeomorphic to a disk everywhere; boundary points are homeomorphic to a half-disk.
For every point x in manifold M, there exists an open ball B_x(r) such that M ∩ B_x(r) is homeomorphic to an open disk.
A parametric surface p(u,v) maps (u,v) ∈ R² to 3D coordinates (x(u,v), y(u,v), z(u,v)); tangent vectors are p_u = ∂p/∂u and p_v = ∂p/∂v.
A regular parametrization requires p_u × p_v ≠ 0, enabling a well-defined surface normal n = (p_u × p_v) / ||p_u × p_v||.
Discrete surfaces (triangle meshes) require approximation of differential quantities; local and global (Laplace-Beltrami) methods exist.

terms.2-manifold: A surface locally homeomorphic to an open disk at every interior point
terms.manifold with boundary: A surface where boundary points are locally homeomorphic to a half-disk
terms.homeomorphic: Topologically equivalent via a continuous bijective mapping with continuous inverse
terms.regular parametrization: A parametrization where the cross product of tangent vectors is non-zero everywhere
terms.tangent plane: The plane spanned by tangent vectors p_u and p_v at a point on a surface
terms.surface normal: Unit vector orthogonal to the tangent plane, n = (p_u × p_v)/||p_u × p_v||

exam_tips: Be precise about the manifold definition: interior points map to open disks, boundary points map to half-disks.
Know how to derive tangent vectors and the surface normal from a parametric representation.
Understand why regularity (p_u × p_v ≠ 0) is required for a well-defined normal.
Distinguish between smooth manifold geometry and its discrete (mesh) approximation.

key_facts: Normal curvature κ_n(φ) is the curvature of the curve formed by intersecting the surface with the plane through the surface normal n and tangent direction t at angle φ.
Euler's theorem: κ_n(φ) = κ_1 cos²φ + κ_2 sin²φ, where κ_1, κ_2 are principal curvatures and principal curvature planes are orthogonal.
Mean curvature H = (κ_1 + κ_2)/2 = (1/2π) ∫₀²π κ_n(φ)dφ; Gaussian curvature K = κ_1 · κ_2.
The Laplace-Beltrami operator applied to the coordinate function gives: Δ_M p = -2Hn (mean curvature normal).
Discrete Laplacian (uniform): L_u(v_i) = (1/|N(i)|) Σ_{j∈N(i)} (v_j - v_i), approximating -2Hn.
Discrete Gaussian curvature: K(v_i) = (1/A_i)(2π - Σ_j θ_j), where θ_j are angles at vertex i and A_i is the local area.

terms.normal curvature: Curvature κ_n(φ) of the curve formed by intersecting the surface with the plane through n and a tangent direction t
terms.principal curvatures: The minimum (κ_1) and maximum (κ_2) normal curvatures over all tangent directions at a point
terms.mean curvature: H = (κ_1 + κ_2)/2, the average of principal curvatures
terms.Gaussian curvature: K = κ_1 · κ_2, the product of principal curvatures; intrinsic property of the surface
terms.Laplace-Beltrami operator: Generalization of the Laplacian to manifold surfaces; Δ_M p = -2Hn when applied to coordinates
terms.osculating circle: Best-fitting circle at a point with radius r = 1/|κ|, tangent to the curve
terms.arc-length parametrization: Parametrization where ||∂p/∂t|| = 1, giving t = ∂p/∂t as unit tangent and ∂²p/∂t² = κ(t)n(t)
terms.umbilical point: Point where κ_1 = κ_2 (spherical/isotropic); all directions are principal directions

exam_tips: Know the classification of surface points: elliptic (K>0), parabolic (K=0), hyperbolic (K<0), spherical (K>0, κ_1=κ_2), planar (K=0, κ_1=κ_2=0).
Be able to derive principal curvatures from H and K: κ_1 = H - √(H²-K), κ_2 = H + √(H²-K).
Understand that the discrete mean curvature is ||L_c(v_i)||/2 where L_c is the cotangent-weighted Laplacian, not the uniform one.
Euler's theorem is frequently tested: the formula κ_n(φ) = κ_1 cos²φ + κ_2 sin²φ and the orthogonality of principal directions.

key_facts: The discrete Laplace-Beltrami operator can be extended to graphs and point clouds using Gaussian weights: h_t(x_i, x_j) = e^{-||x_i - x_j||²/t}.
The graph Laplacian for point clouds: L_g(x_i) = (1/Σh_t(x_i,x_j)) Σ h_t(x_i,x_j)(x_j - x_i), approximating the Laplace-Beltrami operator.
Local surface approximation and global Laplace-Beltrami approaches are the two main methods for computing differential quantities on discrete surfaces.
The mixed-area A_i for a vertex uses circumcenters if triangle angles < π/2, otherwise edge midpoints, summing triangle areas.

terms.geometry processing pipeline: Sequence of steps to acquire, reconstruct, process, and use 3D geometry
terms.point-set surface: Surface represented as an unstructured set of 3D sample points without explicit connectivity
terms.graph Laplacian: Discrete approximation of the Laplace-Beltrami operator defined on a graph or point cloud using weighted differences
terms.cotangent weights: Weights in the discrete Laplace-Beltrami operator based on cotangents of opposite angles in a triangle mesh
terms.mixed area: Local area element A_i around a vertex used in discrete curvature computation, based on circumcenters or edge midpoints

exam_tips: Know how the discrete Laplace-Beltrami extends from meshes to point clouds via Gaussian weighting.
Understand the difference between uniform and cotangent-weighted discrete Laplacians and when each is appropriate.
Be able to explain why circumcenters are used for the area computation and when edge midpoints replace them (obtuse triangles).

key_facts: The animation pipeline includes: storyboarding, concept design, 3D modeling, rigging, blend shapes creation, animation, and post-effects.
Rigging attaches a skeleton (bones + joints + hierarchy) to a 3D mesh; bones store rigid transformations (R_i, t_i); weights w_i(x) determine each bone's influence on mesh vertex x.
Linear Blend Skinning (LBS): T(x) = Σ w_i(x) T_i, x' = T(x)x in homogeneous coordinates; weights must sum to 1 (partition of unity).
LBS produces artifacts (candy-wrapper, elbow collapse) because linear combinations of rotation matrices are not valid rotation matrices: (w_1R_1 + w_2R_2)^T ≠ (w_1R_1 + w_2R_2)^{-1}.
Valid rotation matrices satisfy R^T = R^{-1} and det(R) = 1; the manifold of rotations is SO(3), rigid transformations SE(3).
Dual quaternion blending: b̂ = Σ w_i q̂_i / ||Σ w_i q̂_i|| produces valid rigid transformations, is coordinate-invariant, and approximates shortest-path blending on SE(3).

terms.rigging: Process of attaching an abstract skeleton to a 3D model to enable articulated animation
terms.linear blend skinning (LBS): Deformation method computing vertex positions as weighted linear combination of per-bone transformation matrices
terms.dual quaternion: Extension of quaternions using dual numbers (ε² = 0) representing both rotation and translation; 8 numbers
terms.quaternion: 4-number representation of rotation: q = cos(θ/2) + s·sin(θ/2), where s is the unit rotation axis
terms.SO(3): Manifold of valid 3D rotation matrices satisfying R^T = R^{-1} and det(R) = 1
terms.SE(3): Manifold of valid rigid transformations combining rotation and translation
terms.blend shapes: Set of target shapes used to generate intermediate expressions or poses via weighted combination
terms.partition of unity: Property Σ w_i(x) = 1 required for weights, ensuring volume preservation in blending
terms.candy-wrapper artifact: Volume loss artifact in LBS caused by invalid rotation matrices near joints with large rotations
terms.key-framing: Animation technique specifying poses at discrete time points and interpolating between them

exam_tips: Be able to prove why LBS rotation blending is invalid: show (w_1R_1 + w_2R_2)^T ≠ (w_1R_1 + w_2R_2)^{-1}.
Know the quaternion formula q = cos(θ/2) + s·sin(θ/2) and the rules ij=k, jk=i, ki=j, i²=j²=k²=-1.
Understand why dual quaternion blending requires normalization to produce valid rigid transformations.
The coordinate invariance property of dual quaternion blending (Σw_i q̂ q̂_i = q̂ Σw_i q̂_i) is important.

key_facts: The Rendering Equation: L_o(x, ω_o) = L_e(x, ω_o) + ∫_{H²} f_r(x, ω_i, ω_o) L_i(x, ω_i) cos θ_i dω_i; outgoing = emitted + reflected.
BRDF f_r(x, ω_i, ω_r) = dL_r(x, ω_r) / (L_i(x, ω_i) cos θ_i dω_i) [units: 1/sr], relating differential reflected radiance to incident radiance.
Radiance L(x, ω) = d²Φ(A) / (dω dA^⊥(x,ω)) = d²Φ / (dω dA cos θ) [W/m²sr]; remains constant along a ray in free space.
Direct illumination assumes L_i(x, ω) = L_e(r(x,ω), -ω), i.e., incoming light comes only from emitters, ignoring inter-surface reflections.
Diffuse (Lambertian) BRDF is constant f_r, giving L_r(x) = f_r · E_i(x), independent of viewing direction.
Solid angle Ω = A/r²; differential solid angle dω = dA/r² = sin θ dθ dφ; full sphere integrates to 4π steradians.

terms.radiance: L(x,ω): power per unit solid angle per unit projected area [W/m²sr]; the fundamental quantity in rendering
terms.BRDF: Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function f_r(x, ω_i, ω_r): ratio of differential reflected radiance to incident irradiance
terms.irradiance: E(x): incident power per unit surface area [W/m²]; integral of radiance weighted by cosine over hemisphere
terms.flux: Φ: total radiant power [W = J/s]; energy per unit time
terms.radiant intensity: I(ω) = dΦ/dω [W/sr]: power per unit solid angle from a point source
terms.solid angle: Ω = A/r²: ratio of projected area on sphere to squared radius [steradians]
terms.direct illumination: Lighting model where light reaches surfaces only directly from emitters, ignoring inter-reflections
terms.global illumination: Lighting model accounting for all light bounces; includes indirect illumination
terms.emitted radiance: L_e(x, ω_o): light energy generated at a surface point, non-zero only for light sources
terms.diffuse reflection: Constant BRDF resulting in view-independent reflected radiance

exam_tips: Be able to write and explain every term of the rendering equation, including the hemisphere domain of integration H².
Know the hierarchy of radiometric quantities: energy → flux → intensity → irradiance → radiance, with their units.
Understand why radiance is constant along a ray (no participating media), making it ideal for ray tracing.
For diffuse BRDF, be able to show that L_r is independent of ω_r and equals f_r · E_i(x).

key_facts: Path tracing traces rays from sensor through scene, reflecting at surfaces until reaching light sources; implements global illumination.
Light tracing traces rays from light sources; bidirectional path tracing combines both directions.
Direct illumination uses 2-segment light paths (sensor → surface → light); indirect illumination uses >2 segments.
The reflection equation integral ∫_{H²} f_r L_i cos θ dω must be numerically approximated; Monte Carlo integration is the standard approach.
Importance sampling selects ray directions proportional to the integrand to reduce variance in Monte Carlo estimates.

terms.path tracing: Global illumination algorithm tracing rays from sensor, bouncing through scene to light sources
terms.light tracing: Global illumination algorithm tracing rays from light sources toward sensors
terms.bidirectional path tracing: Algorithm constructing light paths by tracing from both sensor and light source simultaneously
terms.Monte Carlo integration: Numerical integration by averaging function evaluations at random sample points
terms.importance sampling: Sampling strategy where sample distribution is proportional to the integrand to reduce variance
terms.indirect illumination: Light reaching a surface after one or more intermediate surface reflections
terms.light path: Sequence of surface interactions connecting a light source to a sensor

exam_tips: Know the distinction between direct (2-segment) and indirect (>2-segment) light paths.
Understand that importance sampling reduces variance by sampling directions proportional to f_r cos θ or L_i.
Be aware that Monte Carlo ray tracing is unbiased but noisy; variance decreases as O(1/√N) with N samples.

key_facts: Inverse rendering aims to recover scene parameters (geometry, materials, lighting) from images, inverting the forward rendering process.
Differentiable rendering makes the rendering process differentiable with respect to scene parameters, enabling gradient-based optimization.
Inverse problems in graphics are typically ill-posed: multiple scene configurations can produce the same image.
The rendering equation L_o = L_e + ∫ f_r L_i cos θ dω must be differentiated with respect to unknowns for gradient computation.

terms.inverse rendering: Process of estimating scene properties (geometry, reflectance, illumination) from observed images
terms.differentiable rendering: Rendering pipeline where gradients of image pixels with respect to scene parameters can be computed
terms.ill-posed problem: Inverse problem lacking a unique or stable solution; typical of inverse rendering due to ambiguities
terms.forward rendering: Standard process of computing images from a known scene description

exam_tips: Understand the fundamental challenge: the forward rendering integral must be made differentiable, which is non-trivial at surface discontinuities.
Know that inverse rendering is ill-posed and requires regularization or priors to obtain meaningful solutions.
Be able to contrast forward (scene → image) and inverse (image → scene) rendering and their computational challenges.

key_facts: Threads share heap and code but each have their own stack and register set (including PC and SP).
Kernel-level (1:1) threading allows true parallelism on multiple CPUs and handles blocking syscalls; user-level (1:N) threading cannot use multiple CPUs.
Context switches between threads of the same process are cheaper than between processes (no address space switch).
Non-deterministic scheduling means concurrency bugs (race conditions) may be extremely rare and hard to reproduce (Heisenbugs).
Atomicity means a sequence of operations appears indivisible; a read-and-set is needed to implement correct mutual exclusion.
A critical section is a code region that must not be concurrently executed by more than one thread; mutual exclusion ensures this.

terms.thread: An execution context with its own PC, SP, and stack; shares address space with other threads in the same process.
terms.context switch: Saving the state of one thread and restoring another, potentially switching address spaces if between processes.
terms.race condition: A bug where the outcome depends on the nondeterministic interleaving of multiple threads accessing shared data.
terms.mutual exclusion: Guarantee that at most one thread executes within a critical section at any time.
terms.atomicity: A sequence of operations that appears indivisible from the perspective of other threads.
terms.Heisenbug: A concurrency bug that disappears or changes behaviour when observed (e.g., under a debugger).
terms.kernel-level thread: A thread managed directly by the OS kernel, scheduled on physical CPUs; supports true parallelism.
terms.user-level thread: A thread managed by a userspace library; cannot exploit multiple CPUs and may block the whole process on a syscall.

exam_tips: Be clear on what threads share (heap, globals, code) vs. what is private (stack, registers, PC).
Explain why disabling interrupts solves mutual exclusion on a uniprocessor but not on multiprocessors.
Show that a naive 'leave a note' approach fails because checking and setting the flag are not atomic.
Know the trade-offs between 1:1 (kernel), 1:N (user), and M:N (hybrid) threading models.

key_facts: A semaphore has an integer counter and a queue; wait() blocks if counter is 0, signal() wakes a waiting thread or increments the counter.
Initialising a semaphore to 1 gives a binary mutex; initialising to N gives an N-resource allocator.
Initialising a semaphore to 0 provides condition synchronisation: a thread waits until another signals the condition.
In the two-party producer-consumer pattern, no explicit mutex is needed because 'in' and 'out' pointers are each owned by one thread.
Generalised producer-consumer (multiple producers or consumers) requires an additional guard semaphore for mutual exclusion on the buffer indices.
MRSW locks allow multiple concurrent readers but require exclusive access for writers; the simple version can starve writers.

terms.semaphore: A concurrency primitive with an integer counter; supports wait() (decrement/block) and signal() (increment/wake).
terms.condition synchronisation: Using a semaphore (init 0) so one thread blocks until another signals a condition or event.
terms.N-resource allocation: A semaphore initialised to N controls access to N identical resources.
terms.producer-consumer: A concurrency pattern where one thread produces items into a bounded buffer and another consumes them.
terms.backpressure: The mechanism by which a producer blocks when the buffer is full, preventing unbounded queueing.
terms.MRSW lock: Multiple-Readers Single-Writer lock: many threads may read concurrently, but writes require exclusive access.
terms.starvation: A thread is indefinitely prevented from making progress because others always gain access first.
terms.guard semaphore: A binary semaphore used to provide mutual exclusion over buffer index updates in generalised producer-consumer.

exam_tips: Know the exact semaphore pseudo-code for mutual exclusion (init 1), condition sync (init 0), and N-resource allocation (init N).
In the two-party P-C solution, be able to justify why no explicit mutex is needed; this changes for multiple producers/consumers.
Understand how the 'turn' semaphore in the fairer MRSW solution prevents writer starvation.
Remember that forgetting a signal() causes indefinite blocking, while forgetting a wait() corrupts data—both are classic exam mistakes.

key_facts: A monitor groups shared data with its methods; only one thread may execute inside the monitor at a time (implicit mutual exclusion).
Condition variables support wait() (releases monitor lock and blocks), signal() (wakes one waiter), and broadcast() (wakes all waiters).
Signal-and-wait (Hoare) semantics: signaller suspends immediately and signalled thread runs; the condition is guaranteed true on resume.
Signal-and-continue (Mesa) semantics: signaller continues; signalled thread must re-check the condition using while(), not if().
Java's synchronized keyword implements Mesa-style monitors; wait()/notify()/notifyAll() correspond to CV operations.
Pthreads provides mutexes and Mesa-style condition variables; the pattern is: lock, while(!cond) wait(cv, mutex), unlock.

terms.monitor: A structured concurrency primitive combining shared data and methods with automatic mutual exclusion on method entry.
terms.condition variable: A queue of threads waiting for a condition; supports wait(), signal(), and broadcast() within a monitor.
terms.signal-and-wait: Monitor signal semantics where the signaller suspends and the signalled thread runs immediately (Hoare monitors).
terms.signal-and-continue: Monitor signal semantics where the signaller continues; signalled thread must re-test the condition (Mesa monitors).
terms.spurious wakeup: A thread may be woken from wait() without a corresponding signal(); mitigated by re-testing condition in a while loop.
terms.CCR: Conditional Critical Region: a language primitive specifying a region that executes atomically when a guard condition is true.
terms.reentrant lock: A lock that allows the same thread to acquire it multiple times without deadlocking (e.g., Java synchronized).

exam_tips: Always use while() instead of if() with condition variables under Mesa/signal-and-continue semantics—this is a classic exam trap.
Be able to rewrite the producer-consumer monitor solution for both Hoare and Mesa semantics, noting where the bug arises.
Know that pthreads uses Mesa semantics: always protect cond_wait in a while loop with the associated mutex held.
Understand why monitors cannot directly support MRSW: only one thread inside at a time blocks multiple concurrent readers.

key_facts: Deadlock requires all four conditions simultaneously: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait.
In a resource allocation graph, a cycle indicates deadlock (for single-instance resources); for multi-instance resources a cycle indicates potential deadlock only.
Deadlock prevention eliminates one of the four conditions by design (e.g., imposing a partial order on lock acquisition eliminates circular wait).
The Banker's Algorithm detects deadlock by repeatedly finding a thread whose remaining requests can be satisfied with available resources, marking it, and adding its resources to the pool.
Livelock: threads are running but making no useful progress (e.g., repeatedly releasing and re-acquiring locks in synchrony).
Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority thread is blocked by a low-priority thread holding a lock, while a medium-priority thread runs; solved by priority inheritance.

terms.deadlock: A state where a set of threads are each waiting for a resource held by another in the set; none can proceed.
terms.livelock: Threads execute but make no useful progress, typically due to repeated conflicting actions.
terms.resource allocation graph: A directed graph with threads and resources as nodes; edges show held (R→T) and requested (T→R) relationships.
terms.circular wait: A cycle in the wait-for relationship among threads, a necessary condition for deadlock.
terms.Banker's Algorithm: A deadlock detection/avoidance algorithm that checks whether a safe execution order exists by simulating resource allocation.
terms.priority inversion: A scenario where a high-priority thread is effectively blocked by a lower-priority one due to lock holding.
terms.priority inheritance: Temporarily boosting the priority of a lock-holding thread to the maximum priority of any thread waiting for that lock.
terms.ostrich algorithm: Ignoring deadlock entirely; practical in systems where deadlock is rare and recovery (reboot) is acceptable.

exam_tips: Be able to draw a resource allocation graph and identify whether deadlock exists or merely could exist.
Reproduce the Banker's Algorithm step by step: initialise W=V, find rows where R[i]<=W, mark and update W, repeat.
The Dining Philosophers solution (always take lower-numbered fork first) breaks circular wait—know why this works.
Distinguish deadlock (blocked forever), livelock (running but no progress), and starvation (one thread never scheduled).

key_facts: ACID: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (invariants preserved), Isolation (as-if serial), Durability (committed changes survive crashes).
Serializability: a concurrent schedule is serialisable if its outcome is equivalent to some serial execution of the same transactions.
A history graph has a cycle in the transaction-level dependency graph if and only if the schedule is non-serialisable.
Two-phase locking (2PL) guarantees serialisability: an expanding phase acquires locks and a shrinking phase releases them; no new locks after first release.
Strict 2PL holds all locks until commit/abort to prevent cascading aborts; it limits concurrency but avoids dirty reads.
The actor model (e.g., Erlang) and active objects (Ada) avoid shared-memory races by having a single thread own each data object and communicating via messages.

terms.ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability—the four properties of a correct transaction.
terms.serialisability: A correctness criterion: the result of concurrent transactions equals some serial execution of those transactions.
terms.2PL: Two-phase locking: acquire all locks before releasing any; guarantees a serialisable schedule.
terms.strict 2PL: 2PL variant where all locks are held until transaction end; prevents dirty reads and cascading aborts.
terms.cascading abort: When transaction T2 reads uncommitted data from T1 and T1 aborts, T2 must also abort.
terms.history graph: A DAG capturing happens-before relationships among operations; a cycle indicates non-serialisability.
terms.active object: An object with its own thread that serialises access by processing requests from a queue.
terms.actor model: A concurrency model where lightweight processes communicate solely via asynchronous message passing with no shared state.

exam_tips: Given a concurrent schedule, construct the history graph and check for cycles to determine serialisability.
Know the difference between 2PL (non-strict, may allow dirty reads) and strict 2PL (all locks held until end).
Be able to explain why individual operations being atomic is insufficient for composite operations (e.g., debit+credit).
Understand that isolation ≠ serializability: isolation is the goal; serializability is the formal criterion used to define it.

key_facts: Write-ahead logging (WAL): log records must be written to stable storage before the corresponding data pages; log entries contain <txid, obj, old, new>.
A COMMIT log record must be fsynced before reporting success to the client; a crash before COMMIT means the transaction is rolled back.
Checkpoints flush dirty pages to disk and record the set of active transactions; recovery only needs to process entries since the last checkpoint.
Recovery: walk log forward to identify committed (REDO) and incomplete (UNDO) transactions; walk backward to undo incomplete, then forward to redo committed.
Lock-free data structures use CAS/LL-SC directly to update shared state without locks; correctness is defined by linearisability.
Software transactional memory (STM) wraps code in atomic{} blocks; uses OCC internally; composes correctly unlike locks but may livelock under high contention.

terms.write-ahead logging: Protocol requiring log records to be durably written before the data they describe; enables crash recovery.
terms.fsync: OS call that flushes buffered writes to durable storage; required before reporting a transaction as committed.
terms.checkpoint: A log record indicating all dirty pages have been flushed and listing active transactions; bounds recovery work.
terms.REDO: Re-applying operations of a committed transaction whose writes may not have reached disk before a crash.
terms.UNDO: Reversing the operations of an uncommitted transaction to restore the pre-transaction state.
terms.lock-free: A data structure or algorithm that guarantees system-wide progress without using locks, using CAS or LL/SC.
terms.linearisability (lock-free): Correctness criterion for lock-free structures: every operation appears to take effect instantaneously at some point between its invocation and return.
terms.transactional memory: A programming model using atomic{} blocks; the runtime detects conflicts and retries using OCC.

exam_tips: Know the WAL invariant precisely: log record must reach stable storage BEFORE the corresponding data page.
Be able to classify transactions in the checkpoint diagram as requiring REDO, UNDO, or no action.
Lock-free CAS-based insert must handle the case where another thread wins the CAS; the algorithm must retry correctly.
STM atomic{} blocks compose (unlike locks), cannot deadlock, but can livelock under high contention—know this trade-off.

key_facts: In a distributed system, nodes communicate only by message passing; there is no shared memory.
Key challenges vs. local calls: messages can be lost, delayed, or duplicated; the callee may crash mid-execution; retrying may cause duplicate execution.
Marshalling encodes function arguments into a message for transmission; unmarshalling decodes them at the receiver.
An RPC stub has the same signature as the real function but sends a network message instead of executing locally.
Location transparency (hiding whether a resource is local or remote) is not fully achievable because failure modes differ fundamentally.
IDLs (e.g., Protocol Buffers) allow language-independent type signatures; code generators produce stubs for multiple languages.

terms.RPC: Remote Procedure Call: a mechanism making a call to a function on another node appear like a local call.
terms.marshalling: Encoding function arguments (and return values) into a wire format for network transmission.
terms.stub: Client-side proxy with the same signature as the remote function; handles marshalling and network communication.
terms.IDL: Interface Definition Language: a language-neutral specification of RPC interfaces; used to auto-generate stubs.
