terms.at-most-once semantics: RPC sends the request once and does not retry; the operation may not happen if the message is lost.
terms.at-least-once semantics: RPC retries until acknowledged; the operation may execute multiple times.
terms.exactly-once semantics: Achieved by retry + idempotence or deduplication; the operation has the same effect as if it ran exactly once.
terms.location transparency: The goal of making remote resources appear local; not fully achievable due to different failure modes.

exam_tips: Know all three retry semantics (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once) and what is required to achieve each.
Explain why location transparency is not fully achievable: network failures, latency, and partial failure have no local analogue.
Describe how a server deduplicates requests in an at-least-once system (e.g., idempotency keys or a seen-set in stable storage).
Be able to sketch the RPC call flow: client calls stub → marshal → network → unmarshal → execute → marshal result → return.

key_facts: A system model specifies network behaviour (reliable/fair-loss/arbitrary), node behaviour (crash-stop/crash-recovery/Byzantine), and timing assumptions (synchronous/partially synchronous/asynchronous).
Crash-stop: a node fails by stopping permanently. Crash-recovery: a node may stop and restart, losing volatile state but retaining disk. Byzantine: a node may behave arbitrarily.
A fair-loss link can be converted to a reliable link by retry + deduplication; an arbitrary link can be converted to fair-loss using TLS (except blocking).
The FLP result shows no deterministic consensus algorithm is guaranteed to terminate in an asynchronous crash-stop model.
An eventually perfect failure detector may temporarily misclassify nodes but eventually correctly labels crashed nodes; achievable in partially synchronous systems.
Byzantine fault tolerance requires 3f+1 total nodes to tolerate f Byzantine nodes (strictly fewer than 1/3 may be malicious).

terms.crash-stop: Node failure model where a crashed node never recovers; simplest fault model.
terms.crash-recovery: Node failure model allowing restart after a crash; volatile memory is lost but disk state persists.
terms.Byzantine fault: Most general fault model: a faulty node may send arbitrary, conflicting, or malicious messages.
terms.synchronous model: All messages arrive within a known bound and nodes execute at known speeds; enables perfect timeouts.
terms.asynchronous model: No timing bounds on messages or execution; makes consensus impossible deterministically (FLP).
terms.partially synchronous: System is usually synchronous but occasionally asynchronous for unknown finite periods; practical model for real systems.
terms.failure detector: An algorithm that detects whether another node has crashed; may be perfect (exact) or eventually perfect.
terms.network partition: Some links drop or delay all messages for an extended period, partitioning nodes into disconnected groups.

exam_tips: For any algorithm question, first state the system model assumptions (network, nodes, timing).
Know that a perfect failure detector exists only in synchronous crash-stop systems with reliable links.
The FLP result applies to the asynchronous model only; adding clocks (partial synchrony) allows consensus.
Byzantine tolerance (3f+1) is a necessary lower bound—be able to justify why 3 nodes cannot tolerate 1 Byzantine node.

key_facts: Physical clocks drift (quartz ~50 ppm); NTP synchronises to within a few milliseconds using stratum hierarchy and round-trip estimation.
Time-of-day clocks may jump (NTP stepping); monotonic clocks never go backwards but are meaningless across nodes.
The happens-before relation (→) is a strict partial order: a→b if a precedes b on the same node, or a sent a message received at b, or by transitivity.
Lamport timestamps satisfy: if a→b then L(a)<L(b); but L(a)<L(b) does NOT imply a→b (cannot detect concurrency).
Vector clocks satisfy: a→b iff V(a)<V(b); a‖b iff V(a)‖V(b)—they capture the happens-before relation exactly.
Lamport clocks can be extended to a total causal order by breaking ties with node IDs: (L(e), N(e)) uniquely identifies each event.

terms.clock skew: The difference between two clocks' readings at the same instant in real time.
terms.clock drift: The rate at which a clock runs fast or slow relative to a reference; measured in ppm.
terms.NTP: Network Time Protocol: uses a stratum hierarchy and round-trip timing to synchronise clocks to within milliseconds.
terms.monotonic clock: A clock that only advances; not affected by NTP stepping; suitable for measuring elapsed time on one node.
terms.happens-before: A partial order on events: a→b if information could have flowed from a to b via messages or local order.
terms.Lamport clock: Logical clock where each node increments a counter on every event and syncs on message receipt; ensures a→b ⟹ L(a)<L(b).
terms.vector clock: Logical clock using a vector of counters (one per node); captures happens-before exactly: a→b ⟺ V(a)<V(b).
terms.concurrent events: Events a and b where neither a→b nor b→a; written a‖b; vector clocks detect this as incomparable vectors.
terms.leap second: An extra second inserted into UTC to keep it aligned with Earth's rotation; can cause software bugs.

exam_tips: Lamport clocks: memorise both directions: a→b ⟹ L(a)<L(b) is true, but the converse is false—this is the key exam point.
For vector clocks, be able to compute timestamps step by step and determine whether two events are concurrent or ordered.
Explain why physical timestamps cannot be used for causal ordering: NTP skew means concurrent sends can have inverted timestamps.
Know that monotonic clocks are correct for measuring elapsed time on one node but cannot be compared across nodes.

key_facts: A quorum system requires r+w>n (read quorum r, write quorum w, n replicas) to ensure read and write quorums always intersect.
With majority quorums (r=w=⌈(n+1)/2⌉), a system of n replicas tolerates ⌊(n-1)/2⌋ failures.
Read-after-write consistency (read-your-writes) requires the read quorum to intersect the write quorum that acknowledged the write.
Idempotent operations can be safely retried; incrementing a counter is not idempotent but adding to a set is.
State machine replication uses FIFO-total order broadcast: every replica delivers updates in the same order and applies deterministic logic, so all replicas stay identical.
Tombstones (soft deletes with timestamps) allow replicas to distinguish 'deleted' from 'not yet created' during anti-entropy reconciliation.

terms.replica: A node holding a copy of the same data; replication provides fault tolerance and load distribution.
terms.quorum: A subset of replicas that must respond to an operation; read and write quorums must intersect.
terms.read repair: A client, on receiving responses from a read quorum, propagates the most recent value to lagging replicas.
terms.anti-entropy: A background process where replicas periodically exchange state to detect and reconcile inconsistencies.
terms.tombstone: A deletion marker stored with a timestamp; prevents a deleted record from being 'resurrected' during reconciliation.
terms.idempotence: Property of an operation f such that f(f(x))=f(x); idempotent operations can be safely retried.
terms.state machine replication: Replication strategy using total order broadcast; all replicas apply the same deterministic operations in the same order.
terms.last-writer-wins: Conflict resolution policy that keeps the value with the largest timestamp, discarding concurrent writes.

exam_tips: Be able to compute the minimum quorum sizes for n=3,5 and verify r+w>n holds.
Explain why basic quorum reads/writes are not linearisable (Slide 120 scenario) and what additional step ABD requires.
Know when idempotence is insufficient for safety (e.g., add then remove: the retry of add overwrites the remove).
State machine replication requires deterministic logic AND FIFO-total order broadcast—know both requirements.

key_facts: The FLP impossibility result: no deterministic consensus algorithm is guaranteed to terminate in an asynchronous crash-stop system.
Consensus and total order broadcast are formally equivalent: each can be implemented using the other.
Paxos and Raft assume a partially synchronous, crash-recovery model; clocks are used only for timeouts (liveness), not for safety.
Raft uses a term number to detect stale leaders; a node can only vote once per term, and a quorum is required to elect a leader.
To commit a log entry, the Raft leader must receive acknowledgements from a quorum; this ensures any subsequent leader will have the entry.
Safety properties of total order broadcast must hold regardless of timing; only liveness properties depend on clocks and timeouts.

terms.consensus: A distributed agreement problem: nodes propose values and must all decide the same value from the proposed set.
terms.FLP impossibility: No deterministic, asynchronous algorithm can guarantee consensus termination even with one crash fault.
terms.Raft: A consensus algorithm providing FIFO-total order broadcast; uses leader election with terms and log replication.
terms.term: A monotonically increasing integer in Raft; incremented on each election; used to detect stale leaders.
terms.log entry: A record in the Raft replicated log containing a message and the term in which it was proposed.
terms.commit: A log entry is committed once it has been acknowledged by a quorum; it will not be lost despite future leader changes.
terms.split-brain: Erroneously having two leaders simultaneously; prevented in Raft by requiring a quorum to elect or act.
terms.leader election: The process by which one node is chosen as the authoritative sequencer; requires a quorum vote in Raft.

exam_tips: FLP applies to asynchronous+crash-stop only; partial synchrony (clocks for timeouts) allows consensus—know the boundary.
Explain why safety in Raft does not depend on timing: quorum acknowledgements guarantee correct log ordering even with slow messages.
Know that a Raft candidate can only win an election if its log is at least as up-to-date as a quorum (logOk check prevents data loss).
Distinguish atomic commitment (all must commit, any abort causes abort) from consensus (any proposed value may be decided).

key_facts: Two-phase commit (2PC) ensures atomic commitment across multiple nodes; the coordinator is a single point of failure and blocked transactions must wait for it to recover.
Linearizability: every operation appears to take effect atomically at some instant between its invocation and return; it behaves as a single copy.
The ABD algorithm makes quorum get/set linearizable by having each get perform a read-repair write-back to propagate the latest value to a quorum before returning.
Strong eventual consistency (SEC): if two replicas have processed the same set of updates (in any order) they are in the same state; weaker than linearizability.
The CAP theorem: in the presence of a network partition, a system must choose between linearisable consistency and availability.
Linearizability requires a quorum round-trip for every operation; eventual consistency allows operations to proceed locally without any network communication.

terms.two-phase commit: Protocol for atomic commitment: phase 1 collects votes (prepare/ok), phase 2 broadcasts commit or abort.
terms.linearizability: Strongest consistency model: all operations appear atomic and instantaneous; equivalent to a single copy of the data.
terms.serializability: Transactions execute as if in some serial order; does not constrain which order relative to real time.
terms.strict serializability: Combination of linearizability and serializability; the serial order must be consistent with real-time order.
terms.ABD algorithm: Quorum-based protocol for linearizable reads and writes; get includes a write-back phase to propagate the latest value.
terms.eventual consistency: Weak model: if no new updates are made, all replicas eventually converge; no guarantee on when or order.
terms.strong eventual consistency: Ensures convergence whenever two replicas have seen the same set of updates, regardless of order.
terms.CAP theorem: A partitioned system cannot simultaneously provide linearizable consistency and availability; must choose one.
terms.CRDT: Conflict-free Replicated Data Type: a data structure whose merge operation is commutative, associative, and idempotent.

exam_tips: Know 2PC's weakness: the coordinator is a SPOF; an in-doubt transaction cannot proceed without coordinator recovery.
Linearizability is defined in terms of real time (not happens-before); two operations that don't overlap must appear in real-time order.
Show the Slide-120 scenario (non-linearizable quorum reads) and explain why read repair (ABD) fixes it.
CAP: the choice between consistency and availability is forced only during a partition—when no partition exists, both are achievable.

key_facts: Moore's Law predicts transistor density doubles every 18-24 months; process nodes have progressed from 10000nm (1971) to 3nm (~2023).
Dennard scaling broke down around 2006: supply voltage cannot be further reduced because transistors leak too much, causing static power to dominate.
Active power = C·V²·f·A; reducing voltage (V) has a quadratic effect on dynamic power.
Wire delay scales as T ∝ L²/H, so for the same chip area wires do not get faster with technology scaling.
The 'von Neumann bottleneck' refers to the single bus between processor and memory limiting throughput.
Dark silicon: due to the power wall, not all transistors can be active simultaneously, leading to specialised accelerators and heterogeneous cores.

terms.Moore's Law: Observation that transistor density on silicon roughly doubles every 18-24 months.
terms.Dennard scaling: Principle that as transistors shrink, voltage scales proportionally, keeping power density constant — broke down ~2006.
terms.Dynamic power: Power consumed by switching transistors: P = C·V²·f·A.
terms.Static (leakage) power: Power consumed by transistors even when not switching, due to sub-threshold leakage.
terms.DVFS: Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling — adjusts voltage and frequency at runtime to trade performance for power.
terms.Dark silicon: Chip area that must be powered down to stay within thermal/power limits despite available transistors.
terms.Race-to-dark (RTD): Strategy of computing as fast as possible to reach an idle (powered-down) state sooner.
terms.Von Neumann bottleneck: Performance limitation caused by the single bus connecting processor and memory.

exam_tips: Be able to calculate active power using P = C·V²·f·A and explain why reducing V is so effective.
Distinguish between Moore's Law (density) and Dennard scaling (power) — they are separate observations with different failure modes.
Know why wires do not scale beneficially: wire delay ∝ L²/H, so same-area wires are no faster.
Understand why dark silicon arises: transistor count follows Moore's Law but power budget does not scale, forcing selective powering.

key_facts: SystemVerilog always_ff @(posedge clk) describes synchronous (clocked) sequential logic; synthesis tools map this to D flip-flops.
A 5-stage design pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) can be described behaviourally in SystemVerilog and synthesised to an FPGA or ASIC.

FPGAs consist of LUTs (look-up tables), DFFs, block RAMs (BRAMs), DSP blocks, and configurable interconnect; they are ~15× slower per gate than ASICs but far more energy-efficient than general-purpose processors for fixed functions.
Metastability occurs when an asynchronous input violates setup/hold times; MTBF = e^(t/τ) / (f_d · f_c · T₀), and a two-flop synchroniser is the standard mitigation.
Timing analysis determines the critical path (longest combinatorial path) to verify that the design meets the clock period constraint.
Retiming is a synthesis optimisation that moves flip-flops across combinational logic to balance pipeline stage delays without changing functional behaviour.

terms.LUT: Look-Up Table — FPGA primitive implementing arbitrary combinational logic for n inputs.
terms.ALM: Adaptive Logic Module — Cyclone V FPGA building block combining LUTs, DFFs, carry logic, and local routing.
terms.Metastability: Indeterminate output state of a flip-flop when setup/hold times are violated by an asynchronous input.
terms.Critical path: The longest combinatorial delay path in a design, which determines maximum clock frequency.
terms.Place and route (P&R): EDA step that assigns logic to physical locations and routes wires, subject to timing and area constraints.
terms.Retiming: Synthesis optimisation that repositions flip-flops to equalise stage delays and improve clock frequency.
terms.BRAM: Block RAM — dedicated SRAM memory block on an FPGA, efficiently used for caches or memories.
terms.DSP block: Hard arithmetic block on an FPGA optimised for multiply-accumulate operations.

exam_tips: Know the difference between simulation (behavioural, fast, no P&R needed) and gate-level simulation (post-synthesis, more accurate).
Be able to explain why metastability cannot be eliminated, only reduced, and how MTBF depends on synchroniser design.
Understand that FPGA tools stop optimising once timing constraints are met — tighter constraints drive better optimisation.
Distinguish always_ff (sequential, synthesises to DFFs) from always_comb (combinational) in SystemVerilog.

key_facts: RISC-V has six instruction formats: R-type (register), I-type (immediate), S-type (store), B-type (branch), U-type (upper immediate), J-type (jump); register fields rs1, rs2, rd are always in the same bit positions.
RISC-V has 32 integer registers (x0–x31); x0 is hardwired to zero; all 32-bit instructions are fixed-width with optional 16-bit compressed (C) extension.
CISC (e.g., x86-64) uses variable-length instructions (1–15 bytes), implicit register operands, and memory-to-memory operations; modern x86 internally decodes to RISC-like micro-ops.
EDSAC (1949) was an accumulator-based machine requiring memory access for every operation; accumulator architectures have high memory bandwidth requirements.
Java bytecode is a stack-based ISA: operands are pushed/popped from an operand stack, limiting instruction-level parallelism.
The RISC philosophy: make the common case fast, fixed-length instructions, load-store architecture (only load/store access memory), large uniform register file, and simple decode.

terms.ISA: Instruction Set Architecture — the programmer-visible interface defining instructions, registers, and memory model.
terms.R-type instruction: RISC-V format with three register operands (rd, rs1, rs2) and funct3/funct7 fields specifying the operation.
terms.I-type instruction: RISC-V format with one register source, one 12-bit sign-extended immediate, and a destination register.
terms.B-type instruction: RISC-V conditional branch format; immediate encodes a signed byte offset (bit 0 = 0) relative to PC.
terms.Load-store architecture: ISA style where memory is accessed only via explicit load/store instructions; all other operations use registers.
terms.CISC: Complex Instruction Set Computer — ISA with variable-length, complex instructions (e.g., x86).
terms.Calling convention: ABI agreement on which registers are caller-saved vs callee-saved and how arguments/return values are passed.
terms.Pseudo-instruction: Assembler shorthand that expands to one or more real instructions (e.g., MV rd,rs → ADDI rd,rs,0).

exam_tips: Be able to encode/decode any RISC-V instruction format — examiners frequently ask to extract fields or verify instruction encodings.
Know the distinction between caller-saved (t0-t6, a0-a7) and callee-saved (s0-s11, sp) registers in the RISC-V ABI.
Understand why stack-based and accumulator ISAs have worse ILP than register ISAs: implicit dependencies on the stack top or accumulator.
For CISC vs RISC comparison: focus on instruction length variability, decode complexity, and how modern CISC pipelines use micro-op translation.

key_facts: A 5-stage RISC-V pipeline: IF (Instruction Fetch), ID (Decode/Register Fetch), EX (Execute/ALU), MEM (Memory Access), WB (Write-Back); clock period is set by the slowest stage.

Three hazard types: structural (resource conflict, e.g., single memory for IF and MEM), data (instruction depends on result not yet written back), and control (branch outcome not yet known during fetch).

Forwarding (bypassing) eliminates most data hazards by routing EX or MEM stage results directly to EX stage inputs without waiting for WB; load-use hazards require exactly one stall cycle even with forwarding.
Branch hazard: with branch resolved in EX stage, two instructions are fetched in error; with dynamic branch prediction, the penalty only occurs on misprediction.
Pipeline speedup ≈ number of stages when stages are balanced; CPI approaches 1 in the ideal case but is degraded by hazard stalls.
Precise exceptions require the pipeline to complete all instructions before the faulting instruction and flush all younger instructions, preserving the sequential execution model.

terms.Structural hazard: Hazard caused by two instructions requiring the same hardware resource simultaneously.
terms.Data hazard: Hazard where an instruction needs a result not yet produced by an earlier in-flight instruction.
terms.Control hazard: Hazard caused by uncertainty over which instruction to fetch next after a branch.
terms.Forwarding (bypassing): Routing computed results directly from a later pipeline stage back to the EX stage to avoid data hazard stalls.
terms.Load-use hazard: Specific data hazard when a load is immediately followed by an instruction using the loaded value; requires one stall.
terms.Pipeline bubble: A NOP injected into the pipeline to resolve a hazard by delaying dependent instructions.
terms.Branch prediction: Technique of guessing branch outcome to fetch speculatively; misprediction requires pipeline flush.
terms.Precise exception: Exception handling model where processor state reflects exactly the instructions committed before the faulting instruction.

exam_tips: Draw pipeline diagrams showing instruction timing; identify hazards and show exactly which cycles stall or use forwarded values.
Remember that forwarding cannot eliminate a load-use hazard — one stall cycle is always required (data is only available after MEM stage).
Know code scheduling as a compiler technique to avoid load-use stalls by reordering independent instructions.
For branch hazards, know the penalty in cycles for both stall-on-branch and predict-and-flush strategies.

key_facts: Direct-mapped cache: index bits select a cache line, tag bits verify the address, offset bits select a byte within the line; conflict misses are highest.
Set-associative cache with N ways has N direct-mapped caches in parallel; reduces conflict misses at the cost of N comparators; most modern L1 caches are 8–16 way set-associative.
Three sources of cache misses: Compulsory (cold start, first access), Capacity (cache too small), Conflict (non-fully-associative mapping collision).
Write-back caches use a dirty bit; on eviction a dirty line is written to the next level. Write-through caches update both cache and memory on every write, typically buffered with a write buffer.
DRAM access sequence: Row Access (tRCD), Column Read (tCAS), Burst Transfer (tBURST), Precharge (tRP); open-page policy avoids precharge when accessing the same row repeatedly.

TLB (Translation Look-aside Buffer) is a fully-associative cache of Page Table Entries (PTEs); a TLB miss triggers a hardware page table walk in modern processors; a page fault requires OS intervention.

terms.Cache line: Fixed-size block of contiguous memory addresses transferred between cache levels as a unit.
terms.Direct-mapped cache: Cache where each memory address maps to exactly one cache set (one way).
terms.Set-associative cache: Cache organised as sets of N ways; an address maps to one set and can occupy any of the N ways.
terms.Write-back cache: Cache policy where writes update only the cache; dirty lines are written to lower memory on eviction.
terms.Write-through cache: Cache policy where every write updates both cache and next memory level simultaneously.
terms.TLB: Translation Look-aside Buffer — a small fast cache of virtual-to-physical address translations (PTEs).
terms.Page fault: Exception raised when a virtual page has no valid mapping in physical memory, requiring OS to load the page.
terms.Miss penalty: Extra cycles required to service a cache miss by fetching data from a lower level of the hierarchy.
terms.DRAM row buffer: Sense amplifier array that holds the currently open DRAM row, enabling fast column accesses without re-activating the row.

exam_tips: Be able to split an address into tag/index/offset bits given cache size, associativity, and line size — this is a very common exam calculation.
Know all three sources of cache misses (compulsory, capacity, conflict) and which design parameter reduces each.
Understand the DRAM timing parameters (tRCD, tCAS, tBURST, tRP) and be able to explain the open-page vs close-page trade-off.
For virtual memory, trace through a two-level page table walk and explain when TLB hits, TLB misses, and page faults occur.

key_facts: RISC-V has three privilege levels: User (U), Supervisor (S), Machine (M); M-mode has full hardware access, S-mode runs the OS, U-mode runs applications.

ECALL instruction causes a synchronous trap from a lower privilege mode to the next higher level; the EPC register saves the PC of the faulting/calling instruction; STVEC holds the trap handler address.

RISC-V Sv32 uses a two-level page table: virtual address split into VPN[1] (10 bits), VPN[0] (10 bits), page offset (12 bits); first-level lookup uses satp.ppn, second-level uses PPN from the first PTE.
Page table entries include permission bits (R/W/X), validity (V), dirty (D), accessed (A), and user (U) bits; R=X=W=0 means the PTE is a pointer to the next-level table (superpage if R or X = 1).
Precise/restartable exceptions allow the OS to handle the fault and re-execute the faulting instruction; imprecise exceptions cannot be restarted.
TLB coherence on context switches is managed by TLB shootdowns (inter-processor interrupts) or by tagging TLB entries with an ASID (Address Space IDentifier) stored in the satp register.

terms.ECALL: RISC-V instruction that causes a synchronous trap into a higher privilege mode (software interrupt).
terms.SRET/MRET: Trap-return instructions that restore the previous privilege level and PC from EPC.
terms.SEPC: Supervisor Exception Program Counter — holds the PC of the instruction that caused the trap.
terms.STVEC: Supervisor Trap VECtor — register holding the address of the trap handler.
terms.satp: Supervisor Address Translation and Protection register — holds the physical page number of the root page table and ASID.
terms.PTE: Page Table Entry — stores physical page number and permission/status bits for a virtual page.
terms.ASID: Address Space IDentifier — tag in satp and TLB entries to distinguish translations from different address spaces.
terms.Page fault: Exception caused by accessing a virtual address with no valid PTE (not present or permission violation).
terms.TLB shootdown: IPI-based mechanism to flush stale TLB entries across all cores when page tables are modified.

exam_tips: Trace through a complete Sv32 two-level page table walk, computing the physical addresses of both PTE lookups.
Know the difference between a TLB miss (hardware page table walk) and a page fault (OS intervention required).
Understand what each privilege mode can and cannot access — examiners test ECALL flow: save EPC, jump to STVEC, SRET to return.
Be able to explain why precise exceptions are needed for correct OS behaviour and why they are harder to implement in deep pipelines.

key_facts: Amdahl's Law: Speedup(n) = 1 / (B + (1-B)/n); the sequential fraction B fundamentally limits parallel speedup regardless of core count.

MSI cache coherence protocol states: Modified (single dirty copy, memory stale), Shared (clean copies in multiple caches), Invalid (no valid copy); transitions are triggered by local processor reads/writes and snooped bus transactions.
In a snoopy bus protocol, a BusRdX (bus read exclusive) invalidates all other cached copies and grants exclusive write access; a BusRd causes a cache in M-state to flush and transition to S-state.
Memory consistency models: sequential consistency requires all reads/writes appear in program order to all cores; relaxed consistency models allow reordering of accesses to different addresses.
Load-Reserved / Store-Conditional (LR/SC) implement atomic operations on RISC architectures: SC fails (returns 0) if any other write to the reserved address occurred between LR and SC.
Memory barriers (fences) prevent loads/stores from being reordered across the barrier by the processor pipeline or memory subsystem.

terms.Amdahl's Law: Formula showing that speedup with N cores is limited by the sequential fraction B: Speedup = 1/(B+(1-B)/N).
terms.Gustafson's Law: Alternative to Amdahl's: assumes problem scales with compute; Speedup(n) = n + (1-n)·B.
terms.Cache coherence: Property ensuring all processors see a consistent view of shared memory despite private caches.
terms.MSI protocol: Simple 3-state coherence protocol: Modified, Shared, Invalid.
terms.Snoopy bus: Coherence mechanism where all caches monitor (snoop) all bus transactions to maintain consistency.
terms.BusRdX: Bus transaction requesting exclusive ownership of a cache line, causing all other copies to be invalidated.
terms.Sequential consistency: Memory model guaranteeing all operations appear in program order to all processors.
terms.Load-Reserved (LR): RISC-V instruction that loads a value and places a reservation on the address.
terms.Store-Conditional (SC): RISC-V instruction that stores a value only if the reservation from LR has not been broken.
terms.Memory fence (barrier): Instruction that enforces ordering of memory operations across the barrier.

exam_tips: Trace through MSI protocol state diagrams for multi-core scenarios — examiners give sequences of reads/writes and ask for state transitions and bus transactions.
Calculate Amdahl's Law speedup for given parallel fractions and core counts; know that even 99% parallel code is limited to 100× speedup.
Know why spin-locking on a load-reserved (not a raw store) reduces bus traffic — the LD can be served from local cache in S-state.
Distinguish cache coherence (consistency of a single address across caches) from memory consistency (ordering between different addresses).

key_facts: GPUs use SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads): a warp of 32 threads executes the same instruction simultaneously on different data; each thread has private registers and memory.
Branch divergence occurs when threads in a warp take different paths; inactive threads are masked with predicate bits, reducing effective utilisation proportionally.
GPU multithreading hides memory latency: when one warp stalls on a DRAM access, the warp scheduler switches to another ready warp with no context-switch overhead (all register state is resident).

CUDA terminology: kernel (GPU function), thread (single execution), warp (32 threads executed together), thread block (group sharing shared memory and barriers), grid (all thread blocks for a kernel invocation).

GPU memory hierarchy: private local memory (per-thread, in DRAM), shared memory (per SM, on-chip, low latency), global memory (DRAM, accessible by all), constant/texture memory (DRAM, specialised cached).
Google's TPU achieves 15–30× higher performance and 30–80× better performance/Watt than CPUs/GPUs for neural network inference by eliminating out-of-order hardware, caches, and multithreading.

terms.SIMT: Single Instruction, Multiple Threads — GPU execution model where one instruction is issued to many threads simultaneously.
terms.Warp: Group of 32 threads in NVIDIA GPUs that execute the same instruction in lockstep.
terms.Thread block: Group of threads sharing on-chip shared memory and able to synchronise via barriers within one SM.
terms.Streaming Multiprocessor (SM): GPU processing unit containing ALUs, warp schedulers, register file, and shared memory.
terms.Branch divergence: Condition where threads in a warp take different control-flow paths, requiring serialisation via predication.
terms.Predication: Mechanism for conditional execution in SIMT: inactive threads execute instructions but discard results.
terms.Shared memory: On-chip SRAM per SM in a GPU, shared among threads in a block; low latency, high bandwidth.
terms.CUDA kernel: GPU function launched by the host CPU, executed in parallel by many threads on the GPU.
terms.OpenCL: Open heterogeneous compute framework for programming GPUs, CPUs, FPGAs, and other devices.
terms.Spectre: Microarchitectural attack exploiting speculative execution and cache side channels to leak secret data.

exam_tips: Know the CUDA/OpenCL thread hierarchy: thread → warp → thread block → grid, and what memory each level can access.
Explain quantitatively how branch divergence reduces GPU efficiency — if 50% of threads branch, effective utilisation is 50% in diverged regions.
Understand why GPUs do not need large caches: multithreading (many warps in flight) hides DRAM latency instead of reducing it.
For emerging trends, be able to explain Spectre: speculative loads leave cache-state side effects that leak data even after the pipeline is flushed.

key_facts: C's primitive types are char (>=8 bits), int (>=16 bits), float, and double; precise sizes are architecture-dependent.
A variable must be declared before use and defined (storage allocated) exactly once; a definition counts as a declaration.
In C90, variables can only be declared at the start of a block; this restriction was lifted in C99.
The static modifier at file scope prevents a symbol from being exported; at local scope it creates a single persistent instance across calls.
An expression becomes a statement when followed by a semicolon; all operators including assignment return a value.
A function declaration with empty parentheses (e.g. int f();) in C means arguments are not type-checked, not that it takes no arguments—use void for the latter.

terms.declaration: Announces the existence and type of a variable or function without allocating storage.
terms.definition: Allocates storage for a variable or provides a function body; must occur exactly once.
terms.static (global scope): Limits a function or variable's linkage to the current compilation unit.
terms.auto: Default storage class for local variables; in C++11 reused to mean 'infer type from initialiser'.
terms.extern: Declares a variable or function defined in another compilation unit.
terms.enumeration: A set of named integer constants; in C they are just integers, in C++ they form a distinct type.
terms.translation unit: A single source file after preprocessing; the unit of compilation.

exam_tips: Know the difference between declaration and definition; using extern int x; is a declaration only.
Remember that int f() in C means unspecified arguments, not zero arguments—use int f(void) for no arguments.
Be able to trace control flow with comma expressions, e.g. for (i=0, j=n-1; i<j; i++, j--).
Static local variables retain their value between calls and there is only one copy even under recursion.

key_facts: An array name used without an index decays to a pointer to its first element; c and &c[0] are equivalent.
Pointer arithmetic scales by the size of the pointed-to type: if p is int*, p+1 advances by sizeof(int) bytes.
For an array c, *(c+i) == c[i] and c+i == &c[i]; a pointer variable can be incremented but an array name cannot.
A pointer to a function must match the exact type signature; either or both of the * in (*compare) may be omitted due to language generosity.
const int *p is a pointer to a const int (value immutable); int *const p is a const pointer to int (pointer immutable); const int *const p is both.
A bit-field member does not have an address, so the & operator cannot be applied to it.

terms.pointer: A variable holding the memory address of another object.
terms.dereference: Following a pointer to access the value it points to, using the unary * operator.
terms.pointer arithmetic: Adding or subtracting integers from pointers, scaled by the size of the pointed-to type.
terms.void *: A generic pointer type that can point to any object but cannot be legally dereferenced without a cast.
terms.function pointer: A pointer that holds the address of a function and can be used to call it indirectly.
terms.bit-field: A struct member declared with a colon and bit count, giving sub-word granularity access.
terms.union: A type whose members share the same memory; size equals that of the largest member.

exam_tips: const placement matters: 'const int *p' vs 'int *const p' are frequently confused; remember the rule: read right-to-left from the variable name.
When passing an array to a function, it decays to a pointer; the function does not know the array size—bounds must be passed separately.
Function pointers in C are written as int (*fp)(int, int); omitting parentheses changes the meaning entirely.
Pointer arithmetic within a struct (e.g. incrementing past one field to reach another) is undefined behaviour unless within an array.

key_facts: Undefined behaviour (UB) means the C standard places no constraints on what the program may do; the compiler is free to assume UB never occurs, enabling aggressive optimisations.
Signed integer overflow is undefined behaviour in C; unsigned overflow is defined as modular arithmetic.
Accessing memory out of bounds, dereferencing a freed or null pointer, and using an uninitialised variable are all undefined behaviour.
ASan (Address Sanitizer) instruments code at compile time to detect memory errors such as out-of-bounds access, use-after-free, and double-free; typical slowdown is 2x.
MSan (Memory Sanitizer) detects reads of uninitialised memory, which ASan does not catch; it is currently only available in clang.
UBSan (Undefined Behaviour Sanitizer) catches non-memory UB such as signed overflow and null pointer dereference with only ~20% overhead.

terms.undefined behaviour: A program action for which the C standard imposes no requirements; anything may happen.
terms.implementation-defined behaviour: Behaviour that varies between implementations but each implementation must document it.
terms.buffer overflow: Writing past the end of an allocated buffer, potentially corrupting adjacent memory.
terms.use-after-free: Dereferencing a pointer after the memory it points to has been freed.
terms.ASan: AddressSanitizer: a compile-time instrumentation tool detecting memory safety violations at runtime.
terms.UBSan: Undefined Behaviour Sanitizer: detects non-memory undefined behaviour such as integer overflow at runtime.
terms.MSan: MemorySanitizer: detects reads of uninitialised memory; only available in clang.

exam_tips: Distinguish the three sanitizers: ASan catches memory errors, MSan catches uninitialised reads, UBSan catches other UB like integer overflow.
Signed integer overflow is UB; do not assume two's-complement wrap-around—use unsigned arithmetic or compiler builtins for that.
A bug (defect) and the failure (observable crash/wrong output) may be far apart in code and time; debuggers help trace back to the defect.
Valgrind instruments binaries (no recompilation needed) but has very high overhead; sanitizers require recompilation but are faster.

key_facts: malloc returns void* and does not initialise memory; calloc zeroes the allocation; realloc resizes; free deallocates and must not be called twice on the same pointer.
Each allocated pointer must be freed exactly once along every execution path; failure to free causes a memory leak, freeing twice is undefined behaviour.
Recursively freeing a tree that is actually a DAG (with shared nodes) will call free twice on shared nodes; the visited-flag or arena approach prevents this.
An arena pre-allocates a large block; individual objects are allocated by bumping a counter; the entire arena is freed with two calls to free, regardless of how many objects it contains.
Reference counting frees an object only when its count reaches zero; it cannot collect cycles because cyclic structures always have non-zero counts.
A mark-and-sweep garbage collector marks all objects reachable from roots, then sweeps the allocation list freeing unmarked objects; it can collect cycles.

terms.malloc: Allocates a given number of bytes on the heap and returns a void* pointer, without initialisation.
terms.free: Releases heap memory previously allocated by malloc; must be called exactly once per allocation.
terms.memory leak: Allocated heap memory that is no longer reachable and can never be freed.
terms.arena allocator: A pre-allocated memory pool from which objects are carved out; freed all at once.
terms.reference counting: Tracking the number of pointers to an object and freeing it when the count reaches zero.
terms.mark-and-sweep: GC algorithm: mark phase traces reachable objects from roots; sweep phase frees unmarked ones.
terms.DAG: Directed Acyclic Graph; a pointer structure where nodes may be reached by multiple paths, so naive recursive free double-frees shared nodes.

exam_tips: Show that freeing a tree is O(n) but freeing a DAG naively causes double-frees; use a visited bit or arena to avoid this.
Reference counting cannot collect cycles—be ready to give an example of a cycle that leaks under reference counting.
With arenas, the key benefit is that deallocation is O(1) regardless of object count, and individual objects need not be freed separately.
The RAII pattern (destructors called on scope exit) is the C++ analogue of Java try-finally for ensuring resources are released.

key_facts: A cache line is typically 64 bytes; when data accessed together is spread across many cache lines, the miss rate increases and performance degrades.
L1 cache reference takes ~4 cycles; L2 ~10 cycles; main memory ~300 cycles—random memory access patterns are the dominant performance bottleneck in data-intensive code.
An intrusive list embeds the data directly in the list node, removing the extra pointer indirection and improving cache utilisation.
Array-of-structs (AoS) stores all fields of each object together; struct-of-arrays (SoA) stores each field in its own array—SoA improves cache efficiency when only one field is accessed at a time.
Loop blocking (tiling) restructures nested loops so that a small submatrix fits in cache, reducing the number of cache misses for operations like transposed matrix addition.
Linked lists cause cache misses on every tail-pointer traversal; replacing them with arrays eliminates tail pointers and improves sequential access patterns.

terms.cache miss: An access where the requested data is not in cache and must be fetched from a slower memory level.
terms.cache line: The unit of data transferred between cache and memory, typically 64 bytes.
terms.locality: The principle that programs tend to access memory addresses close to recently accessed ones; exploited by caches.
terms.array-of-structs (AoS): Data layout where each record's fields are stored contiguously; poor when only one field is traversed.
terms.struct-of-arrays (SoA): Data layout where each field is stored in a separate array; good for SIMD and field-at-a-time traversal.
terms.loop blocking / tiling: Restructuring a loop to operate on small subsets of data that fit in cache before moving on.
terms.intrusive list: A linked list where the list linkage fields are embedded directly in the data structure.

exam_tips: Be able to explain why SoA is better than AoS when only one field is processed: each cache line holds more relevant data.
For matrix transpose addition, identify which access (A[i][j] or B[j][i]) is non-sequential and explain how blocking fixes it.
Know the rough latency ratios: L1 ~4 cycles, L2 ~10 cycles, RAM ~300 cycles; memory access dominates when data doesn't fit in cache.
Linked lists have poor cache behaviour not only due to tail pointer chasing but also because the pointer wastes space in each cache line.

key_facts: Compile-time errors are caught by the compiler; runtime errors require debugging techniques such as print statements, assertions, and interactive debuggers.
The assert() macro from <assert.h> immediately terminates the program if the given condition is false; it is disabled when NDEBUG is defined.
Side effects must never be placed inside assert() because the assertion may be compiled out with -DNDEBUG.
Compiler flags -g embed debugging symbols (source line to address mappings) in the binary; without them, debuggers show only assembly.
A debugger breakpoint pauses execution at a given function or line; a watchpoint pauses when a specific variable changes value.
Regression testing re-runs a test suite after every change to detect newly introduced failures; unit tests isolate individual functions or modules.

terms.assert: Macro that checks a condition at runtime and aborts with a message if false; disabled by -DNDEBUG.
terms.NDEBUG: Preprocessor macro that disables assert() when defined.
terms.debugging symbols (-g): Compiler-generated metadata mapping machine addresses to source lines and variable names.
terms.breakpoint: An instruction to a debugger to pause program execution at a specific function or line.
terms.watchpoint: A debugger instruction to pause execution whenever a specified variable is read or written.
terms.unit test: A short code fragment that tests a single function or module in isolation.
terms.regression test: A test run after every change to ensure previously passing tests still pass.

exam_tips: Never put side effects inside assert()—if NDEBUG is set the assert is removed and the side effect disappears.
Know that the defect (the bug in the source) and the failure (the observable crash) can be far apart; debugging finds the defect by tracing backwards from the failure.
The debug_printf pattern using the preprocessor (conditional on DEBUG) lets you leave debugging output in the source without affecting production builds.
Understand the difference between unit testing (testing in isolation) and regression testing (checking no existing tests break after changes).

key_facts: A C++ reference provides an alias for a variable; it must be initialised at declaration and cannot be reseated—unlike a pointer which can be reassigned.
C++ struct members default to public access; class members default to private access.
A destructor (~ClassName()) is called automatically when a stack-allocated object goes out of scope or when delete is called on a heap object; there can be only one destructor.
The copy constructor (MyClass(const MyClass&)) is called on initialisation from an existing object (MyClass b = a); assignment (b = a) calls operator= instead.
RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialisation) wraps resources in stack-allocated objects so that destructors automatically release them on scope exit, even when exceptions are thrown.
Non-virtual member functions are resolved at compile time based on the static type of the variable; declare a function virtual to get Java-like runtime dispatch.

terms.reference (&): An alias for an existing variable; must be initialised at declaration and cannot be reseated.
terms.constructor: A member function with the same name as the class, called to initialise an object.
terms.destructor: A member function prefixed with ~ called automatically when an object is destroyed.
terms.copy constructor: A constructor taking a const reference to the same class, invoked when initialising from an existing object.
terms.RAII: Resource Acquisition Is Initialisation: acquire resources in constructors, release in destructors to ensure cleanup.
terms.default constructor: A constructor with no required arguments; generated by the compiler if none is defined.
terms.this: An implicit pointer to the current object inside a non-static member function.

exam_tips: Distinguish copy constructor (called on initialisation: MyClass b = a) from assignment operator (called on b = a when b already exists).
Destructors should be declared virtual whenever a class has subclasses; failing to do so causes the wrong destructor to be called via a base-class pointer.
References cannot be null and cannot be reseated; pointers can be null and can be reassigned—this is the key operational difference.
C++ has no garbage collector; every new must be matched with delete, and new[] with delete[].

key_facts: Operator overloading allows user-defined types to use built-in syntax; almost all operators can be overloaded except :: . .* and ?:.
A virtual function table (vtable) is generated per class when any member function is declared virtual; each object stores a pointer to its class's vtable enabling runtime dispatch.

Multiple inheritance allows a class to have more than one direct base class; if two base classes both inherit from a common base, the derived class gets two copies of the common base unless virtual inheritance is used.
dynamic_cast<T*>(p) performs a checked downcast at runtime using RTTI and returns nullptr if the cast fails; static_cast performs an unchecked compile-time cast.
An abstract class contains at least one pure virtual function (= 0) and cannot be instantiated; a derived class must implement all pure virtual functions to be instantiable.
const_cast removes const or volatile qualifiers; reinterpret_cast reinterprets the bit pattern without conversion; both should be used sparingly.

terms.vtable: A per-class table of function pointers implementing virtual dispatch; each object with virtual functions holds a pointer to its class's vtable.
terms.RTTI: Run-Time Type Information; metadata stored in vtables enabling dynamic_cast and typeid.
terms.virtual function: A member function resolved at runtime based on the dynamic type of the object.
terms.pure virtual function: A virtual function declared '= 0'; forces derived classes to provide an implementation.
terms.abstract class: A class with at least one pure virtual function; cannot be instantiated directly.
terms.multiple inheritance: A class deriving from more than one base class, inheriting members from all bases.
terms.virtual base class: A base class declared with 'virtual' in the inheritance list to ensure only one shared copy exists in diamond inheritance.
terms.dynamic_cast: A cast operator that performs a runtime-checked conversion within an inheritance hierarchy.
terms.static_cast: A cast operator performing compile-time type conversion without runtime checks.

exam_tips: Know that non-virtual methods are resolved by the static type at compile time; calling a non-virtual method through a base-class reference uses the base version even if overridden.
Understand the diamond inheritance problem: classes A->B, A->C, D->B,C gives D two copies of A unless A is declared a virtual base.
dynamic_cast requires RTTI (hence a vtable, hence at least one virtual function in the hierarchy); on failure with pointers it returns nullptr, with references it throws std::bad_cast.
Operator overloading: a member operator= should return *this by reference to support chaining; passing the argument by value would trigger an extra copy constructor call.

key_facts: A compiler translates high-level language (machine-independent, complex syntax, variables, nested scope) to low-level language (machine-specific, simple syntax, registers, flat scope).
The front end consists of lexing, parsing, and type checking; all error-checking happens in the front end, producing an annotated AST.
The middle end performs generic (machine-independent) optimizations on a retargetable intermediate representation; the back end performs machine-specific optimizations.
Compiler-like programs are pervasive: query languages, serialization frameworks, build systems, game scripting, hardware description languages, etc.

The course structure covers: lexing & parsing (lectures 2-6), deriving the translator (lectures 7-11), and assorted topics including linking, calling conventions, memory management, bootstrapping, optimisation, exceptions (lectures 12-16).
A Jargon VM is used as the target for the derived compiler in this course, with bytecode instructions like MK_CLOSURE, APPLY, HALT, LOOKUP, RETURN.

terms.front end: Compiler phases (lexing, parsing, type checking) that process source code and produce an annotated AST; all error checking occurs here.
terms.middle end: Compiler phase performing machine-independent optimizations on an intermediate (retargetable) representation.
terms.back end: Compiler phase performing machine-specific optimizations and generating target code from the intermediate representation.
terms.annotated AST: Abstract syntax tree decorated with type information and other semantic annotations, output of the front end.
terms.retargetable representation: An intermediate form designed to be independent of any specific target machine, enabling the compiler to target multiple architectures.
terms.bootstrapping: The process of compiling a compiler using itself, or using a simpler initial compiler to build a more capable one.

exam_tips: Remember that ALL error-checking (syntax errors, type errors) happens in the front end; the back end assumes correct input.
Be able to distinguish what belongs in the front end vs middle end vs back end and justify why.
The gap between high-level and low-level: know the key differences (machine-independence, scope, type system, executability).
The course derives a compiler from an interpreter via principled refinements—understand this methodology conceptually.

key_facts: Lexing converts a sequence of characters into a sequence of tokens (e.g., 'if a = 3' becomes IF, IDENT "a", EQUAL, INT "3", etc.).
A lexer specification maps regular expressions to tokens; the lexer uses longest match and first-rule-wins for disambiguation.
Regular expressions over alphabet Σ: e → ∅ | ε | a | e∨e | ee | e* ; they denote languages (sets of strings).
The RE→NFA construction (Thompson's construction) builds an NFA with a single final state inductively over regex structure.
The powerset (subset) construction converts an NFA M=⟨Q,Σ,δ,q0,F⟩ to a DFA where states are subsets of Q and δ'(S,a) = ε-closure({q'∈δ(q,a)|q∈S}).
Brzozowski derivatives: ∂_c r is a regex matching s iff r matches cs; ∂_c(rs) = (∂_c r)s | ν(r)(∂_c s) where ν(r)=ε if ε∈L(r), else ∅.

terms.token: A classified lexical unit output by the lexer (e.g., IF, IDENT, INT), the input to the parser.
terms.regular expression: A formal notation for describing sets of strings (languages) using union, concatenation, and Kleene star.
terms.NFA: Nondeterministic Finite Automaton: a finite automaton where δ(q,a)⊆Q, allowing multiple or zero transitions per symbol including ε-transitions.
terms.DFA: Deterministic Finite Automaton: a finite automaton where δ(q,a)∈Q, exactly one transition per state/symbol pair.
terms.\u03b5-closure: The set of NFA states reachable from a given set via zero or more ε-transitions; key operation in powerset construction.
terms.powerset construction: Algorithm converting an NFA to an equivalent DFA by making DFA states correspond to subsets of NFA states.
terms.longest match: Lexer rule: always match the longest possible prefix of the input that is accepted by any rule.
terms.Brzozowski derivative: ∂_c r: the regex that matches string s iff r matches cs; enables direct DFA construction from regexes.
terms.tagged DFA: A DFA for lexing where each accepting state is annotated with the highest-priority matching lexer rule.

exam_tips: Know the Thompson construction cases for each regex operator (∅, ε, a, e1∨e2, e1e2, e*) and be able to draw the resulting NFAs.
Be able to perform the powerset construction step-by-step, computing ε-closures correctly.
Understand why 'longest match' and 'first rule wins' are both needed (e.g., 'ifif' is an identifier, 'if' is a keyword).
For Brzozowski derivatives, know the formula for each case especially ∂_c(rs) involving ν(r), and be able to construct the DFA from derivatives.

key_facts: A CFG is G=⟨N,T,P,S⟩ with nonterminals N, terminals T, productions P⊆N×(N∪T)*, start symbol S; L(G)={w∈T*|S⇒+w}.
Context-free languages are accepted by pushdown automata (PDAs), which are finite automata augmented with a stack; every CFG has an equivalent PDA and vice versa.
Ambiguity: a grammar is ambiguous if some string has two distinct derivation trees; some languages are inherently ambiguous (no unambiguous CFG exists); checking ambiguity is undecidable.
LL(k) parsing: Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, k-symbol lookahead; for LL(1), precompute FIRST, FOLLOW, and NULLABLE sets to build a parsing table M[A,a].
Bottom-up (LR) parsing is strictly more powerful than LL parsing and does not require left-recursion elimination; it attempts a rightmost derivation in reverse.
Left recursion (e.g., E→E+T) causes infinite loops in recursive descent; it can be eliminated by introducing new nonterminals (e.g., E→TE', E'→+TE'|ε).

terms.context-free grammar (CFG): A grammar G=⟨N,T,P,S⟩ whose productions have a single nonterminal on the left-hand side.
terms.derivation: A sequence of sentential forms obtained by repeatedly applying productions; leftmost derivation always expands the leftmost nonterminal.
terms.parse tree: A tree (also called derivation tree or concrete syntax tree) representing the syntactic structure of a string according to a grammar.
terms.abstract syntax tree (AST): A simplified tree retaining only semantically relevant structure, omitting grammar-specific details like parentheses.
terms.pushdown automaton (PDA): A finite automaton augmented with a stack; M=⟨Q,Σ,Γ,δ,q0,Z⟩ where δ(q,a,X)⊆Q×Γ*.
terms.FIRST(\u03b1): The set of terminals that can begin strings derived from α, plus ε if α⇒*ε.
terms.FOLLOW(A): The set of terminals that can immediately follow nonterminal A in any sentential form derived from the start symbol.
terms.NULLABLE(\u03b1): True iff α⇒*ε; computed inductively over grammar structure.
terms.LL(1) parsing table: Table M[A,a] mapping nonterminal A and lookahead terminal a to the unique production to apply.
terms.left recursion: A production of the form A→Aα which causes infinite loops in top-down (recursive descent) parsers.
terms.ambiguity: A grammar is ambiguous if some string has more than one leftmost (or rightmost) derivation / parse tree.

exam_tips: Be able to compute NULLABLE, FIRST, and FOLLOW sets step-by-step for a given grammar and construct the LL(1) parsing table.
Know the LL(1) algorithm: match terminals by popping and reading next token; predict nonterminals using M[X,a]; detect conflicts as multiple entries in M[A,a].
Left-recursion elimination: given A→Aα|β transform to A→βA', A'→αA'|ε; be able to apply this transformation.
Understand why bottom-up parsing handles left-recursive grammars directly while LL(1) cannot, and know the relative power ordering: LL(1) ⊂ LR(1).

key_facts: The derivation of a compiler in this course starts from an interpreter, performs principled refinements (CPS transformation, defunctionalization, etc.), and arrives at a compiler.
Continuation-passing style (CPS) transforms a direct-style interpreter so that control is passed explicitly via continuations, making the evaluation order and stack explicit.
Defunctionalization replaces higher-order functions (continuations) with data structures, enabling a transition from a functional interpreter to a stack-based machine.
Semantic preservation: a correct compiler must ensure that for all programs P, eval(P) = run(compile(P)), i.e., compilation preserves the observable behavior.
The Jargon VM bytecode serves as the target language; the compiled output consists of instructions like MK_CLOSURE, APPLY, LOOKUP, PUSH, RETURN, HALT.
Stack-based compilers can be systematically derived from interpreters by making the implicit call stack explicit through CPS and then representing it as a data structure.

terms.continuation: A function representing 'the rest of the computation'; CPS makes all continuations explicit as function arguments.
terms.CPS transformation: Continuation-passing style transformation: converts a direct-style program so that every function takes an extra continuation argument instead of returning.
terms.defunctionalization: Transformation replacing higher-order functions with tagged data structures and a dispatch function (apply), enabling first-order representation.
terms.semantic preservation: The property that compiled code has the same observable behavior as the source program.
terms.interpreter: A program that directly executes source language programs by recursively evaluating AST nodes.
terms.stack-based compiler: A compiler whose target is a stack machine where operands are pushed/popped from an explicit stack.

exam_tips: Understand the pipeline: interpreter → CPS interpreter → defunctionalized interpreter → compiler; be able to describe each transformation.
Semantic preservation is the key correctness criterion; know how to state it formally: ∀P, eval(P) = run(compile(P)).
Be prepared to trace through small examples of CPS transformation showing how the call stack becomes explicit.
Know the distinction between compile-time and run-time: type checking is compile-time; memory allocation and function calls are run-time.

key_facts: Calling conventions specify how arguments are passed (registers vs stack), who saves/restores registers (caller vs callee), and how the return value is communicated.
Nested scope (closures) requires capturing non-local variables; this is handled at compile time by representing closures as code pointer + environment.
Non-local variables in nested functions are accessed via a static chain (static link) pointing to the enclosing frame, or by closure conversion.
Tuples and arrays are laid out contiguously in memory; field access compiles to a base address plus a fixed offset.
References (mutable cells) are heap-allocated; reading dereferences the pointer, writing stores through it.
The LOOKUP instruction in Jargon VM takes a stack location offset to access variables in enclosing scopes via the runtime stack.

terms.calling convention: The protocol governing how function arguments, return values, and saved registers are managed across a function call.
terms.closure: A pair of a code pointer and an environment (captured variable bindings) representing a function value with free variables.
terms.static link: A pointer stored in an activation record pointing to the enclosing function's frame, used to access non-local variables.
terms.activation record: The stack frame for a function call, containing local variables, parameters, saved registers, and return address.
terms.closure conversion: Transformation that makes captured (free) variables explicit by packaging them with the function code into a closure data structure.
terms.non-local variable: A variable referenced inside a nested function that is defined in an enclosing scope.

exam_tips: Know the difference between caller-saves and callee-saves registers and when each is appropriate.
Be able to describe how closures capture non-local variables and how they are represented at runtime (code pointer + environment record).
For nested procedures, trace through how a non-local variable is accessed via static links or closure environments.
Understand how arrays/tuples are compiled to offset-based memory access and why the offset is a compile-time constant for statically-typed languages.

key_facts: Inline expansion replaces a function call with the function body, eliminating call overhead but potentially increasing code size.
Constant folding evaluates expressions with known constant operands at compile time (e.g., 2+3 → 5), reducing runtime computation.
Tail recursion elimination converts a tail-recursive call into a loop (jump to function start with updated arguments), avoiding stack growth.
Peephole optimization replaces short sequences of instructions with more efficient equivalents by scanning a sliding window over generated code.
A tail call is a function call that is the last action in a function; tail call optimization replaces it with a goto, enabling constant stack space for tail-recursive functions.
Generic (machine-independent) optimizations are applied in the middle end; machine-specific optimizations (e.g., instruction selection, register allocation) in the back end.

terms.inline expansion: Replacing a call site with the body of the called function, eliminating call/return overhead at the cost of code size.
terms.constant folding: Compile-time evaluation of expressions whose operands are all known constants.
terms.tail call: A function call that is the final operation in a function, whose result is immediately returned by the calling function.
terms.tail recursion elimination: Optimization converting tail-recursive calls to jumps, achieving constant stack space for tail-recursive functions.
terms.peephole optimization: Local optimization that examines a small window of generated instructions and replaces inefficient patterns with better sequences.
terms.dead code elimination: Removing code that can never be executed or whose results are never used.

exam_tips: Be able to identify whether a recursive call is a tail call and show the transformation to iterative/loop form.
Know concrete examples of peephole optimizations (e.g., eliminating redundant loads/stores, replacing multiply-by-power-of-2 with shift).
Constant folding and inline expansion interact: inlining can expose new constant folding opportunities.
Understand why tail recursion elimination is especially important for functional languages where loops are expressed as recursion.

key_facts: Garbage collection automatically reclaims heap memory no longer reachable from the program's roots; common algorithms include mark-and-sweep, copying collection, and reference counting.
Static linking incorporates library code into the executable at compile time; dynamic (shared) linking loads library code at runtime, allowing sharing between processes.
Objects in OO languages are represented as records with a pointer to a vtable (virtual dispatch table) containing method pointers for dynamic dispatch.
Dynamic method dispatch (virtual dispatch) looks up the method pointer in the vtable at runtime, enabling polymorphism; direct (static) dispatch is resolved at compile time.
Exception handling requires unwinding the call stack to find a matching handler; implementations use either setjmp/longjmp or table-based zero-cost exception mechanisms.
Inheritance in OO languages is implemented by having subclass vtables extend (and potentially override entries of) superclass vtables.

terms.garbage collection: Automatic memory management that reclaims heap objects no longer reachable from program roots.
terms.mark-and-sweep: GC algorithm: mark all reachable objects from roots, then sweep (free) all unmarked objects.
terms.vtable: Virtual dispatch table: a per-class array of method pointers used to implement dynamic dispatch in OO languages.
terms.dynamic dispatch: Runtime selection of the method implementation to invoke, based on the actual (dynamic) type of the receiver object.
terms.static linking: Incorporating library object code into the final executable at link time.
terms.dynamic linking: Resolving and loading library code at program load time or first use, enabling code sharing between processes.
terms.exception handling: A control-flow mechanism for signaling and handling error conditions, requiring stack unwinding to find a matching handler.
terms.method dispatch: The process of selecting and invoking the correct method implementation for a given message/call and receiver.

exam_tips: Be able to sketch the vtable layout for a class hierarchy with inheritance and method overriding, showing how subclass vtables extend superclass ones.
Know the trade-offs between static and dynamic linking (code size, sharing, versioning, load time).
For garbage collection, understand the root set, what makes an object reachable, and the basic mark-and-sweep algorithm.
Exception handling questions often ask about stack unwinding: know how the runtime finds the nearest enclosing handler and what cleanup is performed.

key_facts: A register machine has finitely many registers R0, R1, ..., Rn each storing a natural number, and a program consisting of labelled instructions of three types: R+ → L' (increment and jump), R- → L', L'' (decrement-or-zero with branch), and HALT.
A configuration is a tuple (ℓ, r0, ..., rn) where Lℓ is the current label; computation is deterministic so initial and final register contents define a partial function.
f ∈ ℕⁿ ⇀ ℕ is register machine computable if there exists a machine M such that starting with R0=0, R1=x1, ..., Rn=xn (all others zero), M halts with R0=y iff f(x1,...,xn)=y.
A computation halts either by reaching a HALT instruction (proper halt) or by reaching a label beyond the program (erroneous halt, which can always be converted to a proper halt).
Computable functions include addition, multiplication, projection, constant zero, successor, truncated subtraction, integer division, integer remainder, exponentiation base 2, and logarithm base 2.
Register machine computation is deterministic: in any non-halting configuration, the next configuration is uniquely determined by the program.

terms.register machine: A computational model with finitely many natural-number registers and a program of labelled increment, decrement-or-branch, and HALT instructions.
terms.configuration: A tuple (ℓ, r0, ..., rn) representing the current instruction label and register contents at a point in computation.
terms.computable function: A partial function f: ℕⁿ ⇀ ℕ computed by a register machine using the standard I/O convention (inputs in R1..Rn, output in R0).
terms.proper halt: Halting by executing a HALT instruction as opposed to an erroneous halt from falling off the end of the program.
terms.partial function: A function f ⊆ X × Y where each x has at most one y with (x,y) ∈ f; written f: X ⇀ Y.
terms.total function: A partial function where f(x) is defined for all x ∈ X.
terms.truncated subtraction: x ÷ y = x - y if y ≤ x, else 0; computable by register machine.

exam_tips: When asked to write or trace a register machine program, carefully track register values at each step using the configuration tuple format (ℓ, r0, r1, ..., rn).
Remember the I/O convention: inputs are in R1, ..., Rn (R0 starts at 0), and the output is left in R0 after halting — examiners frequently test this.
Copying a register requires a temporary register T; you cannot read a register without emptying it in the basic model.
Be careful distinguishing partial functions (f(x) ↓ or f(x) ↑) from total functions; computable definition requires the machine halts iff f is defined.

key_facts: Pairs are encoded as ⟨x,y⟩ = 2^x(2y+1) - 1 (bijection ℕ×ℕ → ℕ) and ⟨⟨x,y⟩⟩ = 2^x(2y+1) (bijection ℕ×ℕ → ℕ>0); 0 serves as a marker for the empty list/HALT.
Lists are encoded by: ⌈[]⌉ = 0 and ⌈x :: ℓ⌉ = ⟨⟨x, ⌈ℓ⌉⟩⟩ = 2^x(2·⌈ℓ⌉ + 1), giving a bijection from listℕ to ℕ.
Instruction bodies are encoded as: ⌈R⁺ᵢ → Lⱼ⌉ = ⟨⟨2i, j⟩⟩, ⌈R⁻ᵢ → Lⱼ, Lₖ⌉ = ⟨⟨2i+1, ⟨j,k⟩⟩⟩, ⌈HALT⌉ = 0; a program P is encoded as ⌈[⌈body0⌉,...,⌈bodyn⌉]⌉.
Any e ∈ ℕ decodes to a unique register machine program prog(e); prog(0) is the empty program that halts immediately.
The Universal Register Machine U takes e (program code) in R1 and a (encoded argument list) in R2, simulates prog(e) on arguments decoded from a, and halts with the same result in R0.
U uses registers: P (program code), A (current register contents), PC (program counter), N (current instruction), C (instruction type), R (current register value), plus auxiliary registers S, T, Z.

terms.G\u00f6del numbering: A systematic encoding of programs (or formulas) as natural numbers.
terms.pair encoding \u27e8x,y\u27e9: The bijection ℕ×ℕ → ℕ defined by ⟨x,y⟩ = 2^x(2y+1) - 1.
terms.pair encoding \u27e8\u27e8x,y\u27e9\u27e: The bijection ℕ×ℕ → ℕ>0 defined by ⟨⟨x,y⟩⟩ = 2^x(2y+1); used for list cons since 0 marks empty list.
terms.prog(e): The register machine program decoded from index e ∈ ℕ; every natural number indexes a unique program.
terms.universal register machine: A register machine U that can simulate any register machine prog(e) on any inputs by interpreting the program code e.
terms.\u03c6\u2091: The unary partial function computed by prog(e); the map e ↦ φₑ enumerates all computable partial functions.

exam_tips: Know why ⟨⟨·,·⟩⟩ is used for list cons (needs 0 as a special marker for the empty list) while ⟨·,·⟩ is used when every number must be decodable as a pair.
Be able to decode a number into its list, then decode each list element into an instruction — examiners often ask you to decode a given number into prog(e).
Remember that the even/odd distinction in instruction encoding (2i for R⁺, 2i+1 for R⁻) is how U determines instruction type from C.
The fact that any e ∈ ℕ decodes to a valid program (prog(0) halts immediately) means there is no 'invalid program index' — every number is a program.

key_facts: The Halting Problem is undecidable: there is no register machine H such that for all e, a, H started with R1=e, R2=⌈[a]⌉ halts with R0=1 iff prog(e) halts on input a.
Proof by contradiction: if H existed, construct C that runs H(e,e) and halts iff H says 'does not halt'; letting e = ⌈C⌉ gives C halts iff C does not halt.
The partial function f defined by f(x) = 0 if φₓ(x)↑, undefined if φₓ(x)↓ is not computable (diagonal argument), since assuming f = φₑ leads to contradiction on e.
A set S ⊆ ℕ is decidable iff its characteristic function χS (= 1 on S, 0 off S) is register machine computable; otherwise S is undecidable.
S₀ = {e | φₑ(0)↓} is undecidable: if it were decidable, one could decide the halting problem by constructing a machine that prepends input setup to prog(e) and runs the decider.
S₁ = {e | φₑ is a total function} is undecidable: decidability of S₁ would imply decidability of S₀ (replace R1 with ⌈R1:=0; prog(e)⌉ and run the S₁ decider).

terms.halting problem: The decision problem of determining, given (e, a), whether prog(e) halts when started with input a.
terms.undecidable set: A set S ⊆ ℕ whose characteristic function is not register machine computable.
terms.decidable set: A set S ⊆ ℕ whose characteristic function χS is register machine computable.
terms.characteristic function \u03c7S: The total function χS: ℕ → ℕ with χS(x)=1 if x∈S and χS(x)=0 if x∉S.
terms.diagonalisation: The proof technique of constructing a function that differs from every function in an enumeration on the diagonal entry, yielding a contradiction.
terms.reduction: A technique showing problem A is undecidable by showing decidability of A would imply decidability of a known undecidable problem (e.g., the halting problem).

exam_tips: In the undecidability proof, clearly construct C explicitly: obtain H', then C by modifying the HALTs to loop when R0=1, and argue using the index c of C — examiners want to see each step.
To prove a set S is undecidable, use reduction: show that a decider for S can be converted into a decider for the halting problem (or for S₀), giving a contradiction.
The uncomputable function f(x)=0 if φₓ(x)↑ uses diagonalisation — distinguish this from the halting problem proof which uses self-application.
Remember S₀ and S₁ as standard examples of undecidable sets beyond the halting problem itself; know the reduction chain: halting problem → S₀ → S₁.

key_facts: A Turing machine is a tuple (Q, Σ, s, δ) with finite state set Q, finite tape alphabet Σ containing ▷ (left endmarker) and ␣ (blank), initial state s ∈ Q, and transition function δ: Q×Σ → (Q∪{acc,rej})×Σ×{L,R,S}; the endmarker ▷ is never overwritten and always causes a rightward move.
A configuration is (q, w, u) where w is the non-empty string of symbols at and left of the head (last character = symbol under head) and u is the string to the right; computation starts in (s, ▷, u).
A Turing machine halts when it reaches state acc or rej; computation may be infinite if neither is ever reached.

Every Turing machine computation can be implemented by a register machine (Theorem 13), and conversely every register machine computation can be implemented by a Turing machine (Theorem 16), so Turing computable = register machine computable.

A function f: ℕⁿ ⇀ ℕ is Turing computable if a Turing machine M, started on tape coding [0, x1, ..., xn], halts iff f(x1,...,xn)↓, and in that case the final tape codes a list whose first element is y = f(x1,...,xn).

The Church-Turing Thesis states that every algorithm in an intuitive sense can be realised as a Turing machine; supported by equivalence of all known formalisms (register machines, λ-calculus, partial recursive functions, etc.).

terms.Turing machine: A formal model of computation consisting of a finite state machine operating on an infinite tape via a transition function.
terms.transition function \u03b4: A function δ: Q×Σ → (Q∪{acc,rej})×Σ×{L,R,S} specifying the new state, symbol to write, and head movement for each (state, symbol) pair.
terms.left endmarker \u25b7: A special tape symbol marking the left boundary of the tape; never overwritten, always causes rightward head movement.
terms.blank \u2423: The default tape symbol; only finitely many cells contain non-blank symbols.
terms.Turing computable: A partial function computable by a Turing machine under the tape-encoding convention using lists separated by 0s and 1s.
terms.Church-Turing thesis: The (unprovable) claim that every intuitively computable function is Turing computable; supported by equivalence of all proposed formalisms.
terms.tape encoding of list: A tape over {▷,␣,0,1} codes [n1,...,nk] as ▷␣...0 1^n1 ␣ 1^n2 ␣ ... 1^nk 0␣..., with exactly two 0-cells bounding the data.

exam_tips: Know the formal definition of a Turing machine configuration (q, w, u) and be able to trace transitions step-by-step as in Example 11.
The equivalence proof sketch (TM → RM: encode config as number, implement δ as RM, loop; RM → TM: implement each RM instruction on tape) is frequently examined at a high level.
The Church-Turing thesis is a thesis (not a theorem) — it cannot be formally proved but is supported by evidence; examiners may ask you to state this distinction.
Remember that ▷ is always on the leftmost cell and the TM always moves right from it — this is a side condition in the definition of δ that is easy to forget.

key_facts: The three basic functions are: projⁿᵢ(x1,...,xn)=xᵢ (projection), zeroⁿ(x1,...,xn)=0 (constant zero), and succ(x)=x+1 (successor); all are register machine computable.
Primitive recursion: given f ∈ ℕⁿ ⇀ ℕ and g ∈ ℕⁿ⁺² ⇀ ℕ, there is a unique h = ρⁿ(f,g) ∈ ℕⁿ⁺¹ ⇀ ℕ with h(x⃗,0)≡f(x⃗) and h(x⃗,x+1)≡g(x⃗,x,h(x⃗,x)).
Every primitive recursive function (f ∈ PRIM) is total; PRIM is closed under composition and primitive recursion but not all computable functions are in PRIM.
Minimisation: µⁿf(x⃗) is the least x such that f(x⃗,x)=0 and f(x⃗,i) is defined and >0 for all i<x; undefined if no such x exists. This introduces partiality.

A partial function is partial recursive (f ∈ PR) iff it can be built from basic functions by composition, primitive recursion, and minimisation; every f ∈ PR is computable AND every computable function is in PR (Theorems 24 & 25).

Ackermann's function ack, defined by ack(0,x2)=x2+1, ack(x1+1,0)=ack(x1,1), ack(x1+1,x2+1)=ack(x1,ack(x1+1,x2)), is a total recursive function that grows faster than any primitive recursive function and hence is not in PRIM.
