terms.primitive recursive (PRIM): The smallest set of total functions containing the three basic functions and closed under composition and primitive recursion.
terms.partial recursive (PR): The smallest set of partial functions containing the basic functions and closed under composition, primitive recursion, and minimisation.
terms.\u03c1\u207f(f,g): The function defined by primitive recursion from f (base) and g (step): h(x⃗,0)=f(x⃗), h(x⃗,x+1)=g(x⃗,x,h(x⃗,x)).
terms.minimisation \u00b5\u207ff: The operation yielding the least x such that f(x⃗,x)=0 with f defined and positive at all smaller values; introduces partiality.
terms.Kleene equivalence \u2261: LHS ≡ RHS means both are undefined or both are defined and equal.
terms.Ackermann's function: A total recursive but non-primitive-recursive function that grows faster than any primitive recursive function.
terms.total recursive function: A member of PR that is also a total function.

exam_tips: To show a function is primitive recursive, express it explicitly using ρⁿ, composition, and basic functions — e.g. add=ρ¹(proj¹₁, succ∘proj³₃), pred=ρ⁰(zero⁰, proj²₁).

Ackermann's function is the canonical example of a computable (total recursive) but non-primitive-recursive function — know its definition and why it escapes PRIM (it grows faster than every PRIM function).
The key step in proving PR ⊆ computable for minimisation: the RM loops computing f at x=0,1,2,... and halts when R0=0, otherwise increments C and repeats.
Conversely for computable ⊆ PR: construct PRIM functions lab, val0, nextM coding TM/RM configurations, define configM by primitive recursion, then haltM by minimisation.

key_facts: λ-terms are built from variables x, y, z, ... by λ-abstraction (λx.M) and application (MN); α-equivalence identifies terms differing only in names of bound variables (λx.M =α λy.M[y/x] when y#M).
β-reduction: one-step (λx.M)N → M[N/x] (capture-avoiding substitution); many-step M ↠ M' is the reflexive-transitive closure; β-conversion M =β N holds iff M and N reduce to a common term.
Church-Rosser theorem: β-reduction ↠ is confluent — if M ↠ M1 and M ↠ M2 then ∃M' with M1 ↠ M' and M2 ↠ M'. Corollary: β-normal forms are unique up to α-equivalence.
Church numerals: n̲ = λfx.fⁿx (n applications of f to x); True=λxy.x, False=λxy.y, Pair=λxyf.fxy, Succ=λx₁fx.f(x₁fx), Pred uses pairs to step (a,b)↦(a+1,a).
Curry's Y combinator: Y = λf.(λx.f(xx))(λx.f(xx)) satisfies YM =β M(YM) for all λ-terms M, providing fixed points for recursive definitions.

A partial function f ∈ ℕⁿ ⇀ ℕ is λ-definable iff it is computable (= register machine computable = partial recursive); the proof uses: every PR function is λ-definable (using Y for recursion), and λ-definable functions are RM-computable (encode λ-terms as numbers and interpret β-reduction).

terms.\u03bb-abstraction: The term (λx.M) representing a function with parameter x and body M.
terms.application: The term (MN) representing applying M to argument N.
terms.free variable FV(M): Variables occurring in M not bound by any enclosing λ; FV(λx.M)=FV(M)\{x}.
terms.\u03b1-equivalence =\u03b1: Terms equal up to consistent renaming of bound variables; λx.M =α λy.M[y/x] when y#M.
terms.\u03b2-redex: A subterm of the form (λx.M)N that can be contracted to M[N/x].
terms.\u03b2-normal form: A λ-term containing no β-redexes; unique up to α-equivalence if it exists.
terms.Church numeral n\u0332: The λ-term λfx.fⁿx encoding natural number n; n̲MN =β Mⁿ N.
terms.Y combinator: Y = λf.(λx.f(xx))(λx.f(xx)); satisfies YM =β M(YM), providing fixed points for recursive λ-terms.
terms.combinator: A closed λ-term with no free variables, e.g. I=λx.x, K=λxy.x, Ω=(λx.xx)(λx.xx).
terms.normal-order reduction: The deterministic strategy of reducing the leftmost, outermost redex first; always reaches the β-normal form if one exists.
terms.\u03bb-definable function: f ∈ ℕⁿ ⇀ ℕ is λ-definable if ∃ closed λ-term F: F x̲₁...x̲ₙ =β ȳ when f(x₁,...,xₙ)=y, and F x̲₁...x̲ₙ has no β-nf when f is undefined.

exam_tips: In β-reduction steps, always perform capture-avoiding substitution — rename bound variables using α-conversion before substituting if the substituted term has free variables matching a binder (e.g. Example 27: (λx.λy.x)y must first α-convert to (λx.λz.x)y).

Know Ω = (λx.xx)(λx.xx) as the canonical non-terminating term: Ω → Ω. Also know that (λx.y)Ω =β y but only if you reduce the outer redex first (normal order always finds the normal form if it exists).
For Church numerals: verify computations by checking n̲ M N =β Mⁿ N explicitly. Know Succ, Pred (using pairs), Eq0, True, False, If, and Pair/Fst/Snd and their β-reduction properties.
The Y combinator gives a fixed point but doesn't 'solve' equations — it tells us a solution exists. Know that Y M =β M(YM) and be able to derive this from the definition.

key_facts: Circuit switching reserves resources end-to-end for the duration of a connection; packet switching uses statistical multiplexing with no reservation.
Total delay in a packet-switched network = transmission delay + propagation delay + queueing delay + processing delay.
The hourglass model places IP as the single narrow waist, allowing many link-layer technologies below and many applications above.
Circuit switching requires a setup phase (reservation packet traverses all n links, confirmation returns) before data transfer begins.
In packet switching with Q packets of size D=H+P over n links: last bit arrives at time n*(D/B) + (Q-1)*(D/B) + n*L (store-and-forward with latency L).
For large file sizes F, packet-switching is faster (no setup overhead); for very small payloads (high header overhead), circuit-switching becomes relatively better.

terms.store-and-forward: A node must receive the entire packet before it can begin transmitting it on the next link.
terms.statistical multiplexing: Multiple flows share a link asynchronously; capacity is allocated on demand rather than reserved.
terms.hourglass model: Network architecture where IP is the single universal layer interconnecting diverse lower-layer technologies and upper-layer applications.
terms.propagation delay: Time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver across a physical medium; depends on distance and medium speed.
terms.transmission delay: Time to push all bits of a packet onto the link; equals packet size divided by link bandwidth.
terms.queueing delay: Time a packet waits in a router buffer before being transmitted; variable and dependent on traffic load.
terms.circuit switching: A network model where a dedicated end-to-end path with reserved resources is established before communication begins.
terms.packet switching: A network model where data is broken into packets that are independently routed and share links with other traffic.

exam_tips: Be able to derive and compare end-to-end transfer times for circuit-switched vs packet-switched networks given n links, bandwidth B, latency L, file size F, packet size D, and header size H.

Questions often ask about the limiting behaviour: large F favours packet switching; tiny payload (large header fraction) favours circuit switching; large B makes the ratio approach circuit:packet = 1 (propagation dominates both).
The end-to-end principle states that functions should be implemented at the endpoints rather than in the network; be ready to discuss NAT as a violation and why it may or may not matter.
Know the distinction between architectural principles (e.g., end-to-end, fate-sharing) and architectural design choices (e.g., 32-bit addresses, TCP as the reliable transport).

key_facts: Ethernet uses CSMA/CD with binary exponential backoff: after k-th collision, node waits a random number of slot times chosen from {0, 1, ..., 2^k - 1}.

The minimum Ethernet frame size (64 bytes) is determined by the need for collision detection: a sender must still be transmitting when a collision signal returns, so frame transmission time >= 2 * propagation delay.
CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) can detect all single-bit errors, all burst errors of length <= degree of generator polynomial, and most longer burst errors.
A simple checksum code always has minimum Hamming distance 2, so it can detect single-bit errors but cannot correct them.
An Ethernet switch learns MAC addresses by observing source addresses of incoming frames and builds a forwarding table; unknown destinations are flooded.
VLANs provide logical separation of broadcast domains on a single physical switch infrastructure, with traffic isolation between VLANs enforced by the switch.

terms.CSMA/CD: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection: listen before transmitting, detect collisions during transmission, back off and retry.
terms.binary exponential backoff: After the k-th collision, choose a random wait from 0 to 2^k-1 slots before retransmitting, up to a maximum.
terms.CRC: Cyclic Redundancy Check: error-detection coding based on polynomial division over GF(2), appending a remainder to transmitted data.
terms.Hamming distance: The number of bit positions in which two codewords differ; minimum distance of a code determines its error-detection/correction capability.
terms.MAC address: 48-bit link-layer address burned into a network interface card, used for frame delivery within a single LAN segment.
terms.Ethernet switch: A link-layer device that selectively forwards frames between ports based on a learned MAC address table.
terms.VLAN: Virtual LAN: logical grouping of ports on a switch so that traffic is isolated as if on separate physical networks.
terms.token ring: A LAN protocol where a special token frame circulates and only the token holder may transmit, preventing collisions.
terms.slotted ring: A ring network divided into fixed-size slots that circulate continuously; nodes insert data into empty passing slots.
terms.Manchester coding: A line code that encodes each bit as a transition (high-to-low or low-to-high) ensuring frequent transitions for clock recovery.

exam_tips: Be able to explain why shared Ethernet has a maximum cable length: the round-trip propagation time must be less than the minimum frame transmission time so collisions can be detected.
Understand why token ring is analysed as shared media even though there is no shared physical medium: bandwidth is shared logically via the token.

For error detection vs correction: packet networks use detection + retransmission (ARQ) because error correction codes are less efficient when loss rates are low; only use FEC where retransmission is impractical (e.g., wireless, streaming).
Know the difference between destination-delete and source-delete in ring networks, and the role of a token monitor and its limitations regarding node hardware failure.

key_facts: Wireless links suffer from path loss, multipath fading, and interference from other sources, making the channel much less predictable than wired links.
IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) uses CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance) rather than CSMA/CD because collision detection is not feasible on wireless links.
The hidden terminal problem occurs when two nodes can both reach an access point but cannot hear each other, causing collisions at the AP that neither sender detects.
CDMA assigns each user a unique chipping code; transmitted bits are XOR'd with the code sequence; receivers recover the original signal by correlating with the same code.
In CDMA, codes are chosen to be nearly orthogonal so that signals from multiple simultaneous transmitters can be separated at the receiver.
Aspects of CDMA and FDM appear at the data-link layer since the DLL must participate in channel/code selection, not purely at the physical layer.

terms.CSMA/CA: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance: nodes sense the channel and use random backoff before transmitting to reduce collision probability.
terms.hidden terminal problem: Two wireless nodes can both reach an access point but are out of range of each other, causing undetected collisions at the AP.
terms.CDMA: Code Division Multiple Access: multiple users share the same frequency band simultaneously using unique spreading codes.
terms.chipping code: A pseudo-random bit sequence assigned to a CDMA user; each data bit is spread by XOR with the entire code sequence.
terms.multipath fading: Signal degradation caused by multiple reflected copies of the transmitted signal arriving at the receiver with different phases.
terms.IEEE 802.11: The family of wireless LAN standards (Wi-Fi) specifying PHY and MAC layer protocols for wireless communication.
terms.access point: A wireless network device that connects wireless clients to a wired network infrastructure.

exam_tips: Know the CDMA encoding/decoding procedure: transmit bit 1 as the code sequence, bit 0 as the bitwise complement; receiver recovers by correlating received signal with the code.
Be able to work through a CDMA example with two simultaneous transmitters: add the two transmitted sequences (treating 0 as -1), then correlate with each code to recover both messages.
Collision detection is not feasible on wireless links because a transmitting node's own signal overwhelms any incoming collision signal; this motivates CSMA/CA and RTS/CTS mechanisms.
The DJG notes indicate that for 25/26 exams, only knowledge of various PHY characteristics and how they manifest at the DLL interface is examinable, not detailed modulation schemes.

key_facts: Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR) requires an IP address plus a subnet mask (prefix length) to define a network; longest-prefix match is used for forwarding decisions.
NAT allows multiple private-address hosts to share a single public IP address by rewriting IP addresses and port numbers and maintaining a mapping table.
NAT breaks the end-to-end principle because it introduces state in the network core and prevents arbitrary external hosts from initiating connections to internal hosts.
DHCP provides dynamic IP address assignment using a four-message exchange: DHCP Discover, DHCP Offer, DHCP Request, DHCP ACK.
Class-based IPv4 addressing wasted large blocks of addresses (e.g., class A gave /8 blocks); CIDR with hierarchical allocation reduces routing table size through aggregation.
ARP maps IP addresses to MAC addresses within a subnet by broadcasting a request; the target replies with its MAC address which is then cached.

terms.CIDR: Classless Inter-Domain Routing: IP addresses paired with prefix-length masks, enabling flexible allocation and route aggregation.
terms.longest prefix match: Forwarding rule where a router selects the routing table entry whose network prefix is the longest (most specific) match for the destination IP.
terms.NAT: Network Address Translation: a middlebox that rewrites source IP/port on outgoing packets and reverses this on incoming packets to share one public IP.
terms.DHCP: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol: automatically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS server addresses to hosts.
terms.ARP: Address Resolution Protocol: discovers the MAC address corresponding to a known IP address within the same subnet via broadcast.
terms.subnet mask: A bitmask indicating which bits of an IP address identify the network (prefix) versus the host.
terms.private address: IP address ranges (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) reserved for use within private networks, not routable on the public Internet.
terms.fragmentation: Splitting an IP datagram into smaller pieces to traverse a link with a smaller MTU; reassembled at the destination.

exam_tips: Be able to apply longest-prefix matching to a forwarding table: convert addresses and prefixes to binary and find the most specific match.
Know the relationship between the gateway and interface fields in a forwarding table: the gateway is the next-hop IP address used for ARP lookup on the outgoing interface.
Understand why NAT violates end-to-end principles but also why this may not matter in practice (most connections are client-initiated; NAT traversal techniques exist).
For IPv6 questions (if asked): IPv6 was only covered briefly in 25/26; focus on the key motivation (address exhaustion) and that SLAAC and DHCPv6 both exist for address configuration.

key_facts: Link-State routing (e.g., OSPF) floods complete topology information to all nodes, then each node independently runs Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest paths; achieves global consistency.
Distance-Vector routing (e.g., RIP) has each node share only its distance vector (cost to each destination) with immediate neighbours; suffers from the count-to-infinity problem.
In Distance-Vector routing, node A updates its cost to destination X via neighbour B as: cost(A,B) + B's advertised cost to X, taking the minimum over all neighbours.
Link-State routing has inconsistency windows during topology changes (nodes may have different views transiently), but converges faster than Distance-Vector.
Forwarding uses a local table to move packets hop-by-hop; routing is the distributed computation that builds and maintains forwarding tables.
For the topology in Figure 1, using Dijkstra: A to B takes path A-C-B (cost 2); A to F takes path A-C-E-F (cost 3) under original link costs.

terms.link-state routing: Routing protocol where each node floods its local link costs to all others; all nodes compute shortest paths from complete topology knowledge.
terms.distance-vector routing: Routing protocol where nodes iteratively exchange cost vectors with neighbours; each node updates routes using the Bellman-Ford equation.
terms.forwarding table: A per-router table mapping destination address prefixes to outgoing interfaces (and next-hop addresses).
terms.Bellman-Ford: Algorithm underlying distance-vector routing: cost to X via neighbour B = cost(self,B) + cost(B,X); take minimum over all neighbours.
terms.count-to-infinity: A slow-convergence problem in distance-vector routing where nodes incrementally increase their distance estimate to an unreachable destination.
terms.Dijkstra's algorithm: Shortest-path algorithm used in link-state routing; computes minimum-cost paths from a source to all other nodes given full topology.
terms.BGP: Border Gateway Protocol: the inter-domain routing protocol of the Internet; routes based on policy as well as path length.
terms.hot potato routing: Routing strategy where a node forwards a packet on the first available outgoing link to get it out of the local network as quickly as possible.
terms.flood routing: Routing strategy where a packet is sent out on all interfaces except the one it arrived on; guarantees delivery but wastes bandwidth.

exam_tips: Be able to trace Distance-Vector routing updates step by step using the table format shown in the exercises: update row X column B as min(current, cost_to_B + B's_cost_to_X).
Know how Link-State routing handles inconsistent updates (e.g., question 17(d)(vi)): if C has stale information, A may compute a different path than C, leading to routing loops transiently.
Distinguish forwarding (data plane, per-packet, uses local table) from routing (control plane, distributed computation, builds the table).

For topology discovery protocol design questions, recall that each node must flood its neighbour information network-wide; total information transmitted is O(n * l * d) where n=nodes, l=links, d=diameter.

key_facts: TCP uses a sliding window ARQ scheme combining sequence numbers, cumulative ACKs, timeouts, and fast retransmit (on 3 duplicate ACKs) for reliable delivery.
TCP slow start doubles CWND each RTT (exponential growth) until ssthresh; congestion avoidance then increases by 1 MSS per RTT (linear). On timeout, ssthresh = CWND/2 and CWND resets to 1 MSS.
The sliding window size needed to fill a pipe = bandwidth * RTT (the bandwidth-delay product); e.g., 100 Mbps * 20 ms = 250,000 bytes.
UDP provides multiplexing/demultiplexing (via port numbers) and optional checksum; it does not provide reliability, ordering, or congestion control.
TCP state (sequence numbers, window sizes, buffers) is held at the endpoints only, consistent with the end-to-end principle; the network core is stateless.
In Go-Back-N, all packets from the lost one onwards are retransmitted; in Selective Repeat, only the lost packet is retransmitted. TCP uses a hybrid closer to Selective Repeat in practice.

terms.sliding window: A flow/error control mechanism where the sender may have up to W unacknowledged packets in flight simultaneously.
terms.ARQ: Automatic Repeat reQuest: a reliability mechanism using acknowledgements and retransmission on timeout or negative acknowledgement.
terms.CWND: Congestion Window: TCP sender-side limit on unacknowledged data in flight, adjusted by congestion control algorithms.
terms.ssthresh: Slow-Start Threshold: when CWND reaches this value, TCP switches from slow-start (exponential) to congestion-avoidance (linear) growth.
terms.fast retransmit: TCP mechanism that retransmits a lost segment immediately upon receiving 3 duplicate ACKs, without waiting for a timeout.
terms.cumulative ACK: An acknowledgement indicating that all bytes up to and including the acknowledged sequence number have been received correctly.
terms.MSS: Maximum Segment Size: the largest amount of data TCP will send in a single segment, negotiated during connection setup.
terms.bandwidth-delay product: The number of bits (or bytes) that can be in transit on a link at one time; equals bandwidth times round-trip time.
terms.piggybacking: Combining an acknowledgement with outgoing data in the same packet to reduce total packet count in bidirectional communication.
terms.silly window syndrome: Performance problem where TCP sends very small segments; cured by Nagle's algorithm (sender) or Clark's solution (receiver).

exam_tips: Be able to calculate retransmission times from first principles: trace packet departure times, add transmission time (1 ms) plus latency (10 ms) for delivery, then add latency for ACK return; apply 3-dup-ACK or timeout rule.
For ACK stream analysis questions, remember that cumulative ACKs mean a duplicate ACK indicates the receiver got an out-of-order segment; a repeated ACK value does not always mean a drop.
Know all four TCP timers: retransmission timer, persistence timer (prevent deadlock when window=0), keepalive timer, time-wait timer (2MSL after connection close).
The bandwidth-delay product calculation is a common exam question: window size in bytes = (link rate in bps * RTT in seconds) / 8.

key_facts: HTTP is a stateless request-response protocol over TCP; persistent HTTP reuses the same connection for multiple requests, reducing connection setup overhead.
DNS uses a hierarchy of servers (root, TLD, authoritative) with recursive or iterative resolution; results are cached with TTLs to reduce load on root servers.
DNS primarily uses UDP (port 53) because queries and responses are small and the overhead of TCP connection setup outweighs reliability benefits; retransmission is handled at the application layer.
A web cache (proxy) can serve cached content without contacting the origin server, reducing latency and origin server load; conditional GET checks whether cached content is still fresh.
There are only 13 root DNS server IP addresses because DNS responses must fit in a single UDP packet (512 bytes original limit); anycast is used to replicate these globally.
Pipelining in persistent HTTP allows multiple requests to be sent without waiting for responses, significantly reducing total transfer time for multiple objects.

terms.HTTP: HyperText Transfer Protocol: stateless application-layer protocol for fetching web resources, runs over TCP.
terms.persistent HTTP: HTTP mode where the TCP connection is kept open across multiple request-response pairs.
terms.DNS: Domain Name System: distributed hierarchical database that maps hostnames to IP addresses.
terms.recursive DNS resolution: The local DNS server contacts other DNS servers on behalf of the client and returns the final answer.
terms.iterative DNS resolution: The DNS server returns a referral to the next server to contact; the client or local server follows the chain itself.
terms.TTL: Time To Live: in DNS, the duration for which a cached record is considered valid before re-querying the authoritative server.
terms.conditional GET: An HTTP GET request including an If-Modified-Since header; the server responds with 304 Not Modified if the resource is unchanged.
terms.web cache: A proxy server that stores copies of recently requested web objects to serve future requests without contacting the origin server.
terms.CDN: Content Delivery Network: geographically distributed servers that serve content from locations close to users, reducing latency.
terms.SMTP: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol: application-layer protocol for sending email between mail servers.

exam_tips: Be able to calculate total transfer time for file retrieval under different HTTP modes (non-persistent sequential, non-persistent concurrent, persistent sequential, pipelined): count TCP handshakes, request RTTs, and data transfer time.

For the web cache timing question: cache hit with valid TTL costs negligible RTT (A-to-C); cache miss costs full path (A-C-B); expired TTL with unchanged file costs conditional GET RTT to B plus response; expired with changed file costs full object transfer.

Know why firewalls accept HTTP but may block other protocols: HTTP uses well-known port 80 and is assumed benign; this also explains why tunnelling other protocols over HTTP undermines firewall security.
DNS caching means root servers are rarely consulted in practice; understand the full resolution chain and where caching occurs at each level.

key_facts: Multimedia applications have specific QoS requirements: video requires high bandwidth and low jitter; audio requires low latency and low jitter; both are tolerant of some packet loss.
CDNs serve content from geographically distributed servers selected via DNS redirection or anycast, reducing latency and origin server load.
Streaming video can tolerate some loss (missing frames can be concealed) but is highly sensitive to jitter; playback buffers are used to smooth out network delay variation.
Different local DNS servers can return different IP addresses for the same hostname, directing users to the nearest or least-loaded CDN replica.

MPEG encoding uses inter-frame compression (P and B frames referencing I frames), making it efficient but sensitive to I-frame loss; Motion JPEG encodes each frame independently, trading compression for resilience.
For wireless MPEG delivery, FEC or interleaving can be used to protect against burst losses characteristic of wireless channels.

terms.jitter: Variation in packet arrival times; particularly harmful for real-time applications like VoIP and video streaming.
terms.playout buffer: A buffer at the receiver that delays playback to absorb jitter and smooth packet arrival irregularities.
terms.CDN: Content Delivery Network: a distributed system of servers that delivers content to users from geographically nearby locations.
terms.MPEG: A family of video compression standards using both intra-frame (spatial) and inter-frame (temporal) compression.
terms.Motion JPEG: Video encoding where each frame is independently JPEG-compressed; no inter-frame dependencies, simpler but less efficient than MPEG.
terms.SIP: Session Initiation Protocol: an application-layer signalling protocol for establishing, modifying, and terminating multimedia sessions.
terms.RTP: Real-time Transport Protocol: an application-layer protocol providing timing information, sequence numbers, and payload identification for real-time media streams.
terms.FEC: Forward Error Correction: adding redundant data to allow receivers to recover from packet loss without retransmission.
terms.anycast: A routing method where packets are delivered to the topologically nearest of a group of servers sharing the same IP address.

exam_tips: Note that compression (MPEG, JPEG) was not lectured in 25/26 per the DJG notes; focus on delivery requirements and CDN architecture rather than codec details.
Understand the trade-off between resilience and compression efficiency: Motion JPEG is easier to implement and loss-resilient (no inter-frame dependencies) at the cost of larger file sizes.
CDN DNS-based load balancing: different DNS servers return different IPs for the same name; no IP-layer changes needed as routing is handled normally once the IP is resolved.

Know that web caches and CDN edge servers both reduce latency and origin load but differ in deployment: web caches are typically at ISP or enterprise boundaries, CDN servers are purpose-deployed by content providers.

key_facts: HCI has three waves: 1st wave (1980s) from Human Factors/Cognitive Science focusing on efficiency; 2nd wave (1990s) from Anthropology/Sociology focusing on socio-technical systems; 3rd wave (2000s) from Art/Cultural Studies focusing on user experience and culture.
Gestalt theory of perceptual organisation is an empirically-based theory of visual perception that can be applied to predict and explain design choices.
The design process is iterative with divergent phases (creative exploration) and convergent phases (selection and evaluation); HCI theories support both invention and critique.
Alternative future perspectives include positive computing (wellbeing, flow), accessibility/inclusion, and feminist utopianism (Bardzell) which amplifies marginalised voices.
First-wave HCI used cognitive science to model perception, decision and action to predict usability; the primary goal was efficiency (speed and accuracy) for well-defined tasks.
Second-wave HCI used ethnography and Contextual Inquiry, recognising computers as part of a socio-technical system; participatory design empowered users.

terms.Three waves of HCI: Framework describing the historical shift in HCI theory from cognitive science (1st), to social science (2nd), to art/culture (3rd).
terms.Gestalt theory: Empirically-based theory of visual perceptual organisation used to predict and explain design choices.
terms.Divergent phase: Creative exploration phase in iterative design where theories provide a basis for invention and recognising new alternatives.
terms.Convergent phase: Selection and evaluation phase in iterative design where theory provides critique and comparison of design options.
terms.Socio-technical system: 2nd-wave HCI concept that computers are only one part of a broader social system including conversations, paper, and physical environment.
terms.Speculative design: 3rd-wave design approach (e.g. Dunne and Raby) created to provoke and question, with criteria relating to how interesting or thoughtful the interaction is.
terms.Critical perspective: The ability to use theory to systematically compare cases to each other and relate opinions to scientific evidence.

exam_tips: Be able to classify a given piece of HCI research into one of the three waves by identifying its objectives, methods, and theoretical references (e.g. psychology → 1st wave, anthropology → 2nd wave, philosophy/art → 3rd wave).
Distinguish clearly between the design goals of each wave: efficiency for 1st, social/contextual fit for 2nd, user experience/meaning for 3rd.
Examiners may ask how theory is applied in divergent vs convergent design phases — know that theories support both invention and critique.
Do not confuse the CHI conference (pronounced 'kai') with any other acronym; it is the primary academic community for HCI research.

key_facts: The fundamental principle of visual display design is establishing a correspondence between an invisible information structure and visible marks on a display surface.
Five major categories of visual representation exist: typography/text, maps/graphs, schematic drawings, pictures, node-and-link diagrams, and icons/symbols.

The 'resemblance fallacy' is the mistaken belief that drawings depict real objects because perceiving the flat image simulates perceiving the real scene; pictorial representations rely on shared interpretive conventions.
Node-and-link diagrams exploit 'secondary notation' — use of the 2D plane to carry information unrelated to the technical content (e.g. the London Underground diagram).

The desktop metaphor is best analysed through theories of visual representation (not visual metaphor): its value lies in enabling secondary notation and using window borders as typographic separators.
Theories of visual metaphor (e.g. the desktop-as-office analogy) are less useful than theories of visual representation; attempts to elaborate metaphors (e.g. Microsoft Bob) have historically failed.

terms.Correspondence: The mapping between elements/relationships in an invisible information structure and visible marks on a display surface.
terms.Secondary notation: Use of the display plane to carry information in ways not related to the formal technical content (e.g. spatial arrangement of nodes in a diagram).
terms.Resemblance fallacy: The mistaken belief that visual representations depict reality because perceiving them simulates perceiving the real scene.
terms.Semiotics: The field offering sophisticated analysis of how marks correspond to meanings, including pictorial, symbolic, and metonymic correspondence.
terms.Typographic grid: The structured layout system used in text-based displays, inherited from paper document design conventions.
terms.Visual metaphor: The use of analogy to a familiar physical domain (e.g. desktop) to explain an interface; less analytically useful than visual representation theory.
terms.Node-and-link diagram: A connectivity diagram where node positions are irrelevant to technical content, allowing secondary notation; popularised by the London Underground map.

exam_tips: Know the distinction between visual metaphor and visual representation theory — examiners may ask which better explains the Macintosh desktop and why.
Be able to identify which category of visual representation (map, graph, schematic, picture, node-and-link, icon) applies to a given example and justify it.
Secondary notation is a key concept: be able to explain it with the node-and-link diagram example and relate it to cognitive dimensions (Lecture 8).
Do not claim that pictorial representations 'simulate' reality — always emphasise that they rely on shared conventions, which shift across cultures and historical periods.

key_facts: First-wave HCI meta-theory: user interaction can be modelled as search through a state space toward a goal, using breadth-first or depth-first strategies.
Cognitive Walkthrough evaluates interfaces by identifying the next user goal, checking action availability, label recognisability, and system feedback of progress.
Bounded rationality describes goal-directed planning that takes computational expense into account; users use satisficing strategies (satisfactory rather than optimal plans).
Kahneman and Tversky's Prospect Theory models human decisions via a utility function weighting estimated benefits by likelihood, without requiring complete state space knowledge.

Wicked problems (Rittel and Webber) have 10 characteristics including: no definitive formulation, no stopping rule, solutions are good-or-bad not true-or-false, and every problem is essentially unique.

Attention Investment theory explains why end users prefer repeated manual actions over programming automation: the utility function weighs future attention savings against costs and risks of abstract specification.

terms.Bounded rationality: Goal-directed planning that accounts for the expense of computation, leading to satisficing rather than optimal strategies.
terms.Satisficing: Choosing a plan that is satisfactory within constraints rather than optimal.
terms.Prospect theory: Kahneman and Tversky's model of human decision-making as a utility function weighting estimated benefits by likelihood.
terms.Availability heuristic: Reasoning based on examples easily to hand rather than systematic calculation.
terms.Loss aversion: Cognitive bias where losses hurt more than equivalent gains feel good.
terms.Wicked problem: A problem class (Rittel & Webber) with no definitive formulation, no stopping rule, and unique solutions that cannot be tested in advance.
terms.Attention Investment: Theory explaining end-user programming decisions as a cost-benefit analysis of attention spent programming vs. attention saved by automation.
terms.Persuasive design: HCI field that considers how users might be influenced to choose or modify their goals, often using nudge methods.

exam_tips: Be able to list and apply all 10 characteristics of wicked problems — examiners frequently ask students to apply these to real-world HCI scenarios.
Distinguish clearly between optimising search (complete state space knowledge) and prospect theory (estimated utility with bounded rationality).
Know the specific heuristics and biases from behavioural economics (availability, affect, representativeness, loss aversion, expectation bias, bandwagon effect) and be able to give HCI examples.
Attention Investment is a specific theory for end-user programming — do not confuse it with general bounded rationality; be precise about its utility function components.

key_facts: A uniform prior keyboard model yields ~4.8 bits per key press, but English compresses to ~2.1 bits per character — the difference represents information inefficiency in equal-probability key designs.
Information gain per action is h(x_i) = log(1/p(x_i)); smart systems reduce redundancy by using non-uniform priors based on language models.

Adaptive user interfaces dynamically change layout to maximise context-specific information gain (e.g. making 'u' larger after 'Q'), but layout changes require relearning, typically following a power law of practice.
Bayes' theorem is used to model next-token probability given prior context, with the advantage that relative ratios do not require explicit computation of context probability.
Smart interfaces are implicitly normative: optimising for one statistical distribution (e.g. English prose) makes other distributions (e.g. source code) harder.
LLMs offer sophisticated token prediction but users benefit only to the extent their goals correspond to the prior encoded by the model publisher.

terms.Uniform prior: Bayesian assumption that all outcomes are equally probable; used as a baseline for keyboard efficiency analysis.
terms.Information gain: h(x_i) = log(1/p(x_i)); the amount of information conveyed by observing outcome x_i.
terms.Adaptive user interface: An interface that dynamically adjusts its layout based on probabilistic context to maximise information transfer efficiency.
terms.Power law of practice: Empirical regularity whereby the time to perform a task decreases as a power function of the number of times the task has been practiced.
terms.Semantic pointing: Technique that modifies mouse-to-pixel mapping so effective target width increases and effective distance decreases for more probable targets.
terms.Normative design: A design optimised for a particular use case that implicitly disadvantages other use cases with different statistical distributions.
terms.Error repair cost: The additional interaction cost when user actions diverge from intentions in ways not captured by the language model.

exam_tips: Be able to derive or apply the information gain formula h(x_i) = log(1/p(x_i)) and explain why equal-probability key designs are inefficient for natural language input.
Examiners may ask you to write a Bayesian expression for word prediction — know how Bayes' theorem applies to next-token selection given context.
Understand the trade-off between theoretical optimality and skill acquisition rate (power law of practice) — adaptive interfaces are not always better in practice.
Be aware of the normative limitation of smart interfaces: always consider what other user populations or tasks are disadvantaged by the chosen statistical model.

key_facts: Fitts' Law: Time = k·log(2D/W), where D is distance to target and W is target width; the index of difficulty is the ratio D/W and represents information gained by the pointing action.

The Keystroke Level Model (KLM) models expert-user task completion time as a sum of unit operations: keystrokes (~200ms), mouse homing (~400ms), mental preparation (~1500ms), and system response time.

A/B testing is a randomised controlled trial where different users receive different interface versions; click-through and behavioural data are compared to determine which version produces desired behaviour.

The Hawthorne Effect: productivity improved regardless of direction of change in factory lighting experiments, showing that being observed (not the treatment) can cause apparent performance improvements.
The speed-accuracy trade-off can be described as an information channel: faster actions produce more noise, so information gain per unit time does not increase proportionally with action rate.

Taylorism is the optimisation of human efficiency by treating workers as measurable components; second-wave HCI arose partly in reaction to this, incorporating worker rights via trade union collaboration.

terms.Fitts' Law: Time = k·log(2D/W); the time to point at a target is proportional to log of distance/width, capturing speed-accuracy trade-off in pointing tasks.
terms.Index of difficulty: The ratio D/W (distance to width) in Fitts' Law; represents the information gained by a successful pointing action.
terms.Keystroke Level Model (KLM): A quantitative analytic method modelling expert task completion time as a sum of unit operation times (keystrokes, mouse moves, mental preparation, system response).
terms.GOMS: Goal/Operator/Method/Selection — a cognitive model successor to KLM that more accurately predicts mental preparation time.
terms.A/B test: A randomised controlled trial in which different users receive different interface versions and behavioural outcomes are compared.
terms.Hawthorne Effect: The finding that worker productivity improved regardless of the direction of experimental changes, due to motivation from being observed.
terms.Taylorism: Scientific management approach (F.W. Taylor) that measures and optimises human worker efficiency as if workers were machine components.
terms.Semantic pointing: Modification of mouse-to-pixel mapping to increase the effective size of probable targets, exploiting Fitts' Law for efficiency gains.

exam_tips: Know Fitts' Law formula precisely (Time = k·log(2D/W)) and be able to apply it to design decisions such as target sizing and semantic pointing.
KLM requires the user to be expert — examiners may test whether you know its limitations (does not model errors, learning, or unfamiliar task reasoning).
Distinguish between statistical significance and practical importance: very small effects can be statistically significant with large samples, but HCI design research prioritises large effect sizes.
Be prepared to critique experimental user studies using concepts like the Hawthorne Effect, experimental demand, and the distinction between internal and external validity.

key_facts: Design ethnography attempts to observe people in their normal environment over a substantial period, documenting activities and artefacts through photography, video, audio and notes.
Outputs of ethnographic study include task flow diagrams, journey maps, concept generation maps, activity timelines, written reports, and 'thick' (detailed, experiential, literary) descriptions.
Ethnography rarely provides a simple list of requirements; instead it develops in-depth understanding of users and inspires design activity and prototype creation.
Mixed methods combine qualitative ethnography with quantitative research (e.g. surveys) to achieve broader understanding than either method alone.
Ethnography helps teams understand: (A) what things happen (everyday life, technology experiences, user strategies) and (B) how things matter (social integration, meaning-making, user control).

Ethnographic studies can be integrated iteratively alongside design workshops: first to understand current experiences, then to evaluate novel scenarios, then to assess early product designs in real-world context.

terms.Design ethnography: Holistic, in-context observation of people in their natural environment over time, used to drive innovation and inform design decisions.
terms.Thick description: Detailed, experiential, even literary accounts of observed behaviour that capture meaning and context beyond mere factual recording.
terms.Mixed methods: Research approach combining qualitative (e.g. ethnography) and quantitative (e.g. surveys) methods for richer understanding.
terms.Contextual Inquiry: A second-wave HCI research method involving observation and interview in the user's own work environment.
terms.Participatory design: Design approach that integrates diverse stakeholders — including end users — into the design process to empower users and acknowledge multiple value systems.
terms.Journey map: Ethnographic output showing the timeline of a user's activities and experiences across a process or service.

exam_tips: Distinguish ethnography from requirements capture: ethnography provides understanding and inspiration, not a simple feature list.
Be able to describe what ethnography reveals under both categories: what things happen (observable activities) and how things matter (social and personal meaning).
Know how ethnography fits within the three waves of HCI — it is a second-wave method but also central to third-wave user experience research.
Mixed methods questions may ask you to justify combining qualitative and quantitative approaches — emphasise that each addresses limitations of the other.

key_facts: Fundamental distinction: Formative evaluation (early, open questions, refining ideas) vs Summative evaluation (later, closed questions, testing against predefined criteria).
Two further axes: Analytical (theory-based, no users needed) vs Empirical (observation/measurement of users); and Quantitative (numbers) vs Qualitative (words, pictures, video).

Randomised Controlled Trials require: (1) a performance measure, (2) a representative sample with informed consent, and (3) an experimental task; results reported as effect size, correlation, and significance.

Categorical coding assigns transcript segments to a predefined coding frame; inter-rater reliability (Cohen's Kappa for 2 raters, Fleiss' Kappa for more) should be assessed, with 0.6–0.8 being 'substantial agreement'.
Grounded theory is used for open qualitative questions: open coding → memo writing → axial coding → constant comparison → saturation.
Bad evaluation techniques to avoid: purely affective reports (biased), unsupported claims ('it was deemed'), and single-subject introspective reports (HiPPO — Highest-Paid Person's Opinion).

terms.Formative evaluation: Evaluation used early in design to compare, assess and refine ideas; typically involves open research questions.
terms.Summative evaluation: Evaluation used later to test a completed or near-complete system against predefined criteria; typically involves closed research questions.
terms.Internal validity: The degree to which a study was conducted correctly (reproducibility, scientific integrity, refutability).
terms.External validity: The degree to which study results generalise to real-world situations (representative sample, task, and context).
terms.Cohen's Kappa: Statistical measure of inter-rater reliability for two coders, comparing agreement relative to chance; 0.6–0.8 is substantial.
terms.Grounded theory: Qualitative data analysis method for open questions: open coding, memo writing, axial coding, constant comparison, reaching saturation.
terms.Categorical coding: Qualitative analysis method for closed questions: create coding frame, segment text, assign segments to categories, compare frequencies.
terms.HiPPO: Highest-Paid Person's Opinion — a bad evaluation technique where a single authority's subjective view substitutes for evidence.
terms.Cognitive walkthrough: Analytical formative evaluation method simulating user goal-directed steps through an interface.

exam_tips: Be able to place any given evaluation method in the 2×2 matrix of Analytical/Empirical × Quantitative/Qualitative, and explain whether it suits formative or summative evaluation.
Know the specific steps of both grounded theory and categorical coding — examiners distinguish between them and may ask when each is appropriate.
Always consider both internal and external validity when critiquing or designing an experiment — a technically correct study may have poor external validity.
Explicitly identify bad evaluation techniques if asked to critique a methodology: affective ratings from friends, unsupported claims, and single-subject introspection are all invalid.

key_facts: Complex systems require 'broad-brush' analysis techniques because task-based approaches cause 'death by detail' through combinatorial explosion of tasks and domain elements.
Interaction spaces (e.g. programming languages, APIs, AI systems, CAD tools) contrast with defined-use systems: users select and configure components rather than following prescribed tasks.

The Cognitive Dimensions of Notations (CDs) framework provides a vocabulary for design discussion of interaction spaces, based on the observation that all designs involve trade-offs — improving one dimension often degrades another.
Viscosity (resistance to change) is an example CD: small conceptual changes requiring large numbers of edits (e.g. changing a type in Java across many functions) represent high viscosity.
CDs define six notational activities: Search, Incrementation, Modification, Transcription, Exploratory Design, and Collaboration — different activities prioritise different dimensions.
Ethical and accountability issues in complex AI systems extend beyond the interface: who provides training data, how are they paid, and whether systems advance or undermine human rights and SDGs.

terms.Interaction space: A complex system (e.g. programming language, API) in which users select and configure components rather than following predefined task sequences.
terms.Cognitive Dimensions of Notations (CDs): A broad-brush vocabulary for discussing design trade-offs in interaction spaces, covering dimensions such as viscosity, visibility, and abstraction.
terms.Viscosity: CD: resistance to change — the degree to which small conceptual changes require large amounts of editing work.
terms.Hidden dependencies: CD: important links between entities that are not visible to the user, making the impact of changes hard to predict.
terms.Secondary notation: CD: extra information conveyed through means other than formal syntax (e.g. spatial layout, colour choices).
terms.Premature commitment: CD: constraints that force the user to make decisions before they have enough information to make them well.
terms.Progressive evaluation: CD: the ability to check work-in-progress at any point without completing the full specification.
terms.Closeness of mapping: CD: how directly the notation corresponds to the domain being represented.
terms.Exploratory design: Notational activity combining incrementation and modification where the desired end state is not known in advance (e.g. 'hacking').
terms.Death by detail: The failure mode of task-based analysis when applied to complex systems, caused by combinatorial explosion of possible tasks and actions.

exam_tips: Be able to define and give examples of at least 6 cognitive dimensions — viscosity, hidden dependencies, secondary notation, premature commitment, visibility, and closeness of mapping are most commonly tested.
Know all six notational activities and be able to explain which CDs are most relevant to each (e.g. viscosity is critical for exploratory design and modification, less so for incrementation).
Understand the trade-off principle: you cannot maximise all CDs simultaneously. Examiners may give a design scenario and ask you to identify which trade-offs have been made.
Be prepared to discuss ethical dimensions of complex AI systems (bias, accountability, data labelling labour) as part of HCI — the course explicitly extends 'beyond cognition and interaction'.

key_facts: A Prolog program consists of clauses (facts and rules); a rule has the form Head :- Body where the body is a conjunction of goals.
Unification does not have a direction: variables unify with anything, atoms unify with themselves, compound terms unify if they have the same functor/arity and all arguments unify.
Prolog execution is depth-first, left-to-right search through clauses in the order they appear in the program.
The occurs check (e.g. X = a(X)) is not performed by default in standard Prolog, potentially creating circular structures.
Comments use %, argument modes in comments use + (instantiated input), ? (anything), - (output); the query prompt is ?- and consult uses [filename].
A compound term is written functor(arg1,...,argN) with functor/arity notation; e.g. foo(a,b(1),c) is foo/3.

terms.fact: A clause with no body; asserts an unconditional relation, e.g. tall(andy).
terms.rule: A clause with a head and body separated by :-; head succeeds if all body goals succeed.
terms.unification: The process of making two terms identical by substituting variables; the core operation in Prolog.
terms.functor: The name of a compound term; together with arity uniquely identifies a relation.
terms.arity: The number of arguments of a compound term or predicate.
terms.query: A conjunction of goals posed to the Prolog interpreter using the ?- prompt.
terms.clause: A single fact or rule in a Prolog program.
terms.goal: A term in the body of a rule or a query that Prolog attempts to satisfy.
terms.atom: A basic constant in Prolog; a lower-case identifier or quoted string.
terms.variable: An upper-case or underscore-prefixed identifier that can be bound via unification.

exam_tips: Know the unification rules precisely: variables unify with anything, atoms only with themselves, compound terms require same functor, same arity, and pairwise argument unification.
Do not confuse a(Y) used as a COMPOUND TERM (in unification) with a(Y) used as a RELATION CALL (in a goal context).
Remember that changing the order of clauses within a relation affects execution order and termination, but changing the order of relations in the program does not affect declarative meaning.
Argument mode annotations (+, ?, -) are for documentation only; Prolog itself does not enforce them.

key_facts: The is/2 operator evaluates a ground numeric expression on the RHS and unifies the result with the LHS variable or number; e.g. X is 1+2 gives X=3.
1+1 is a compound term +(1,1) and does NOT evaluate to 2 under unification alone; only 'is' triggers arithmetic evaluation.
Lists are represented as [H|T] where H is the head and T is the tail; [] is the empty list; [a,b,c] is syntactic sugar for [a|[b|[c|[]]]].
Last Call Optimisation (LCO) applies when a predicate is determinate at the point it calls the last goal in a clause body, allowing O(1) stack space instead of O(n).
An accumulator is an extra argument threaded through recursive calls to carry a partial result, enabling LCO and avoiding the need to unwind the stack.
The naive len/2 without accumulator uses O(n) stack space; the accumulator version len_acc/3 can use O(1) stack space with LCO.

terms.is/2: Built-in operator that evaluates the RHS as an arithmetic expression and unifies with LHS.
terms.accumulator: An extra argument passed through recursive calls to accumulate a partial result, copied to output at the base case.
terms.Last Call Optimisation (LCO): Space optimisation that reuses the current stack frame when a predicate is determinate before its last recursive call.
terms.list: A recursive data structure; either [] (empty) or [H|T] where H is the head element and T is a list tail.
terms.head: The first element of a non-empty list, bound by the pattern [H|T].
terms.tail: The remainder of a list after the head, itself a list, bound by the pattern [H|T].
terms.deterministic: A predicate that has at most one solution and leaves no choice points.
terms.ground term: A term containing no unbound variables.

exam_tips: Never write X = max(L) expecting functional evaluation; always flatten: max(L, X), then use X.
To apply LCO, the predicate must be deterministic at the point of the last call; if there are remaining choice points, LCO cannot apply.
The accumulator base case copies the accumulator to the output argument (e.g. len_acc([], Acc, Acc)); do not forget this.
Distinguish 'N is M + 1' (arithmetic evaluation) from 'N = M + 1' (unification with the compound term +(M,1)).

key_facts: Cut (!) is an extra-logical predicate that, when reached, commits to the current clause and discards all choice points created since the parent goal was called; backtracking through a cut fails the entire containing predicate.
Cut placed inside a relation only removes choice points for that relation's clauses; it does not affect choice points in other relations called before it.
Negation as failure \+(Goal) succeeds if and only if Goal fails; it is implemented as: try Goal, if it succeeds fail, if it fails succeed.

Because \+(Goal) succeeds only when Goal fails, any variable bindings made during the attempted proof of Goal are lost; \+ should only be used with fully instantiated (ground) goals for predictable behaviour.
A \ = B succeeds if A and B cannot be unified (deterministic); this is safer than \+ for inequality testing.
The preferred way to avoid cut is to use arithmetic guards (e.g. N > 1) or orthogonal clause heads rather than cut-fail combinations.

terms.cut (!): A goal that always succeeds but removes all choice points created since the parent call; prevents backtracking into earlier clauses of the same predicate.
terms.negation as failure (\\+): Extra-logical predicate: \+(G) succeeds iff G cannot be proved; loses any bindings made during the attempt.
terms.choice point: An internal record of an alternative clause to try if the current computation fails.
terms.green cut: A cut that does not change the logical meaning of a program but improves efficiency by preventing redundant search.
terms.red cut: A cut that changes the logical meaning of a program; removing it would give different answers.
terms.\\=: Succeeds if two terms cannot be unified; deterministic inequality test.

exam_tips: Avoid using cut (!) unless the question explicitly asks for it or permits it; use arithmetic guards instead for clean programs.
When tracing execution with cut, remember cut only affects choice points within the predicate where it appears, not in callers.
\+(X) loses variable bindings; never rely on bindings from inside a negation-as-failure call.
The exam question will typically say 'avoid unnecessary use of cut' — use guards like N > 1 in the rule body instead.

key_facts: Prolog's default search strategy is depth-first, left-to-right; this is complete for finite search spaces but can loop on infinite branches.
Generate-and-test is a core Prolog pattern: a non-deterministic generator produces candidates via backtracking, and a deterministic test filters them.
To find a path in a graph without cycles, maintain an accumulator of visited nodes and check new nodes against it using notmember before recursing.
Iterative deepening adds a depth/error bound parameter to the test clause; solutions within the bound are found first, then the bound is relaxed, finding simpler solutions before complex ones.
The accumulator pattern for graph search stores the path so far; at the base case the accumulated path is reversed to give the solution in correct order.
Interleaving generate and test steps (as in Sudoku) prunes the search space dramatically compared to generating all candidates first.

terms.generate-and-test: A search pattern where a non-deterministic generator produces candidates and a deterministic predicate filters them.
terms.depth-first search: Prolog's default execution: explores one branch fully before backtracking to try alternatives.
terms.iterative deepening: Search strategy that adds a bound parameter and gradually relaxes it, finding shallower solutions first while avoiding infinite loops.
terms.path accumulator: An extra argument that records nodes visited so far in a graph search, used to detect and avoid cycles.
terms.perm/2: A relation generating all permutations of a list via take/3; used as a generator in generate-and-test.
terms.take/3: Non-deterministic relation: take(L, X, R) succeeds when X is an element of L and R is L with X removed.
terms.notmember/2: Deterministic test that succeeds if an element is not in a list; used in cycle detection during search.

exam_tips: For graph search problems, always include a visited/closed list to prevent infinite loops on cyclic graphs.
Interleave test steps with generation steps to prune early; do not generate all solutions then test — this is inefficient and may not terminate.
perm/2 is a useful generator but factorial in complexity; when the question asks about efficiency, note this explicitly.
The accumulator for paths needs to be reversed at the base case since elements are prepended during recursion.

key_facts: A difference list represents a list as a pair Front-Back where Front is a list with a free variable tail and Back is that same free variable; the represented list is the difference Front \ Back.
Appending two difference lists is O(1): append(A-B, B-C, A-C) — simply unify the tail variable of the first with the head of the second.
An empty difference list is represented as X-X (any variable unified with itself), not []-[].
To convert a difference list to a normal list, unify the tail variable with []: if DL = List-[], then List is the normal list.
The canonical append for difference lists is the single fact: append(A-B, B-C, A-C).
Quicksort with difference lists eliminates the append call by propagating the tail variable through recursive calls, reducing O(n log n) append work.

terms.difference list: A representation of a list as a pair L-T where L is a list with free-variable tail T; the content is the elements between the front and T.
terms.append(A-B, B-C, A-C): The single-clause O(1) append for difference lists; works by sharing the tail variable.
terms.open-ended list: A list whose tail is an uninstantiated variable, allowing elements to be added in O(1) by instantiating the tail.
terms.tail variable: The unbound variable at the end of an open-ended list, used as the 'hole' in a difference list.
terms.difference list termination: Unifying the tail variable with [] to close the list and obtain a normal Prolog list.

exam_tips: When asked to write a difference list append, the answer is simply the fact append(A-B, B-C, A-C) — no recursive clauses needed.
Use X-X for an empty difference list when generating; use []-[] only when testing for the empty case.
In quicksort with difference lists, show how substituting the tail variable eliminates the append; the key transformation is replacing the accumulated list variable with [P|R1] directly.
A common error is treating A-B as subtraction; in difference list context, (-)/2 is just an ordinary functor used by convention.

key_facts: Constraint Logic Programming (CLP) extends Prolog by allowing constraints over domains (e.g. integers, reals) to be posted and propagated rather than requiring fully ground values; this prunes the search space early.
In pure Prolog, arithmetic via 'is' requires the RHS to be fully ground at call time; CLP(FD) allows arithmetic constraints to be solved lazily.
Tabled resolution (tabling/memoisation) caches the results of subgoals to avoid redundant recomputation and can prevent infinite loops in recursive predicates.
Prolog's closed-world assumption means that anything not provable is assumed false; this is a limitation compared to open-world reasoning.
Extra-logical predicates such as assert/retract modify the database at runtime and findall/bagof/setof collect all solutions; these break pure declarative semantics.
Prolog programs written as meta-interpreters can reason about other Prolog programs since a Prolog program is itself a valid Prolog term.

terms.constraint logic programming (CLP): Extension of Prolog where variables range over constrained domains and constraints are propagated, reducing search.
terms.CLP(FD): Constraint logic programming over finite domains of integers; allows arithmetic constraints without requiring ground values.
terms.tabling: Memoisation of subgoal results to avoid recomputation and prevent infinite loops in recursive predicates.
terms.closed-world assumption: The Prolog assumption that any goal that cannot be proved is false.
terms.assert/retract: Extra-logical predicates that add or remove clauses from the Prolog database at runtime, breaking referential transparency.
terms.findall/3: Extra-logical predicate that collects all solutions to a goal into a list.
terms.meta-interpreter: A Prolog interpreter written in Prolog itself, exploiting the fact that Prolog programs are valid Prolog terms.

exam_tips: The course emphasises avoiding assertz, findall, and \+ unless explicitly permitted; use pure Prolog (generate-and-test, accumulators) instead.
Understand the difference between posting a constraint (CLP) and evaluating an expression (is); the former does not require ground terms.
Know that Prolog's depth-first strategy is incomplete for infinite search spaces; iterative deepening or tabling are remedies.
Extra-logical predicates break declarative reading; if asked to explain a program's behaviour, note where side effects occur.

key_facts: A formula is valid (tautology) if every interpretation satisfies it; satisfiable if some interpretation satisfies it; unsatisfiable if no interpretation satisfies it.
S |= A if and only if {¬A} ∪ S is unsatisfiable; |= A iff {¬A} is unsatisfiable.
CNF is a conjunction of disjunctions of literals; DNF is a disjunction of conjunctions of literals; NNF uses only ∧, ∨, ¬ with ¬ applied only to atoms.
To convert to CNF: (1) eliminate ↔ and →, (2) push negations inward (gives NNF), (3) distribute ∨ over ∧, (4) simplify by removing tautological disjuncts and subsumed clauses.
Operator precedence (highest to lowest): ¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔.
A ≃ B (metalanguage equivalence) is the same as |= A ↔ B; both say A ↔ B is a tautology.

terms.interpretation: A function mapping propositional symbols to {0,1}.
terms.tautology: A formula that is true under every interpretation.
terms.satisfiable: A set of formulas for which some interpretation satisfies every formula in the set.
terms.unsatisfiable: A set of formulas for which no interpretation satisfies all formulas simultaneously.
terms.entailment (|=): S |= A means every interpretation satisfying all of S also satisfies A.
terms.literal: An atomic formula or its negation.
terms.CNF: Conjunctive Normal Form: conjunction of disjunctions of literals.
terms.DNF: Disjunctive Normal Form: disjunction of conjunctions of literals.
terms.NNF: Negation Normal Form: only ∧, ∨, ¬ used, with ¬ applied only to atoms.
terms.duality: Every propositional equivalence yields another by swapping ∧↔∨ and t↔f.

exam_tips: When converting to CNF, remember to fully simplify: delete disjuncts containing both P and ¬P (tautological), and delete clauses subsumed by shorter clauses.
Do not confuse A ↔ B (object-language formula) with A ≃ B (metalanguage statement); only |= A ↔ B is the same as A ≃ B.
To show a formula is not valid, exhibit a falsifying interpretation; to show it is not satisfiable, show every interpretation falsifies it.
Examiners frequently ask you to convert formulas to CNF/DNF and determine validity/satisfiability — always show each step.

key_facts: A sequent Γ ⇒ Δ is valid if whenever all formulas in Γ are true, at least one formula in Δ is true.
A basic sequent A, Γ ⇒ A, Δ is always valid because the same formula A appears on both sides.
Sequent rules are used backwards: start from the goal and reduce to basic sequents.
Right rules correspond to natural deduction introduction rules; left rules correspond to elimination rules.
The cut rule allows use of lemmas and is eliminable (cut-elimination theorem), though elimination may cause exponential blowup.
The Hilbert system uses axioms K (A→(B→A)), S ((A→(B→C))→((A→B)→(A→C))), DN (¬¬A→A) and Modus Ponens as sole inference rule.

terms.sequent: An expression Γ ⇒ Δ where Γ and Δ are finite sets of formulas.
terms.basic sequent: A sequent where the same formula appears on both sides: A,Γ ⇒ A,Δ.
terms.Modus Ponens: Inference rule: from A→B and A, derive B.
terms.Hilbert system: Proof system with axiom schemes and Modus Ponens as the only inference rule.
terms.natural deduction: Proof system with introduction and elimination rules for each connective, operating under explicit assumptions.
terms.cut rule: Sequent rule allowing a lemma A to be proved and then used: Γ⇒Δ,A and A,Γ⇒Δ give Γ⇒Δ.
terms.cut-elimination theorem: States that the cut rule is admissible: any proof using cut can be transformed to one without it.
terms.soundness: Every theorem generated by the proof system is a tautology.
terms.completeness: Every tautology can be proved by the proof system.
terms.weakening: Structural rule allowing additional formulas to be added to either side of a sequent.

exam_tips: Always work backwards in sequent calculus proofs: apply rules to the goal sequent until all branches reach basic sequents.
For the (→r) rule: A moves to the left and B stays on the right; for (→l): the antecedent A moves to the right and consequent B to the left.
A failed proof (non-basic leaf sequent) is not a proof of invalidity by itself — you must also exhibit a falsifying interpretation.
Know all six connective rules (negation, conjunction, disjunction, implication — left and right versions of each) for exam questions.

key_facts: FOL extends propositional logic with quantifiers ∀ (for all) and ∃ (there exists) over a non-empty universe of individuals.
Terms denote individuals (built from variables, constants, and function symbols); formulas denote truth values (built using predicate symbols and connectives).
A variable is free in a formula if it is not bound by a quantifier; a sentence is a formula with no free variables.
An interpretation for FOL specifies a non-empty domain D, an assignment of functions D^n→D for each n-place function symbol, and relations D^n→{0,1} for each n-place predicate symbol.
Substitution [t/x]A means replacing all free occurrences of x in A with term t, with care to avoid variable capture.
Quantifier equivalences: ¬∀x A ≃ ∃x ¬A and ¬∃x A ≃ ∀x ¬A; ∀x(A∧B) ≃ ∀xA∧∀xB; ∃x(A∨B) ≃ ∃xA∨∃xB.

terms.term: Expression denoting an individual: variable, constant, or f(t1,...,tn) for function symbol f.
terms.atomic formula: An expression P(t1,...,tn) where P is a predicate symbol and ti are terms.
terms.free variable: A variable occurrence not bound by any enclosing quantifier.
terms.bound variable: A variable occurrence within the scope of a quantifier that binds it.
terms.sentence: A formula with no free variables.
terms.interpretation (FOL): A non-empty domain D together with meanings for all function and predicate symbols.
terms.substitution [t/x]: Replacement of all free occurrences of variable x by term t in a formula, avoiding capture.
terms.variable capture: Occurs when substituting a term with a free variable into a formula causes that variable to become bound.
terms.universe: The non-empty domain of individuals over which quantifiers range.
terms.predicate symbol: A symbol denoting a relation on the domain; applied to terms it forms an atomic formula.

exam_tips: When performing substitution, always check for variable capture — rename bound variables first if necessary.
Distinguish free and bound occurrences: the same variable name can appear both free and bound in the same formula.
Know the quantifier-negation equivalences thoroughly: ¬∀xA ≃ ∃x¬A and ¬∃xA ≃ ∀x¬A are frequently tested.
Be precise about what an interpretation must specify: domain, function denotations, and predicate denotations.

key_facts: A clause is a disjunction of literals; clause form (CNF) represents a formula as a set of clauses (conjunction).
The empty clause □ is unsatisfiable (the empty disjunction is false); the empty set of clauses is valid.
DPLL (Davis-Putnam-Logemann-Loveland) is a backtracking SAT algorithm using unit propagation and pure literal elimination as simplification steps.
Unit propagation: if a clause contains exactly one literal L, assign it true, simplify all clauses containing L, and remove ¬L from other clauses.
Pure literal elimination: if a literal L appears only positively (or only negatively) across all clauses, assign it true and remove all clauses containing it.
Resolution rule: from clauses (P ∨ A) and (¬P ∨ B), derive resolvent (A ∨ B); if A and B are empty, the empty clause is derived, showing unsatisfiability.

terms.clause: A disjunction of literals, represented as a set of literals.
terms.clause form: Representation of a formula as a conjunction (set) of clauses.
terms.empty clause (\u25a1): The clause with no literals; equivalent to false/unsatisfiable.
terms.unit clause: A clause containing exactly one literal.
terms.DPLL: Davis-Putnam-Logemann-Loveland: backtracking SAT algorithm with unit propagation and pure literal elimination.
terms.unit propagation: Simplification step that forces assignment of a unit clause's literal to true.
terms.pure literal: A literal that appears in only one polarity across all clauses.
terms.SAT problem: The problem of determining whether a propositional formula is satisfiable (NP-complete).
terms.resolution rule: From (P∨A) and (¬P∨B), derive (A∨B) by eliminating the complementary pair P/¬P.
terms.resolvent: The clause derived by applying the resolution rule to two parent clauses.

exam_tips: In DPLL, apply unit propagation and pure literal elimination exhaustively before branching — show each simplification step clearly.
Remember that deriving the empty clause □ proves unsatisfiability of the clause set; this is used to prove validity of A by refuting ¬A.
Convert to clause form before applying resolution: eliminate ↔ and →, push negations in (NNF), distribute to get CNF, then write each conjunct as a clause.
Examiners test understanding of when resolution is complete (refutation complete) versus when additional refinements are needed.

key_facts: Prenex normal form (PNF) has all quantifiers at the front: Q1x1...Qnxn M where M is quantifier-free.
Skolemisation eliminates existential quantifiers: replace ∃y in scope of universals ∀x1...∀xn by a new Skolem function f(x1,...,xn); if no universal quantifiers precede it, use a Skolem constant.
Skolemisation is satisfiability-preserving but not equivalence-preserving: the Skolem form is equisatisfiable with the original.
A Herbrand universe is the set of all ground terms constructible from the function and constant symbols of the language (with at least one constant added if none exist).
Herbrand's theorem: a set S of clauses is unsatisfiable iff there exists a finite unsatisfiable set of ground instances of clauses from S.
Unification: two terms t1 and t2 are unifiable if there exists a substitution σ (most general unifier, MGU) such that σ(t1) = σ(t2); the MGU is unique up to renaming.

terms.prenex normal form (PNF): Formula with all quantifiers at the outermost level: Q1x1...Qnxn M, M quantifier-free.
terms.Skolem function: A new function symbol introduced to replace an existentially quantified variable, whose arguments are the universally quantified variables in whose scope it lies.
terms.Skolem constant: A Skolem function of arity 0, used when an existential quantifier has no enclosing universal quantifiers.
terms.equisatisfiable: Two formulas are equisatisfiable if one is satisfiable iff the other is (but they need not be logically equivalent).
terms.Herbrand universe: The set of all ground terms built from the constant and function symbols of the language.
terms.Herbrand interpretation: An interpretation whose domain is the Herbrand universe and where each ground term denotes itself.
terms.Herbrand's theorem: A clause set is unsatisfiable iff some finite set of its ground instances is unsatisfiable.
terms.unification: The process of finding a substitution making two terms syntactically identical.
terms.most general unifier (MGU): The most general substitution σ such that σ(t1)=σ(t2); all other unifiers are instances of it.
terms.occurs check: Check that a variable x does not occur in term t before binding x→t during unification, to prevent circular terms.

exam_tips: When Skolemising, the arity of a Skolem function equals the number of universal quantifiers whose scope contains the existential — get this right.
Skolemisation preserves satisfiability, not equivalence — never claim the Skolem form is logically equivalent to the original.
To convert to PNF before Skolemising: rename variables to be distinct, then pull quantifiers outward using the standard equivalences (being careful with ∃ under ¬ and in antecedents of →).
For unification, always perform the occurs check and show the MGU step by step; unification failure should be explicitly identified.

key_facts: Binary resolution: given clauses C1 = {L} ∪ R1 and C2 = {¬L} ∪ R2 (after applying MGU σ to unify L with the complement of a literal in C2), the resolvent is σ(R1 ∪ R2).
Factorisation: if two literals in the same clause unify (via MGU σ), replace them by a single literal; needed for completeness of first-order resolution.
Resolution is refutation-complete for FOL: a set of clauses is unsatisfiable iff the empty clause can be derived by resolution and factorisation.
Prolog uses SLD resolution (linear resolution with definite clauses): clauses have exactly one positive literal (Horn clauses), and resolution proceeds left-to-right on goals.
In Prolog, the search is depth-first with backtracking; it is incomplete for full FOL because it may loop and does not perform occur check by default.
Model elimination is a refinement of resolution that maintains an ancestor list to restrict which clauses can be resolved, making it more goal-directed.

terms.binary resolution: Inference rule deriving a resolvent from two clauses by unifying a literal in one with the complementary literal in the other.
terms.factorisation: Merging two unifiable literals in the same clause into one, using their MGU.
terms.SLD resolution: Selective Linear Definite resolution: the basis of Prolog execution, using Horn clauses.
terms.Horn clause: A clause with at most one positive literal.
terms.definite clause: A Horn clause with exactly one positive literal (a Prolog program clause).
terms.refutation: A proof of unsatisfiability by deriving the empty clause.
terms.linear resolution: A resolution strategy where one parent of each resolution step is the most recently derived clause.
terms.model elimination: A resolution refinement using ancestor lists to guide and restrict proof search.
terms.unification (resolution): Finding an MGU to make a literal and the complement of another literal identical, enabling resolution.
terms.depth-first search: Prolog's search strategy: explores one branch completely before backtracking.

exam_tips: Show unification steps explicitly when performing resolution — state the MGU used and the resulting resolvent at each step.
Remember factorisation is required for completeness — if two literals in a clause unify, you may need to factor before resolving.
Distinguish Prolog (SLD, Horn clauses, incomplete, no occurs check) from general resolution (complete for refutation of arbitrary FOL clause sets).
To prove a formula A valid using resolution: negate A, Skolemise, convert to clause form, then derive □ by resolution.
