key_facts: SMT (Satisfiability Modulo Theories) generalises SAT by checking satisfiability with respect to background theories such as linear arithmetic, arrays, or bit-vectors.
A decision procedure for a theory T determines whether a quantifier-free formula in T is satisfiable; examples include Simplex for linear arithmetic and congruence closure for equality.
The DPLL(T) framework integrates a SAT solver with theory solvers: the SAT solver handles Boolean structure while theory solvers check consistency of assignments over theory atoms.
A formula is T-satisfiable if there exists an interpretation in the theory T that satisfies it; T-valid if all T-interpretations satisfy it.
Nelson-Oppen combination allows combining decision procedures for different theories if the theories are signature-disjoint and share only the theory of equality.
SMT solvers use lemma learning (conflict-driven clause learning, CDCL) to prune the search space during backtracking.

terms.SMT: Satisfiability Modulo Theories: deciding satisfiability of formulas with respect to background theories.
terms.decision procedure: An algorithm that always terminates and decides a particular logical problem (e.g., satisfiability in a theory).
terms.theory: A set of axioms defining a mathematical domain (e.g., linear arithmetic, uninterpreted functions).
terms.DPLL(T): Framework combining DPLL SAT solving with pluggable theory solvers for SMT.
terms.theory solver: A decision procedure for the satisfiability of conjunctions of ground theory literals.
terms.congruence closure: Decision procedure for the theory of equality with uninterpreted functions.
terms.Nelson-Oppen: Method for combining decision procedures for signature-disjoint theories via equality sharing.
terms.CDCL: Conflict-Driven Clause Learning: SAT/SMT technique that learns new clauses from conflicts to avoid repeated search.
terms.linear arithmetic: Theory of linear equalities and inequalities over integers or rationals; decidable.
terms.T-valid: A formula is T-valid if it holds in all models of theory T.

exam_tips: Know the high-level architecture of DPLL(T): SAT solver proposes Boolean assignments, theory solver checks T-consistency of assigned literals.
Understand that decision procedures apply to quantifier-free fragments — full FOL theories are generally undecidable.
Be able to describe example theories (linear arithmetic, equality with uninterpreted functions) and their decision procedures.
Distinguish SAT (purely propositional) from SMT (propositional structure plus theory constraints over terms).

key_facts: A BDD (Binary Decision Diagram) is a rooted DAG representing a Boolean function; each internal node is labelled by a variable with two children (low=0, high=1), and leaves are 0 or 1.
A Reduced Ordered BDD (ROBDD) is canonical: for a fixed variable ordering, every Boolean function has a unique ROBDD.
To build an ROBDD: apply the Shannon expansion f = (¬x ∧ f[0/x]) ∨ (x ∧ f[1/x]) recursively, then reduce by merging isomorphic subgraphs and eliminating redundant nodes.
A node is redundant (and can be eliminated) if its low and high children are the same node.
BDD operations (AND, OR, NOT, etc.) are performed by the Apply algorithm, which recursively combines two BDDs using a computed-table cache for efficiency.
Variable ordering critically affects BDD size: a good ordering can yield polynomial size while a bad ordering can cause exponential blowup.

terms.BDD: Binary Decision Diagram: DAG representing a Boolean function with decision nodes and 0/1 leaf nodes.
terms.ROBDD: Reduced Ordered BDD: canonical form of a BDD for a fixed variable ordering, with no redundant nodes and no duplicate subgraphs.
terms.Shannon expansion: f = (¬x ∧ f[0/x]) ∨ (x ∧ f[1/x]): splits a Boolean function on variable x.
terms.variable ordering: A fixed total order on Boolean variables used throughout an ROBDD; critically affects size.
terms.Apply algorithm: Algorithm for computing the ROBDD of f op g from the ROBDDs of f and g, using a cache.
terms.redundant node: A BDD node whose low and high edges point to the same child; can be eliminated without changing the function.
terms.computed table: Cache used in the Apply algorithm to avoid recomputing results for identical subproblems.
terms.satisfying assignment: An assignment of variables making the BDD evaluate to 1; found by tracing a path to the 1-leaf.
terms.tautology check (BDD): A formula is a tautology iff its ROBDD is the single node 1.

exam_tips: When constructing a BDD, always apply reduction (merge duplicate subgraphs, remove redundant nodes) to obtain the canonical ROBDD.
Checking tautology with BDDs is O(1) once the ROBDD is built: it is a tautology iff the ROBDD is the constant node 1.
Variable ordering is crucial — examiners may ask you to show how different orderings lead to different sized BDDs for the same function.
Be able to trace through the Apply algorithm for simple operations (AND, OR) on small BDDs, using the Shannon expansion.

key_facts: Modal logic extends propositional logic with operators □ (necessarily/always) and ◇ (possibly/sometimes); ◇A ≃ ¬□¬A.
Semantics uses Kripke structures (possible worlds): a frame is a set of worlds W with an accessibility relation R; □A is true at world w if A is true at all worlds accessible from w.
A formula is valid in a frame if it is true at every world under every valuation; it is valid in a class of frames if it is valid in every frame in that class.

Different axioms correspond to different properties of the accessibility relation: T (□A→A) requires reflexivity; 4 (□A→□□A) requires transitivity; B (A→□◇A) requires symmetry; 5 (◇A→□◇A) requires Euclidean property.
System K is the basic modal logic with the distribution axiom K: □(A→B)→(□A→□B) and the necessitation rule (if ⊢A then ⊢□A).
Common modal systems: K (no frame conditions), T (reflexive), S4 (reflexive + transitive), S5 (equivalence relation = reflexive + symmetric + transitive).

terms.modal logic: Extension of propositional logic with □ (necessity) and ◇ (possibility) operators.
terms.\u25a1 (box): Modal necessity operator: □A means A holds in all accessible worlds.
terms.\u25c7 (diamond): Modal possibility operator: ◇A means A holds in some accessible world; ◇A ≡ ¬□¬A.
terms.Kripke structure: A model (W, R, V) with a set of worlds W, accessibility relation R ⊆ W×W, and valuation V.
terms.accessibility relation: Relation R on worlds specifying which worlds are accessible from which, determining □ and ◇.
terms.validity (modal): A formula is valid if true at all worlds in all Kripke structures (or all structures in a given class).
terms.system K: Basic modal logic with axiom K: □(A→B)→(□A→□B) and necessitation rule.
terms.system T: Modal logic K plus T axiom: □A→A (accessibility relation is reflexive).
terms.system S4: T plus axiom 4: □A→□□A (reflexive and transitive accessibility).
terms.system S5: S4 plus axiom B or 5: accessibility is an equivalence relation.
terms.necessitation rule: If A is a theorem, then □A is a theorem.

exam_tips: Match axioms to frame properties: T↔reflexive, 4↔transitive, B↔symmetric, 5↔Euclidean — examiners test this correspondence.
When proving modal formulas using Hilbert-style proofs, remember necessitation only applies to theorems, not to assumptions.
For Kripke semantics questions, explicitly verify truth at each relevant world and check the accessibility relation.
◇A ≡ ¬□¬A is fundamental — use it to simplify formulas involving ◇ into formulas involving □ and ¬.

key_facts: Tableaux methods are a refutation-based proof procedure: to prove A, attempt to build a model of ¬A; if every branch closes (contains a contradiction), A is proved.
A branch closes when it contains both a formula and its negation (a complementary pair).
The tableau method can be seen as a simplified sequent calculus where rules are applied to decompose formulas until atomic contradictions are found.
For first-order tableaux, unification is integrated: a branch closes when a literal and its negation can be unified (using the MGU), without requiring ground instances.
Alpha (α) rules conjunctively decompose formulas (e.g., A∧B splits into A and B on the same branch); beta (β) rules disjunctively decompose formulas (e.g., A∨B creates two branches).
Connection tableaux and model elimination are refinements that restrict the tableau expansion using connections between literals to improve efficiency.

terms.tableau: A tree-based refutation procedure where branches represent partial models and closure indicates a contradiction.
terms.branch: A path from root to leaf in the tableau tree; represents a partial attempt to satisfy the formula.
terms.closed branch: A tableau branch containing a formula and its negation (complementary pair); represents an inconsistency.
terms.closed tableau: A tableau where every branch is closed; constitutes a refutation proof.
terms.alpha (\u03b1) rule: Tableau rule that conjunctively decomposes a formula, adding both components to the same branch.
terms.beta (\u03b2) rule: Tableau rule that disjunctively decomposes a formula, creating two new branches.
terms.complementary pair: A literal L and its negation ¬L on the same branch; causes branch closure.
terms.analytic tableau: A tableau that only uses subformulas of the initial formula in its expansion.
terms.free-variable tableau: Tableau using free variables and unification to defer instantiation, allowing more flexible branch closure.
terms.connection tableau: A goal-directed tableau refinement that only extends branches using formulas connected to the current goal literal.

exam_tips: Clearly label each tableau rule applied (α or β) and show which formula is being decomposed at each step.
A tableau proves A by refuting ¬A — start by negating the goal, then apply rules until all branches close or an open branch remains.
When using unification in first-order tableaux, state the MGU explicitly when closing a branch with a complementary pair.
Distinguish the tableau method (builds refutation tree) from the sequent calculus (works backward from goal sequent) — examiners sometimes ask you to compare them.

key_facts: TIME(f(n)) is the set of all languages accepted by some deterministic Turing machine with running time O(f(n)).
P = union over k>=1 of TIME(n^k), i.e., the class of languages decidable in polynomial time.
The strong Church-Turing thesis states that any two reasonable models of computation are polynomially equivalent — each can simulate the other within a polynomial factor of complexity.
Whether a language is decidable in polynomial time is a property of the language itself, independent of reasonable computational model chosen.
Sorting has a lower bound of Omega(n log n) proven via computation tree argument: n! possible orderings require n! leaves, and a binary tree with n! leaves has height at least log2(n!).

An algorithm running in theta(x) steps on input x is NOT polynomial time if x is exponential in the input length (e.g., trial division for primality requires sqrt(n) steps, exponential in bit-length).

terms.TIME(f(n)): The class of languages accepted by a deterministic Turing machine with running time O(f(n)).
terms.P: The complexity class of languages decidable in polynomial time; union of TIME(n^k) for all k>=1.
terms.SPACE(f(n)): The class of languages accepted by a Turing machine using at most O(f(n)) tape cells on inputs of length n.
terms.Church-Turing thesis (strong form): Any two reasonable models of computation are polynomially equivalent, each simulating the other within a polynomial factor.
terms.Tractable: A problem is tractable (feasible) if it belongs to P.
terms.Intractable: A problem is intractable if it provably does not belong to P.
terms.Running time: Function r(n) giving the length of the longest accepting computation of machine M on an input of length n.
terms.O(g): f = O(g) if there exist n0 and constant c such that for all n > n0, f(n) <= c*g(n).

exam_tips: Remember that input size for numbers is the number of bits, not the number itself — algorithms like trial division are exponential in input size even if polynomial in n.
Be able to state and justify why polynomial time is a robust, model-independent notion of feasibility (unlike linear time).
The computation tree lower bound argument for sorting is a common proof technique — know how n! leaves imply Omega(n log n) height.
Do not confuse worst-case running time of an algorithm with the complexity of the problem; upper bounds come from algorithms, lower bounds require proving no algorithm can do better.

key_facts: A nondeterministic Turing machine has a transition relation (not a function), allowing multiple possible next configurations from any configuration.
A nondeterministic machine accepts string x if there exists at least one computation path leading to an accepting configuration.

NP is the class of languages decidable by a nondeterministic Turing machine in polynomial time, equivalently the class of languages whose members have polynomial-length certificates verifiable in polynomial time.
The Cook-Levin theorem states that SAT (and 3SAT) is NP-complete — every language in NP is polynomial-time reducible to SAT.
A language L is NP-complete if: (1) L is in NP, and (2) every language in NP is polynomial-time reducible to L.
A polynomial-time many-one reduction from L1 to L2 is a polynomial-time computable function f such that x in L1 iff f(x) in L2; if L2 is in P then L1 is in P.

terms.NP: The class of languages decidable by a nondeterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
terms.Nondeterministic Turing machine: A Turing machine where the transition function is replaced by a relation, allowing multiple possible transitions from each configuration.
terms.Certificate: A polynomial-length witness whose correctness can be verified in polynomial time, characterising NP membership.
terms.NP-complete: A language that is in NP and to which every language in NP is polynomial-time reducible.
terms.NP-hard: A language to which every language in NP is polynomial-time reducible (not necessarily in NP itself).
terms.Polynomial-time reduction: A polynomial-time computable function f such that x in L1 iff f(x) in L2, written L1 <=_p L2.
terms.SAT: The satisfiability problem: given a Boolean formula, does there exist a truth assignment that satisfies it?
terms.3SAT: SAT restricted to formulas in conjunctive normal form where each clause has exactly 3 literals; NP-complete.
terms.Cook-Levin theorem: SAT is NP-complete; the first proved NP-completeness result.
terms.Computation tree: Tree of all possible configuration sequences of a nondeterministic machine on a given input.

exam_tips: To prove a problem is NP-complete, you must show both that it is in NP (exhibit a polynomial certificate) and that it is NP-hard (reduce a known NP-complete problem to it).
The direction of reductions matters: to show L is NP-hard, reduce FROM a known NP-complete problem TO L.
A common mistake is confusing 'NP' with 'non-polynomial' — NP stands for nondeterministic polynomial time.
Know that if any NP-complete problem is in P, then P=NP, and every problem in NP is in P.

key_facts: 3-Colourability (3COL): given a graph, can its vertices be coloured with 3 colours such that no two adjacent vertices share a colour? This problem is NP-complete.
The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP): find a minimum cost Hamiltonian cycle in a weighted graph; best known algorithm is O(n^2 * 2^n), far above the Omega(n log n) lower bound.
Set Cover is NP-complete: given a universe and a collection of sets, find the minimum number of sets whose union covers the universe.
Knapsack/Subset Sum is NP-complete: given integers and a target, determine if some subset sums to the target.
Scheduling problems (e.g., job scheduling with deadlines) are generally NP-complete, proven by reduction from known NP-complete problems.
NP-completeness of graph problems like 3COL is typically shown by reduction from 3SAT, constructing gadgets that encode variable assignments and clause satisfaction.

terms.3-Colourability (3COL): The problem of determining whether a graph's vertices can be coloured with 3 colours so no two adjacent vertices share a colour; NP-complete.
terms.Graph colouring: Assignment of colours to vertices of a graph such that adjacent vertices have different colours.
terms.Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP): Find a minimum cost tour visiting all vertices exactly once; NP-complete in decision form.
terms.Set Cover: Given sets over a universe, find the fewest sets whose union is the universe; NP-complete.
terms.Knapsack problem: Given items with weights/values and a capacity, maximise value within capacity; NP-complete in decision form.
terms.Subset Sum: Determine if any subset of integers sums to a given target; NP-complete.
terms.Scheduling: Problems of assigning tasks to processors/times satisfying constraints; many variants are NP-complete.
terms.Gadget: A subgraph or substructure used in reductions to encode logical constraints locally.

exam_tips: When proving NP-completeness of a new problem, clearly identify which known NP-complete problem you reduce from and describe the reduction function explicitly.
For graph problems like 3COL, reductions from 3SAT use variable gadgets and clause gadgets — understand the structure of these.
Remember that NP-completeness does not mean the problem is always hard in practice — it means worst-case hardness assuming P≠NP.
Knapsack has a pseudo-polynomial time algorithm (dynamic programming), so it is 'weakly' NP-complete — be aware of the distinction between strong and weak NP-completeness.

key_facts: coNP is the class of languages whose complements are in NP; equivalently, problems where NO-instances have polynomial certificates.
It is unknown whether NP = coNP, but NP ≠ coNP would imply P ≠ NP.
PRIME (testing if a number is prime) is in NP ∩ coNP; it was shown to be in P by the AKS algorithm (2002), though the course notes treat it as a candidate for NP ∩ coNP.
One-way functions are functions computable in polynomial time but whose inverse is not computable in polynomial time; their existence is related to P ≠ NP.
The class UP consists of languages accepted by nondeterministic polynomial-time machines with at most one accepting computation path for each input.
If one-way functions exist, then P ≠ NP, since a one-way function whose inverse is in NP would be a polynomial-time checkable but not solvable problem.

terms.coNP: The class of languages whose complements are in NP; problems with polynomial-length certificates for NO-instances.
terms.Complement class: For a class C, the complement class co-C consists of the complements of all languages in C.
terms.NP \u2229 coNP: Problems that are in both NP and coNP; have polynomial certificates for both yes and no answers.
terms.PRIME: The language of prime numbers in binary; known to be in P (AKS), previously known to be in NP ∩ coNP.
terms.One-way function: A function computable in polynomial time for which no polynomial-time algorithm computes the inverse.
terms.UP: The class of languages accepted by nondeterministic polynomial-time Turing machines with at most one accepting path per input.
terms.Primality testing: Determining whether a given integer is prime; in P via AKS algorithm.
terms.Cryptographic complexity: Study of computational hardness assumptions (like one-way functions) that underlie cryptographic security.

exam_tips: Remember that coNP is NOT the complement of NP as a class — it is the class of complements of languages in NP, and NP ∩ coNP is non-trivial (e.g., contains PRIME and graph isomorphism).
A common exam question: explain why showing a problem is in NP ∩ coNP is evidence it is not NP-complete (assuming NP ≠ coNP).
Know that the existence of one-way functions implies P ≠ NP but the converse is not known.
Be prepared to give examples of problems in NP ∩ coNP and explain the significance of this class for cryptography.

key_facts: SPACE(f(n)) is the class of languages accepted by a Turing machine using at most O(f(n)) work tape cells on inputs of length n.
PSPACE = union over k of SPACE(n^k); it is known that P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE.
Savitch's theorem states that NSPACE(f(n)) ⊆ SPACE(f(n)^2), so nondeterminism gives at most a quadratic savings in space; in particular NPSPACE = PSPACE.
The Reachability problem is solvable in O(n) space (linear space) but can also be solved in O(log^2 n) space (NSPACE(log n) by nondeterminism, and SPACE(log^2 n) by Savitch's theorem).
Hierarchy theorems: TIME(f(n)) is strictly contained in TIME(g(n)) if g grows sufficiently faster than f (time hierarchy theorem); similarly for space.
L = SPACE(log n) and NL = NSPACE(log n) are important small space classes; NL = coNL by Immerman-Szelepcsényi theorem.

terms.SPACE(f(n)): Class of languages decidable by a Turing machine using O(f(n)) work tape cells.
terms.PSPACE: Class of languages decidable in polynomial space; equals NPSPACE by Savitch's theorem.
terms.NPSPACE: Class of languages decidable by a nondeterministic machine in polynomial space; equals PSPACE.
terms.Savitch's theorem: NSPACE(f(n)) ⊆ SPACE(f(n)^2) for f(n) >= log n; nondeterministic space can be squared to get deterministic space.
terms.L: SPACE(log n); languages decidable in logarithmic space.
terms.NL: NSPACE(log n); languages decidable by a nondeterministic machine in logarithmic space; contains Reachability.
terms.Time hierarchy theorem: For well-behaved f and g with f(n) log f(n) = o(g(n)), TIME(f(n)) ⊊ TIME(g(n)).
terms.Space hierarchy theorem: For space-constructible f and g with f(n) = o(g(n)), SPACE(f(n)) ⊊ SPACE(g(n)).
terms.Work tape: A separate tape (not the read-only input tape) on which space complexity is measured.

exam_tips: Savitch's theorem is a key result — know the statement precisely: nondeterministic space f(n) ⊆ deterministic space f(n)^2, implying PSPACE = NPSPACE.
Hierarchy theorems prove that more resources strictly increase computational power — be able to sketch the diagonalisation argument.
Space complexity assumes a read-only input tape; space is only counted on work tapes — this is crucial for sublinear space classes like L and NL.
Know the containment chain: L ⊆ NL ⊆ P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE, and that at least one of these containments is strict (since L ≠ PSPACE).

key_facts: BPP (Bounded-error Probabilistic Polynomial time) is the class of languages decidable by a probabilistic polynomial-time Turing machine with error probability at most 1/3 on any input.
BPP ⊆ PSPACE and P ⊆ BPP; it is unknown whether P = BPP, but most researchers believe they are equal.
Derandomisation is the process of converting randomised algorithms into deterministic ones; pseudorandom generators are a key tool.
Error probability in BPP can be reduced exponentially by repeating the algorithm polynomially many times and taking a majority vote.
BQP (Bounded-error Quantum Polynomial time) is the quantum analogue of BPP; it is known that BPP ⊆ BQP and that factoring is in BQP (Shor's algorithm) but factoring is not known to be in P.
Communication complexity studies the minimum number of bits two parties must exchange to jointly compute a function of their inputs; it provides lower bound techniques for other models.

terms.BPP: Bounded-error Probabilistic Polynomial time; languages decidable by a probabilistic polynomial-time machine with error <= 1/3.
terms.Probabilistic Turing machine: A Turing machine with access to random coin flips; accepts if the probability of acceptance exceeds a threshold.
terms.Derandomisation: Converting a randomised algorithm into a deterministic one, ideally with only polynomial overhead.
terms.Pseudorandom generator: A deterministic function that stretches a short seed into a longer string indistinguishable from truly random bits.
terms.BQP: Bounded-error Quantum Polynomial time; the class of languages decidable by a quantum computer in polynomial time with bounded error.
terms.Quantum polynomial time: Computation using quantum mechanical operations (superposition, entanglement) in polynomial steps.
terms.Error reduction: Technique of repeating a BPP algorithm polynomially many times and taking majority vote to reduce error probability exponentially.
terms.Communication complexity: Minimum number of bits two parties must communicate to compute a function of their distributed inputs.
terms.Error-correcting codes: Codes that allow recovery of original data despite errors; related to complexity via hardness amplification and PCP theorem.

exam_tips: Know the definition of BPP carefully — error probability bounded by 1/3 (or any constant < 1/2) from both sides (two-sided error), and understand that this threshold is arbitrary due to error reduction.
Be able to explain the error reduction argument: repeating O(k) times and majority voting reduces error to 2^{-k}.
Understand the significance of BQP: Shor's algorithm puts factoring in BQP, giving a separation from classical expectations but not a proven separation from P.
Communication complexity lower bounds are proved using combinatorial arguments about matrix rank or fooling sets — know the basic definitions.

key_facts: IP is the class of languages having interactive proof systems, where a probabilistic polynomial-time verifier interacts with an all-powerful prover; IP = PSPACE.
In an interactive proof, the verifier must accept all true statements with high probability (completeness) and reject false statements with high probability (soundness).
Zero-knowledge proofs allow a prover to convince a verifier of a statement's truth without revealing any additional information beyond the truth of the statement.
The PCP theorem states that every language in NP has a proof that can be verified probabilistically by reading only O(1) bits of the proof (with constant soundness error).
The PCP theorem implies that approximating many NP-hard optimisation problems within a constant factor is itself NP-hard (inapproximability results).
Graph non-isomorphism is in IP (complement of graph isomorphism): verifier sends a random permutation of one of the graphs, prover identifies which original graph it came from.

terms.IP: Interactive Proof; class of languages with interactive proof systems between a probabilistic polynomial verifier and an unbounded prover; IP = PSPACE.
terms.Interactive proof system: A protocol where a verifier exchanges messages with a prover and accepts/rejects based on the interaction; completeness and soundness required.
terms.Completeness: If x is in the language, the prover can convince the verifier to accept with high probability.
terms.Soundness: If x is not in the language, no prover strategy can convince the verifier to accept except with small probability.
terms.Zero-knowledge proof: An interactive proof where the verifier learns nothing beyond the truth of the statement; the interaction can be simulated without the prover.
terms.PCP theorem: Every NP language has a probabilistically checkable proof verifiable by reading O(1) random bits with constant soundness error; NP = PCP(log n, 1).
terms.Probabilistically Checkable Proof (PCP): A proof format where a verifier can check correctness by reading a constant number of randomly chosen bits.
terms.Inapproximability: The result that approximating certain optimisation problems within a ratio better than some constant is NP-hard, often derived from the PCP theorem.
terms.Arthur-Merlin games: Public-coin interactive proofs where the verifier's random coins are visible to the prover; AM is the corresponding class.

exam_tips: The key result IP = PSPACE is examinable — know that IP ⊆ PSPACE (verifier can be simulated in PSPACE) and PSPACE ⊆ IP (PSPACE-complete problems like TQBF have interactive proofs).
For zero-knowledge proofs, be able to give the graph isomorphism example and explain the three properties: completeness, soundness, and zero-knowledge (simulatability).
The PCP theorem's connection to inapproximability is important: understand that a gap-introducing reduction from PCP gives hardness of approximation.
Do not confuse the prover in IP (computationally unbounded) with the verifier (probabilistic polynomial time) — the asymmetry is essential.

key_facts: A transition system consists of a set Config and a binary relation → ⊆ Config × Config; elements of Config are called configurations or states.
L1 configurations are pairs ⟨e, s⟩ where e is an expression and s is a finite partial function from locations L to integers Z.
Values in L1 are V = B ∪ Z ∪ {skip}; a configuration ⟨e, s⟩ is stuck if e is not a value and ⟨e, s⟩ ↛.
The (while) rule unfolds: ⟨while e1 do e2 done, s⟩ → ⟨if e1 then (e2; while e1 do e2 done) else skip, s⟩.
Rules (op1) and (op2) enforce left-to-right evaluation order: (op1) reduces the left operand first, (op2) only fires when the left operand is already a value.
The reduction relation → is defined as the smallest subset of A closed under all rule implications, equivalently characterised by existence of a finite derivation tree.

terms.transition system: A set Config of configurations together with a binary transition relation → ⊆ Config × Config
terms.structural operational semantics: Style of semantics where reduction rules are defined by induction on the structure of expressions
terms.configuration: A pair ⟨e, s⟩ of an expression and a store used as states in the L1 transition system
terms.store: A finite partial function from locations L to integers Z, representing mutable memory
terms.stuck configuration: A configuration ⟨e, s⟩ where e is not a value and no transition rule applies
terms.reflexive transitive closure: →* holds between c and c' iff there exist k≥0 steps c=c0→c1→...→ck=c'
terms.derivation: A finite tree of elements of A where every node is justified as a concrete rule instance, witnessing membership in →
terms.value: An expression in V = B ∪ Z ∪ {skip} that cannot reduce further

exam_tips: When asked to derive a transition sequence, show each step as a derivation tree citing the rule name and metavariable instantiations explicitly.
Remember (op2) requires the left operand to already be a value — a common mistake is applying (op2) when e1 is not yet a value.
The (while) rule produces an if-then-else; do not try to directly evaluate the body — the rule only unfolds one level.
Distinguish between a configuration that is stuck (not a value, no transition) and one that has terminated at a value — examiners test this distinction.

key_facts: L1 type environments Γ are finite partial functions from locations L to location types (e.g. intref), written as l1:intref, ..., lk:intref.
The typing judgement Γ ⊢ e : T means 'in type environment Γ, expression e has type T'.
L1 has types including int, bool, and unit (the type of skip), plus intref for integer-holding locations.
Type safety combines progress (a well-typed non-value can always reduce) and preservation (reduction preserves types).
Untyped L1 admits nonsensical expressions like 3 + true which become stuck; typing rules out such stuck states.
The type system is defined inductively by typing rules, one or more per language construct, mirroring the structure of the semantics rules.

terms.type environment: A finite partial function Γ mapping locations (or variables) to their types, providing context for typing judgements
terms.typing judgement: The assertion Γ ⊢ e : T stating expression e has type T under environment Γ
terms.intref: The L1 type assigned to store locations that hold integer values
terms.type safety: The property that well-typed programs do not get stuck, combining progress and preservation
terms.progress: If Γ ⊢ e : T and e is not a value, then there exists e', s' such that ⟨e,s⟩ → ⟨e',s'⟩
terms.preservation: If Γ ⊢ e : T and ⟨e,s⟩ → ⟨e',s'⟩ then Γ ⊢ e' : T

exam_tips: State progress and preservation separately and precisely when asked about type safety — conflating them loses marks.
When writing typing derivations, apply rules bottom-up and cite each rule, checking that side conditions (e.g. ℓ ∈ dom(Γ)) are satisfied.
Remember that the type environment for L1 maps locations to types, not variables — variables are introduced only in L2.

key_facts: Structural induction on expressions: to prove P(e) for all e, prove P for each base case (n, b, ℓ, skip) and for each compound form assuming P holds for all immediate subexpressions.
Rule induction: to prove that all elements of an inductively defined relation R satisfy property P, prove P is preserved by each rule, i.e. if the premises satisfy P then so does the conclusion.
An inductive definition of a relation specifies it as the smallest set closed under a given set of rules.
A derivation is a finite tree where each node is a concrete rule instance; membership in the inductively defined relation is equivalent to existence of such a derivation.
Mathematical induction on natural numbers is a special case: base case n=0, inductive step assuming true for n implies true for n+1.
Type safety proofs use rule induction on the typing derivation (for preservation) and structural or rule induction on expressions (for progress).

terms.structural induction: Induction over the abstract syntax tree of an expression, with cases for each syntactic construct
terms.rule induction: Proving a property of an inductively defined relation by showing each inference rule preserves the property
terms.inductive definition: A definition of a set or relation as the smallest set closed under a specified collection of rules
terms.derivation tree: A finite tree witnessing membership in an inductively defined relation, where each node is a concrete rule instance
terms.abstract syntax tree: A tree representation of a program's structure, distinct from its concrete textual syntax

exam_tips: For rule induction proofs, explicitly state the property P being proved and show it holds for every rule by assuming it holds for all premises.
Do not confuse structural induction (on syntax) with rule induction (on derivations) — exam questions often require one specifically.
When proving preservation, induct on the typing derivation; when proving progress, induct on the structure of the expression or typing derivation.

key_facts: Call-by-value (CBV): the argument is fully evaluated to a value before substitution into the function body.
Call-by-name (CBN): the argument expression is substituted directly into the function body without prior evaluation.
Substitution {e/x}e' denotes replacing all free occurrences of variable x in e' with expression e, respecting alpha-equivalence to avoid capture.
Function types are written T1 → T2, meaning a function taking an argument of type T1 and returning type T2.
The typing rule for function application: if Γ ⊢ e1 : T1→T2 and Γ ⊢ e2 : T1 then Γ ⊢ e1 e2 : T2.
Local recursive definitions (let rec / fun) require the function name to be in scope within its own body, necessitating special treatment such as a fix-point combinator or dedicated let rec rule.

terms.call-by-value: Evaluation strategy where function arguments are reduced to values before substitution into the function body
terms.call-by-name: Evaluation strategy where unevaluated argument expressions are substituted directly into the function body
terms.alpha-conversion: Renaming of bound variables in a term to avoid variable capture, defining abstract syntax up to alpha-equivalence
terms.substitution: The operation {e/x}e' of replacing free occurrences of x in e' with e
terms.free variables: Variables occurring in an expression not bound by any enclosing abstraction, fv(e)
terms.function type: The type T1→T2 classifying functions taking T1 inputs and producing T2 outputs
terms.let rec: A local definition construct allowing recursive reference to the defined function within its own body

exam_tips: Be precise about whether a question uses call-by-value or call-by-name — the reduction rules differ and produce different results for non-terminating arguments.
When performing substitution, check for variable capture and apply alpha-conversion if needed before substituting.
For typing function application, ensure the domain type of the function matches the type of the argument exactly.

key_facts: Product type T1 × T2 has introduction form (e1, e2) and elimination forms fst e and snd e; fst(v1,v2) → v1 and snd(v1,v2) → v2.
Sum type T1 + T2 has introduction forms inl e and inr e, and elimination via match e with inl x → e1 | inr x → e2.
Records extend products to named fields: {lab1=e1,...,labk=ek} with projection e.lab; typing assigns a record type {lab1:T1,...,labk:Tk}.
References (mutable store) in L3: locations have type Tloc, dereferencing !ℓ reads the store, assignment ℓ:=e updates it.
Evaluation contexts E define a deterministic evaluation order by specifying the unique hole position where reduction occurs next.
The L3 store maps locations to values of arbitrary types (not just integers), so the store type environment maps ℓ to Tloc.

terms.product type: Type T1×T2 whose values are pairs (v1,v2) with v1:T1 and v2:T2
terms.sum type: Type T1+T2 whose values are either inl v (with v:T1) or inr v (with v:T2), representing disjoint union
terms.record type: Type {lab1:T1,...,labk:Tk} whose values are records with named fields
terms.reference type: A location type Tloc classifying mutable store locations holding values of a given type
terms.evaluation context: A term E with a hole [] specifying where the next reduction step occurs, used to factor out congruence rules
terms.inl / inr: Sum type injection constructors: inl e injects into the left component, inr e into the right

exam_tips: When writing reduction rules for products or sums, remember values must be fully evaluated before elimination rules fire.
Evaluation contexts compress many congruence rules into one; know how to read off which subexpression is reduced next from the context definition.
For references in L3, the store type environment must be consistent with the store — examiners test well-typed store conditions.

key_facts: Subtyping T <: T' means a value of type T can be used wherever a value of type T' is expected (subsumption rule).
Record subtyping: a record type with more fields is a subtype of one with fewer fields (width subtyping), e.g. {a:int, b:bool} <: {a:int}.
Field types may also be covariant for read-only records (depth subtyping), but this interacts with mutation.
The subsumption rule: if Γ ⊢ e : T and T <: T' then Γ ⊢ e : T'.
Simple objects can be encoded as records of functions (methods) sharing a hidden state via references, capturing basic OO behaviour.
Subtyping is reflexive (T <: T) and transitive (T <: T' and T' <: T'' implies T <: T'').

terms.subtype: T <: T' means type T is a subtype of T', i.e. T-values are acceptable wherever T'-values are required
terms.subsumption: The typing rule allowing an expression of type T to be given type T' whenever T <: T'
terms.width subtyping: Record subtyping rule allowing a record with more fields to be used as one with fewer fields
terms.depth subtyping: Record subtyping rule allowing field types to be replaced by subtypes
terms.object encoding: Representation of objects as records of functions (methods) that close over shared mutable state

exam_tips: Remember width subtyping goes in the direction of more fields <: fewer fields — a common source of errors is reversing this.
When writing subtype derivations, cite each subtyping rule used (reflexivity, transitivity, width, depth) explicitly.
Depth subtyping for mutable references is unsound — examiners may ask why read/write references are invariant.

key_facts: Two program phrases e and e' are semantically equivalent at type T in environment Γ (written e ≃^T_Γ e') if they behave identically in all program contexts of appropriate type.
Contextual equivalence is a congruence: if e ≃ e' then C[e] ≃ C[e'] for all contexts C.
Semantic equivalence is the largest congruence contained in observational equivalence — phrases are equivalent iff no context can distinguish them.
Equivalence examples: different implementations of a counter with the same observable behaviour are equivalent; reordering independent assignments may or may not be equivalent depending on aliasing.
Non-equivalence is shown by exhibiting a distinguishing context C such that C[e] and C[e'] produce different observable outcomes.
Observable outcomes in L1 are typically termination with a particular value or divergence, observed by a complete program context.

terms.semantic equivalence: e ≃^T_Γ e' iff for all contexts C of appropriate type, C[e] and C[e'] have the same observable behaviour
terms.contextual equivalence: Equivalence defined by indistinguishability under all program contexts; same as semantic equivalence
terms.congruence: A relation R is a congruence if e R e' implies C[e] R C[e'] for all contexts C
terms.context: A program expression C with a hole [] such that C[e] denotes e placed in the hole
terms.distinguishing context: A context C witnessing non-equivalence by producing different results on C[e] vs C[e']
terms.observational equivalence: Equivalence based on identical observable results (termination/value) across all closing contexts

exam_tips: To prove equivalence, argue about all possible contexts — typically via a bisimulation or logical relation argument; to disprove, exhibit one distinguishing context.
The congruence property is key: state it precisely as 'e ≃ e' implies C[e] ≃ C[e'] for all C' when asked about properties of ≃.
Examiners often ask for examples of equivalent and non-equivalent pairs; aliasing of store locations is a standard source of non-equivalence.

key_facts: Concurrent semantics for shared-variable programs uses interleaving: a step of the combined system is a step of any one thread, leaving others unchanged.
A concurrent configuration typically consists of a tuple of thread expressions plus a shared store ⟨e1 || ... || en, s⟩.
Mutex semantics: a mutex m is either locked or unlocked; lock(m) blocks if m is already locked, unlock(m) releases it.
A serializability property states that the result of a concurrent execution is the same as some serial (sequential) execution of the threads.
Interleaving semantics can produce results not achievable by any serial execution, violating serializability unless synchronisation is used.
Thread transitions are labelled with actions a (e.g. read, write, lock, unlock) to track interactions with shared state.

terms.interleaving semantics: Concurrent semantics where each transition is a step by exactly one thread, modelling shared-memory concurrency
terms.mutex: A mutual exclusion lock that ensures at most one thread executes a critical section at a time
terms.serializability: Property that every concurrent execution produces a result equivalent to some sequential execution of the same threads
terms.thread action: A label a on a transition recording what interaction with shared state occurred (read, write, lock, unlock)
terms.shared variable: A store location accessible by multiple concurrent threads, the source of race conditions
terms.critical section: A code region that must not be executed concurrently by multiple threads, protected by a mutex

exam_tips: When constructing interleaving derivations, show each step with the rule and which thread fires — examiners check that non-stepping threads are left unchanged.
Serializability proofs require showing every interleaved execution matches some serial order; find a witness serial ordering explicitly.
A common exam task is exhibiting an interleaving that produces a result no serial execution could, demonstrating why mutexes are necessary.
